target_sections: Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Libraries as Playful Spaces and Service Environments|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Outreach, Participation, and New Audiences|Programming Formats and Service Models|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services
summary_text:
The provided text is an informational document titled "April Showers Bring Superpowers," dated 2023, associated with the American Library Association (ALA). It functions as a promotional schedule and resource guide for a virtual event hosted collaboratively by three ALA Round Tables: Films & Media (FMRT), Games & Gaming (GameRT), and Graphic Novels & Comics (GNCRT). The document does not present original empirical research, statistical analysis, or theoretical arguments. Instead, it serves as a crowd-sourced compilation of media recommendations centered on the theme of "superheroes," intended for librarians and library professionals interested in these genres.

The primary purpose of the text is to outline the agenda for an event scheduled for Friday, April 28, 2023, which was broadcast via Twitch. The document explicitly states that the media list is "crowd-sourced" from members of the three round tables. It includes detailed presenter biographies for key figures such as Danielle Costello (LSU), Liz Brown (UCCS), Kit Steinkellner, and Sebastian Kadlecik, who are associated with specific segments like a comics reading of *Quince* by Fanbase Press. The text also provides logistical details, including Twitch links, a Zoom link for audience chat, and an Airtable survey for prize entry.

The core content consists of categorized lists of recommended media. These categories include Books (e.g., *Cute Mutants*, *Dreadnought*), Comics (e.g., *Batman: The Court of Owls*, *She-Hulk*), Films & TV (e.g., *Blue Beetle*, *Loki*, *Moon Knight*), and Games. The gaming section is further subdivided into Board & Card Games (e.g., *Marvel Champions*, *Sentinels of the Multiverse*), Tabletop Role-Playing Games (TTRPGs) (e.g., *City of Mist*, *Masks*), and Video Games (e.g., *Marvel’s Spider-Man*). Additionally, an "Other Content" section lists podcasts, TikTok videos, and articles, reflecting a broad interpretation of media engagement. A distinct section titled "ChatGPT Information" lists resources on AI in higher education, such as "AI In Higher Ed: An Introduction without the Hype," indicating a concurrent interest in artificial intelligence literacy within these professional circles.

From the perspective of Games-Based Library Services (GBLS), this document is relevant as it demonstrates active curation and promotion of game-related media by library professionals. The inclusion of TTRPGs, board games, and video games alongside traditional print media highlights the interdisciplinary nature of modern library services. The document underscores the role of ALA Round Tables in facilitating community building and resource sharing among librarians specializing in niche media formats. However, as it is an event schedule and recommendation list rather than a research article, it offers no data on user engagement, learning outcomes, or the efficacy of games-based services. It serves primarily as evidence of professional interest and curatorial practice within the library community regarding superhero-themed media and gaming. The text concludes with contact information and links for joining the round tables, reinforcing its function as a recruitment and engagement tool for ALA members.

citation: Kobolds Ate My Baby! by 9th Level Games (2024)
target_sections: Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Libraries as Playful Spaces and Service Environments|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Fandom Festivals and Pop-Culture Events|Game Design Workshops, Jams, and Participatory Creation
summary_text:
The provided source text is a promotional and informational document regarding the tabletop role-playing game (TTRPG) *Kobolds Ate My Baby!*, published by 9th Level Games in 2024. The text does not constitute an academic study or empirical research article; rather, it functions as a resource guide for players and community members. Its primary objective is to introduce the game’s thematic elements, provide access to instructional and entertainment content, and establish behavioral norms for associated online events.

The document begins by outlining the narrative premise of the game. Players assume the roles of kobolds serving "King Torg." The tone is explicitly described as "silly, brutal, and short." The text lists specific, humorous hazards that characters may encounter, such as being crushed by falling cows, obliterated by meteorites, betrayed by friends, or struck by frying pans. The core gameplay objective is survival: players must endure these threats long enough to retrieve food ("a tasty treat") for the Kobold Caves, thereby avoiding becoming "lunch" themselves. This description positions the game within the genre of comedic, low-stakes fantasy role-playing.

Regarding data and population, the text does not present empirical findings or analyze a specific sample group. It references an "Actual Play" session featuring individuals named Yeet, Bitey, and Fomo, but these are identified as content creators rather than research participants. Consequently, no statistical analysis or qualitative data regarding player behavior or learning outcomes is reported. The "Findings" section of this extraction therefore consists solely of the factual details provided about the game’s mechanics, resources, and community guidelines.

The text provides several resources for engagement with the game. It includes links to Twitch Video on Demand (VOD) recordings of sessions, a "How to Play" guide promising instruction in under ten minutes, and character sheet examples. Furthermore, it situates *Kobolds Ate My Baby!* within a broader cultural context by listing "Play Alikes" and "Read Alikes." Similar games include *Goblin Quest*, *Paranoia*, *Mork Borg*, *Fiasco*, *Dread*, *Ten Candles*, and *Munchkin*. Recommended reading includes *9 Goblins* by T. Kingfisher, *Goblin Quest* by Jim C. Hines, and *Legends & Lattes* by Travis Baldree. These comparisons suggest the game appeals to audiences interested in humorous fantasy narratives and unconventional role-playing experiences.

A significant portion of the text is dedicated to community safety and moderation. It lists "Safety Tools" commonly used in TTRPG communities, such as "Script Change" and "The Door is Open," along with references to blogs by Lizzie Stark and Eirik Fatland, and the "TTRPG Safety Toolkit." Additionally, it outlines a "GameRT Twitch Policy" for associated streams. This policy mandates kindness and respect, prohibits hate speech, reserves the right to remove users from chat, and provides mechanisms for reporting harassment via moderators or direct messages. The inclusion of these resources indicates a deliberate effort to foster an inclusive environment, which is relevant to discussions on games-based library services regarding the management of public gaming events.

The document concludes with a "Stream Summary" that structures the associated event into three parts: a technical check and introduction to rules, the actual play session, and a discussion period. It also provides contact information for GameRT, including links to their blog, social media, resources, and email address. Overall, the text serves as a comprehensive guide for accessing and understanding *Kobolds Ate My Baby!*, emphasizing both the game’s humorous nature and the importance of community safety in online gaming spaces. It does not offer evidence-based insights into the efficacy of games-based library services but provides a concrete example of how such services might curate and present TTRPG content to the public.

citation: Abrams, S. S., & Van Eck, R. N. (2021). Checked Out? Videogame Programming in U.S. Libraries and Implications for Videogame Literacies. *Journal of Adolescent & Adult Literacy*, 65(3), 227–236.
target_sections: Service Purposes and Library Functions|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy|Community, Belonging, Wellness, and the Public Commons|Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services
summary_text:
This article by Abrams and Van Eck (2021) examines the state of videogame programming in U.S. libraries, focusing on its prevalence, structural forms, and the barriers influencing its adoption. The study is grounded in the premise that videogaming constitutes a form of literacy involving multimodal meaning-making, social interaction, and iterative learning. Despite the recognized benefits of gaming for engagement and problem-solving, the authors note a gap in understanding why such programming is not more ubiquitous in library settings.

The research utilizes an integrated mixed-methods approach, analyzing data from a survey of 1,016 librarians across all 50 U.S. states. The sample was predominantly female (79.3%) and experienced (average 12 years), with representation from urban, suburban, and rural settings. Data collection occurred between August 2017 and May 2018 via an online survey containing both quantitative Likert-scale items and qualitative open-ended questions. The analytical framework distinguishes between first-order barriers (external constraints like resources and training) and second-order barriers (internal factors like beliefs and confidence), adapted from Ertmer’s model of technology integration.

Key findings reveal that videogame programming is not yet standard practice: only 48% of respondents reported offering such programs, while 52% did not. Among libraries with gaming initiatives, the majority (63%) had established them within the last five years, indicating a recent trend rather than an entrenched practice. The most common format was scheduled access (48%), which the authors critique as potentially restrictive compared to open play, which better supports the iterative nature of videogame literacies.

The study identifies significant barriers to adoption. First-order barriers were prominent, with librarians reporting high difficulty in securing funding and resources (mean score 3.71/5). Training was largely informal, derived from independent research or peer advice, rather than formal Library Science education (less than 12%). Second-order barriers included low self-efficacy among librarians regarding their ability to facilitate gaming (mean confidence barrier score 3.76/5), despite high rates of personal device ownership (69%). Qualitative data highlighted that librarians who valued programming appreciated the social bonding and community building it fostered, whereas those opposed cited management challenges, noise, hygiene concerns, and lack of administrative support.

The authors conclude that expanding videogame literacies in libraries requires addressing both resource deficits and attitudinal barriers. They recommend integrating gaming literacy into Library Science curricula and accreditation standards, enhancing professional development, and restructuring programming to align with youth interests while reducing managerial burdens. The study underscores the potential of libraries as informal learning spaces where videogames can support complex literacy practices, provided that systemic and individual obstacles are effectively mitigated.

citation: Adams, M. J., Finch, M., & Gamble, B. (2023). More than a Game: Scenario Planning, Imagination, and the Public Library’s Future. *Public Library Quarterly*, 42(6), 665–687. https://doi.org/10.1080/01616846.2022.2154068
target_sections: Service Purposes and Library Functions|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Ground Services in Purpose, Audience, and Institutional Context|Institutional Capacity and Sustainable Service Models
summary_text:
This article presents a reflective case study on the application of scenario planning within the Reading Public Library (RPL) in Pennsylvania, aiming to navigate strategic uncertainty during the early 2020s. The authors, including RPL’s outgoing and incoming Executive Directors and a scenario planning consultant, argue that traditional strategic planning methods are often insufficient for addressing "TUNA" conditions—Turbulence, Uncertainty, Novelty, and Ambiguity. Instead, they advocate for the Oxford Scenario Planning Approach, which uses storytelling and systems mapping to create plausible alternative futures, thereby enhancing organizational flexibility and adaptability.

The methodology involved a collaborative process with RPL’s strategic planning committee, comprising key staff and board members. Participants engaged in virtual workshops and created an online mural map of the library’s transactional environment, identifying flows of value and external uncertainties. Through iterative discussions, the committee identified two critical axes of uncertainty: the degree of social cooperation versus fragmentation, and the extent to which civic life would be conducted in digital spaces versus physical presence. Using these axes, they developed three scenarios for the year 2040: "Life in the Clouds," depicting a highly digital, cooperative society; "The Wild Frontier," characterized by social fragmentation and retreat into isolated groups; and "Paris on the Schuylkill," envisioning a culturally rich, cooperative community with strong physical civic engagement.

From these scenarios, the library derived four strategic "tracks" designed to remain relevant regardless of which future materializes: Co-designed Programs, Intentional Partnerships, Staff Reflects the Community, and Create Digital Opportunities. The article details how these tracks informed specific operational decisions. For instance, the decision to convert an empty workspace into a Maker Space was justified by its alignment with multiple scenarios: it fostered community co-design (addressing fragmentation risks), provided digital access tools (supporting the digital future), and served as an innovation hub (aligning with the cultural flourishing scenario). Similarly, the development of an Art Display Policy was driven by the need to anticipate potential challenges arising from social polarization, as depicted in "The Wild Frontier" scenario.

The authors emphasize that this process facilitated a seamless leadership transition, as the scenarios provided a shared strategic language and framework that persisted beyond individual tenures. The article concludes by framing scenario planning as a form of "serious play," allowing library professionals to experiment with new identities and strategies in a low-risk environment. This approach not only aids in internal strategic resilience but also positions libraries as proactive partners in community future-making, capable of adapting to unpredictable shifts in the social and technological landscape. The study underscores the value of imagination and narrative in strategic planning, suggesting that libraries can leverage these tools to maintain relevance and agility in an increasingly complex world.

citation: Adetayo, A., Oladipo, Y., Adeleke, O., & Odunewu, A. (2023). Gamification of Cataloguing and Classification Education in Nigeria Library and Information Science Schools: A Technology-Enhanced Approach. Journal of Education for Library and Information Science, 64(4), 370–384.
target_sections: Service Purposes and Library Functions|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Ground Services in Purpose, Audience, and Institutional Context|Gaps in the Literature and Future Research Agenda
summary_text:
This article by Adetayo et al. (2023) addresses the pedagogical challenges associated with teaching cataloguing and classification in Nigerian Library and Information Science (LIS) schools. The authors argue that these core librarianship skills are often perceived by students as technical, monotonous, and overly theoretical, leading to disengagement and inadequate practical preparation for professional roles. To counter this, the study explores gamification—a technology-enhanced approach that integrates game mechanics into non-gaming educational contexts—as a strategy to improve student motivation, engagement, and learning outcomes.

The methodology consists of a comprehensive literature review rather than primary empirical data collection. The authors synthesize existing research on gamification in education, the current state of LIS curricula in Nigeria, and technological platforms suitable for implementation. The review highlights that while gamification has been applied in various educational sectors (e.g., economics, general e-learning), its application to cataloguing and classification remains underexplored, particularly in the Nigerian context where awareness among librarians is low.

The study identifies several critical issues in current LIS education in Nigeria. Many students enter the field without strong intrinsic interest, often viewing librarianship as "books and boring." The curriculum heavily emphasizes theory over practice, focusing on standards like AACR2 and LCSH while neglecting hands-on experience with modern tools. This disconnect results in graduates who lack the technical proficiency required by employers. Furthermore, students frequently exhibit negative attitudes toward cataloguing courses, associating them with difficulty and redundancy.

To address these challenges, the authors propose a conceptual model for gamifying cataloguing and classification education, supported by cloud computing infrastructure. Cloud computing is recommended to reduce the financial burden on institutions by eliminating the need for extensive hardware upgrades, allowing for scalable, accessible learning environments. The proposed framework rests on five pillars: motivation, short-term tasks, reward systems, task design, and teamwork.

Regarding motivation, the authors draw on Self-Determination Theory, distinguishing between intrinsic and extrinsic motivators. They argue that gamification should foster intrinsic motivation by creating a "flow" state where students are absorbed in challenging yet achievable tasks. Extrinsic rewards (e.g., points) should be used carefully to avoid undermining intrinsic interest, with an emphasis on unexpected rewards and positive verbal feedback.

The model breaks down complex cataloguing processes into short-term, manageable tasks. For instance, instead of overwhelming students with full bibliographic records, the gamified system would guide them through sequential steps: identifying tools, describing physical features, assigning subject headings, and finally creating class marks. Each step provides immediate feedback, allowing students to correct errors in a low-stakes environment. Task design is structured to increase in difficulty progressively, ensuring that students build competence gradually. Teamwork is integrated to encourage peer collaboration and the sharing of strategies, enhancing social learning.

The authors illustrate how this model could be implemented using cloud-based platforms (e.g., Google Cloud, Microsoft Azure) to deliver interactive modules. For example, a classification game might require students to drag and drop items into correct Library of Congress categories to earn points. This approach transforms repetitive drills into engaging activities, potentially improving retention and practical skill acquisition.

However, the study acknowledges significant limitations and barriers to implementation. The primary constraint is the lack of empirical evidence specifically linking gamification to improved outcomes in Nigerian LIS cataloguing courses; the proposed model is theoretical and requires validation through future empirical studies. Additionally, financial constraints pose a major challenge, as developing high-quality gamified content involves costs for design, testing, and specialist consultation. Technological adoption is another hurdle, particularly among older faculty members who may lack proficiency with digital tools. The authors recommend targeted training workshops for academics and advocate for stakeholder collaboration to secure funding and policy support.

In conclusion, Adetayo et al. present a compelling case for integrating gamification into LIS education in Nigeria. By leveraging cloud computing and game-based learning principles, library schools can potentially transform the perception of cataloguing from a tedious obligation to an engaging, skill-building experience. The article serves as a foundational blueprint for future research and practical implementation, emphasizing the need for institutional support, financial investment, and faculty development to realize these benefits.

citation: Al-Hafdi, F.S., & Alhalafawy, W.S. (2024). Ten Years of Gamification-Based Learning: A Bibliometric Analysis and Systematic Review. International Journal of Interactive Mobile Technologies (iJIM), 18(7), 188–212.
target_sections: Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern|Games for Learning, Health and Well Being|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Libraries as Playful Spaces and Service Environments
summary_text:
Al-Hafdi and Alhalafawy (2024) present a comprehensive bibliometric analysis and systematic review of gamification-based learning (GBL) literature spanning ten years (2012–2021). The study addresses a gap in existing research by monitoring the developments, trends, and effects of gamification in educational environments. The primary objective is to provide scholars with a roadmap of key areas of emphasis, including prevalent terminology, geographic contributions, influential authors, and empirical findings regarding game elements and learning outcomes.

The methodology consists of two distinct phases. First, a bibliometric analysis was conducted using the Web of Science database. Search strings included terms such as "gamification," "gameful," and "gaming elements" combined with "education." This phase identified 3,196 records. Second, a systematic literature review was performed following PRISMA guidelines. Strict inclusion criteria required studies to be from Q1 or Q2 quartile journals, contain empirical evidence (quantitative, qualitative, or mixed methods), and explicitly detail the gamification components and their impact on learning outcomes. After excluding 3,043 records for various reasons (e.g., lack of empirical data, abstracts only, missing impact factors), a final sample of 52 studies was selected for detailed coding and analysis.

The bibliometric results reveal significant growth in the field, with publications increasing from seven in 2012 to 917 in 2021. The United States emerged as the leading contributor with 660 papers, followed by Spain (408) and Britain (276). *Computers in Human Behavior* and *Computers & Education* were identified as the most influential journals. Juho Hamari was cited as the most prolific and influential author in the domain. Keyword analysis highlighted "gamification," "motivation," "engagement," and "game-based learning" as central themes, alongside digital platforms like Moodle and Kahoot.

The systematic review of the 52 selected studies provided insights into practical applications. Undergraduate education was the most frequent setting for GBL implementation (35 studies), followed by primary education (14 studies). Secondary and intermediate levels were significantly less represented. Regarding game mechanics, points, leaderboards, and badges were the most commonly utilized elements. These elements are linked to educational theories such as goal-setting, self-efficacy, social comparison, and operant conditioning. The learning outcomes most frequently measured were achievement (24 studies), performance (16), engagement (14), and motivation (10). Other outcomes like satisfaction, attitude, and cognitive load were less commonly addressed.

The authors conclude that gamification is a rapidly developing field with substantial potential to enhance learner engagement and academic performance. They recommend that educators and designers prioritize points, leaderboards, and badges in digital platforms. Furthermore, they call for faculty training to align with gamification mechanisms and suggest future research expand into qualitative analyses of teacher and student perspectives, mixed-methods studies, and specific subject areas like science and mathematics. The study acknowledges limitations, including the exclusion of non-English publications and a heavy reliance on quasi-experimental designs in the analyzed literature.

citation: Alomair, Y., & Hammami, S. (2024). Enhancing the Achievement of Learning Outcomes for Foreign Language Learners Based on Gamification and Card Games. International Journal of Computer Games Technology, 2024, Article ID 8084687. https://doi.org/10.1155/2024/8084687
target_sections: Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Analog, Digital, and Hybrid Affordances|Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services
summary_text:
This study by Alomair and Hammami (2024) addresses a critical gap in foreign language learning (FLL) literature: the tendency of gamified and game-based approaches to prioritize student motivation while neglecting the rigorous enhancement of Student Learning Outcomes (SLOs). The authors propose and evaluate a novel framework that integrates gamification mechanics, card games, agent technology, and microlearning strategies to improve proficiency in listening, speaking, reading, and writing.

The methodology employed a quasi-experimental design involving 120 intermediate school students (aged 12–15) from Saudi Arabia, divided equally into an experimental group (n=60) and a control group (n=60). The experimental group utilized the proposed FLL framework, which centers on a Card Drafting Agent (CDA) developed using the Java Agent Development Framework (JADE). This agent retrieves content from a MySQL database to generate randomized card sets aligned with specific learning outcomes. Each card set includes microlearning content and quizzes for four language skills, plus a "prize card" to enhance engagement. The control group received traditional instruction. Data were collected over two six-week periods using achievement tests (quizzes and standardized evaluations) and surveys assessing motivation and dialectal challenges.

The findings demonstrate that the framework significantly improved SLO achievement. For Knowledge Learning Outcome (LO1), 72% of the experimental group showed improvement compared to 55% in the control group. For Skill Learning Outcome (LO2), the improvement rate was 90% in the experimental group versus 53% in the control group. Furthermore, survey data indicated higher motivation levels in the experimental group, with 75% strongly agreeing that the approach enhanced their persistence, compared to 45% in the control group. However, the study identified dialectal variation as a barrier for some students. Instructors largely endorsed the framework (86%) as a valuable adjunct to traditional teaching but advised against exclusive reliance on it due to technical access issues and the importance of teacher-led instruction.

The authors acknowledge several limitations, including the relatively small sample size constrained by time, the lack of qualitative data, and the study’s focus solely on English language outcomes. They recommend future research expand the participant pool, incorporate mixed-methods approaches, and test the framework’s applicability across different academic disciplines and diverse linguistic backgrounds. This work contributes to Games-Based Library Services (GBLS) literature by providing empirical evidence that structured, agent-driven gamification can effectively bridge the gap between engagement and measurable educational outcomes in language acquisition.

citation: Alvarez, Vivian G. 2017. "Power to the Pupil: Student Agency in the School Library." *Knowledge Quest* 45 (4): 40–48.
target_sections: Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy|Community, Belonging, Wellness, and the Public Commons|Game Design Workshops, Jams, and Participatory Creation
summary_text:
Vivian G. Alvarez’s 2017 article, "Power to the Pupil: Student Agency in the School Library," presents a qualitative case study advocating for the use of modern tabletop games as educational tools in library settings. The primary objective is to demonstrate how analog gaming can cultivate "student agency"—defined as the capacity for students to construct possibilities for themselves and act toward desired goals. Alvarez argues that while digital media often promotes passive consumption and instant gratification through trial-and-error mechanics, modern tabletop games require active strategic thinking, collaboration, and resilience, thereby fostering skills transferable to academic and professional contexts.

The study is situated in a public library media center in Chicago that functions as an out-of-school learning environment for middle and high school students from schools lacking dedicated libraries. The methodology relies on the author’s observational data collected over several years, beginning in spring 2015. Alvarez implemented a progressive gaming curriculum, starting with simpler games to build confidence and gradually introducing more complex titles. She developed an informal assessment framework to evaluate games based on three criteria: reliance on skills (strategy), reliance on luck (chance), and reading frequency (text engagement). This framework allowed her to categorize games such as *Agricola*, *Dominion*, *Splendor*, and *Pandemic* against traditional luck-based games like *Monopoly*.

The findings indicate a significant evolution in student engagement and skill acquisition. Initially, students struggled with text-heavy games like *Magic: The Gathering*, exhibiting low comprehension and interest due to habits formed by digital gaming that minimized literacy requirements. However, by introducing scaffolded games like *Splendor* and *Star Realms*, which required less reading but emphasized strategy, Alvarez successfully engaged participants. Over time, students progressed to complex cooperative and competitive games, such as *Mansions of Madness* and *Agricola*. Observations revealed that these experiences improved students’ ability to interpret rules, collaborate with peers, manage resources, and reassess strategies after failure. Notably, student preferences shifted from seeking luck-based outcomes to valuing self-determined strategic success, indicating a growth in agency and confidence.

Alvarez contrasts this analog experience with digital gaming trends, noting that many popular video games rely on repetitive trial-and-error mechanics that do not require critical thinking or deep engagement with instructions. In contrast, tabletop games provide a "level playing field" where outcomes are determined by player decisions rather than chance. The article highlights specific skills aligned with Tony Wagner’s seven skills for success, including critical thinking, collaboration, and adaptability. For instance, *Agricola* requires players to manage limited resources and prioritize tasks, mirroring the time management and decision-making required in academic settings.

The implications of this work suggest that libraries should actively curate modern tabletop games to support student development. Alvarez emphasizes the role of the educator or librarian as a moderator who scaffolds learning, helping students navigate complex rules and fostering an inclusive environment. By engaging in these analog experiences, students develop stamina, improve their comprehension skills, and learn to view failure as a strategic opportunity rather than a discouragement. The article concludes that integrating such games into library services can effectively bridge the gap between play and academic readiness, empowering students to take ownership of their learning trajectories.

citation: Anderson, K. (2022). Libraries & Lizards: Playing D&D at Public Libraries Program Framework.
target_sections: Programming_and_Facilitation|Service_Area|Service_Conditions_Addressed|Conceptual_Theme
summary_text:
Kate Anderson’s 2022 article, "Libraries & Lizards: Playing D&D at Public Libraries Program Framework," serves as an instructional guide for librarians seeking to implement Dungeons & Dragons (D&D) programs for young adults. The primary objective is to address the accessibility barriers young players face in finding games by utilizing the library as a safe, familiar, and resource-rich environment. The framework is explicitly designed for adolescents aged 15 to 18, targeting groups of 3–5 participants facilitated by a single staff member acting as the Dungeon Master (DM).

The methodology presented is prescriptive rather than empirical. Anderson outlines a detailed operational plan that includes material requirements, budget estimates, publicity strategies, and a structured timetable. The program utilizes the *Curse of Strahd* campaign guide, recommended for beginners, and can be adapted for one-off sessions or ongoing campaigns, both in-person and virtually. Session 1 is dedicated to introductions, character creation, and rule instruction, lasting approximately 4–5 hours. Subsequent sessions focus on gameplay, following a similar duration with breaks for snacks and resource exploration. The guide emphasizes staff preparation, recommending that DMs review materials thoroughly and utilize free resources from the official D&D website.

A significant component of the framework is its alignment with the Search Institute’s (1997) "40 Developmental Assets for Adolescents." Anderson claims that the program supports various developmental goals, including safety, positive peer influence, creative activities, and interpersonal competence. For instance, the game mechanics are described as fostering responsibility through consequence-based decision-making and enhancing self-esteem through success and confidence building. The library setting is positioned as crucial for providing supervised, emotionally safe interactions between youth and staff, as well as among peers.

The article provides a detailed budget analysis based on 2021 prices, estimating costs between $84 and $442 CAD for initial materials such as campaign guides, player handbooks, dice, and snacks. Anderson notes that while these upfront costs may seem high, they are largely one-time investments that can be reused over extended periods. The use of digital platforms like D&D Beyond is suggested as a cost-effective alternative for accessing books and tools, particularly for virtual programs.

Evaluation within this framework is informal, relying on staff debriefing with participants after each session to assess enjoyment and identify areas for improvement. The article does not present quantitative data or empirical results from implemented programs but rather offers a replicable model for library services. It concludes by emphasizing the flexibility of the program, allowing libraries to adapt it to their specific resources and community needs, thereby promoting literacy, social skills, and creative engagement among young adults.

citation: Asercion, C. (2023). "i'm sorry did you say street magic" & there are names here more powerful than our own. GameRT Resources.

summary_text:
The provided text is a descriptive resource document from 2023, authored by Caro Asercion, detailing two tabletop role-playing games (TTRPGs): *i'm sorry did you say street magic* and *there are names here more powerful than our own*. The document does not present empirical research or data analysis; rather, it functions as a promotional and informational guide, likely intended for library patrons or gaming communities associated with GameRT.

The primary focus is on defining the mechanics and scope of *i'm sorry did you say street magic*, which is characterized as a "GMless city-building story game." The text specifies that this game supports two to six players and has a duration of three hours or more. The core gameplay loop involves collaborative discovery and imagination, where players construct a city filled with neighborhoods, landmarks, and residents. A key mechanic highlighted is the discovery of "true names" and the intersection of these entities, allowing players to set events in motion that alter relationships within the constructed world.

The second title, *there are names here more powerful than our own*, is described as a "worldbuilder" and a sister game to the primary title. It is noted for its versatility, capable of being used as a standalone tool by one or more players or in conjunction with *street magic*. The text provides links to video recordings (VOD) of actual play sessions, offering visual evidence of the games in action, though no quantitative data from these sessions is analyzed.

A significant portion of the text is dedicated to contextualizing these games within a broader ecosystem of media and safety practices. It lists "Play Alikes," comparing the titles to other TTRPGs such as *Microscope* by Ben Robbins and *The Quiet Year* by Avery Alder, as well as video games like *Cozy Grove* and *Dorfromantik*. This suggests a thematic alignment with cozy, atmospheric, and narrative-driven experiences. Similarly, "Read Alikes" include works by N.K. Jemisin (*The City We Became*) and Neil Gaiman (*Neverwhere*), reinforcing the urban fantasy and mythic undertones of the games.

Crucially for library services, the text explicitly lists "Safety Tools," including *Script Change*, *Lines and Veils*, and *The Door is Open*. This indicates a deliberate emphasis on creating safe and inclusive gaming environments, a critical consideration for public library programming. The document also outlines the GameRT Twitch Policy, which mandates kindness, prohibits hate speech, and provides mechanisms for reporting harassment, further underscoring the importance of community standards in these spaces.

In summary, this text serves as a curated entry point for librarians and patrons interested in narrative-driven, GMless TTRPGs. It provides essential metadata regarding player count, duration, and thematic content, while also offering resources for safety and related media. The absence of empirical findings limits its utility for quantitative analysis, but it offers valuable qualitative insights into the design philosophy and community guidelines surrounding these specific games.
