The primary objective is twofold: to advocate for greater adoption of formative assessment among instruction librarians and to demonstrate that educational games serve as effective vehicles for such assessments. The author argues that while summative assessments (e.g., LibQual+, Project SAILS) are prevalent, they are often resource-intensive and occur too late to improve immediate student learning. In contrast, formative assessments provide continuous feedback during the learning process, allowing instructors to adjust instruction in real-time.

The methodology consists of a qualitative synthesis of literature on formative assessment and game-based learning, followed by a case study analysis of two information literacy games developed at Lycoming College: "Secret Agents in the Library" and "Goblin Threat." The author identifies eight pedagogical elements shared by both fields: active learning, feedback, motivation, moratoriums (low-stakes environments), scaffolding, critical thinking/self-assessment, social learning, and stealth assessment.

The population for the case studies includes freshman composition students at Lycoming College for "Secret Agents in the Library," a hybrid physical-digital game designed to teach research skills. For "Goblin Threat," an online plagiarism prevention game, the data encompasses usage by hundreds of Lycoming students and over seventy external institutions. Quantitative data reveals that "Goblin Threat" received over 54,000 page visits between January 2011 and the time of publication. Qualitative data includes observations of student engagement, focus group feedback during development, and post-game satisfaction surveys.

Key findings indicate strong alignment between game mechanics and formative assessment principles. For instance, games provide immediate, specific feedback (e.g., sound effects, mini-tutorials after incorrect answers) which mirrors the ideal characteristics of formative feedback. The "moratorium" concept is illustrated by allowing unlimited attempts in "Goblin Threat," creating a safe space for students to practice without grade penalties. Observations from "Secret Agents" showed high intrinsic motivation, with students engaging in role-playing behaviors beyond game requirements. Survey results for "Goblin Threat" indicated that students found the experience enjoyable and preferred it to traditional instruction methods. The author notes that while technical limitations often constrain library games to simple formats (e.g., multiple-choice), effective pedagogical design is more critical than technological sophistication.

The implications for library practice are significant. Broussard recommends that librarians consciously integrate formative assessment into one-shot sessions, utilizing games to foster intrinsic motivation and reduce assessment anxiety through "stealth assessment." The article emphasizes the necessity of aligning game activities with learning objectives from the initial design phase and incorporating debriefing sessions to facilitate knowledge transfer. The author concludes that increasing awareness of these methodologies can lead to more effective, engaging, and responsive library instruction.

citation: Brown, R. T., & Kasper, T. (2013). The Fusion of Literacy and Games: A Case Study in Assessing the Goals of a Library Video Game Program. *Library Trends*, 61(4), 755–778.
target_sections: Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy|Community, Belonging, Wellness, and the Public Commons|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services
summary_text:
This article presents a qualitative case study investigating the impact of a library video game program on youth participants, with a specific focus on learning outcomes and assessment practices. Conducted by Ron T. Brown and Tamara Kasper, the research addresses the growing integration of gaming in libraries and the need to evaluate how these programs meet institutional goals such as improved literacy and positive behavior.

The study was conducted over a four-month period (June–October 2010) across two branches of the Elizabeth County library system in South Carolina. The primary methodology involved 90 hours of participant observation by one researcher, who recorded field notes on staff interactions, participant behaviors, and assessment procedures. Data were analyzed using a grounded theory framework to identify emergent themes. The setting included two distinct environments: Library A, which had a dedicated, enclosed gaming room with specialized staff, and Library B, a smaller facility where the gaming area was integrated into the main entrance space with dual-role staff.

The analysis yielded three primary thematic categories: staff dynamics, participant experiences, and assessment practices. Regarding staff, the researchers found that library employees served as effective role models who enforced rules while fostering an innovative learning environment. Staff members actively mentored youth, used "teachable moments" to introduce information literacy concepts, and adapted programming based on participant interests, such as initiating robotics clubs or digital arts projects. Rule enforcement was strict regarding dress code and academic performance; participants were required to maintain a report card with no more than one "D" grade to retain gaming privileges.

Concerning game club members, the study observed the development of a strong community identity, described by participants as an "extended family." Motivation was largely driven by a reward-based system where reading books or completing tasks earned additional gaming time. While most interactions were positive, the researchers noted instances of emotional volatility, including frustration during competitive play and disruptive behavior following losses. Despite these challenges, participants demonstrated improvements in various skill sets, including technology literacy, information literacy, and social skills such as collaboration and respect for shared resources.

The third theme focused on assessment. The existing program relied on basic metrics such as attendance, book checkout counts, and report card verification. The authors argue that while these measures track participation, they fail to capture the depth of learning occurring within the informal setting. The study suggests that current assessments are insufficient for measuring complex outcomes like critical thinking or specific literacy gains.

In their discussion and recommendations, Brown and Kasper propose several enhancements for library gaming programs. They advocate for the adoption of standardized assessment tools, such as the Motivation to Read Profile or the Library Anxiety Scale, to quantitatively measure changes in participant literacy and comfort levels. Furthermore, they recommend shifting from punitive measures for poor behavior to reward-based systems that incentivize positive social conduct and academic achievement. The authors also suggest physical improvements, such as placing reference materials and dictionaries directly within gaming areas to facilitate immediate literacy interventions. Finally, they emphasize the potential for libraries to partner with schools to provide targeted support for disinterested learners, thereby leveraging the motivational power of games to reengage youth in educational processes. The study concludes that while library game clubs are valuable community resources, their full potential can only be realized through more sophisticated and comprehensive assessment strategies.

citation: Buchanan, K., & Vanden Elzen, A. M. (2012). Beyond a Fad: Why Video Games Should Be Part of 21st Century Libraries. Education Libraries, 35(1-2), 15–33.
target_sections: Games as Mainstream Cultural Media|Games for Learning, Health and Well Being|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Community, Belonging, Wellness, and the Public Commons|Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern
summary_text:
This article by Kym Buchanan and Angela M. Vanden Elzen argues that video games are a significant medium worthy of inclusion in 21st-century library services. The authors contend that libraries must evolve to remain relevant, particularly in the face of budget cuts and changing media landscapes. They propose that libraries can fulfill their core missions of providing access, motivation, and guidance by integrating video games into their collections and programming.

To illustrate the communicative power of video games, the authors analyze two specific titles: *September 12th* and *Beyond Good & Evil*. *September 12th*, a browser-based game released in 2003, simulates missile strikes on a Middle Eastern city to demonstrate the collateral damage and cyclical nature of violence in the "war on terror." The authors use this example to highlight how interactivity allows players to experience consequences firsthand, creating a more impactful message than passive media. *Beyond Good & Evil*, a console game from 2003, is presented as a sophisticated narrative tool that explores themes of patriotism, propaganda, and dissent through the role of an investigative journalist. These examples serve to demonstrate that games can encode complex ideas and provoke critical thinking, thereby honoring the spirit of the First Amendment.

The authors define video games by three distinguishing traits: they are visual, interactive, and based on simulations. They argue that these traits demand a mix of traditional literacies (such as interpreting metaphor and author intent) and new literacies (such as understanding simulation mechanics and systemic consequences). For instance, players must develop mental models of the simulated world through trial and error, while also critically assessing the designer's assumptions and biases.

The article identifies three primary motivations for playing video games: immersion, challenge, and connection. Immersion refers to the semi-voluntary experience of being transported into a new context, which can be more intense in games due to player agency. Challenge relates to the concept of "flow," where optimal difficulty leads to engagement and learning; the authors note that the low stakes of the "magic circle" of play encourage risk-taking and mistake-making, which are beneficial for learning. Connection encompasses self-discovery through identity exploration, social bonding with other players, and broader societal dialogue about values and ethics.

Practically, the authors provide guidelines for librarians to implement these concepts. For selection, they advise considering the target audience (general public, educators, or scholars), age appropriateness via ESRB ratings, and genre diversity. They recommend waiting 8–12 months after a new console release before purchasing hardware to ensure market stability. For access, libraries should catalog games effectively, consider circulating consoles, and address security concerns with protective cases. To motivate patrons, the authors suggest organizing gaming tournaments, forming interest groups for multiplayer online games (MMOs), and using games as gateways to related books or non-fiction materials. They also emphasize the importance of staff training to help librarians understand gaming culture and facilitate critical discussions.

The authors acknowledge potential harms, such as sedentary behavior and exposure to inappropriate content, but argue that libraries can mitigate these through guidance and by promoting active gaming systems like the Nintendo Wii. Ultimately, the article posits that by embracing video games, libraries can reach new audiences, foster critical literacy, and support the evolving cultural landscape of media consumption.

citation: Buller, R. (2017). Lending video game consoles in an academic library. portal: Libraries and the Academy, 17(2), 337-346. https://doi.org/10.1353/pla.2017.0020
target_sections: Defining Games-Based Library Services|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Ground Services in Purpose, Audience, and Institutional Context|Service Purposes and Library Functions
summary_text:
Ryan Buller’s 2017 article, "Lending Video Game Consoles in an Academic Library," presents a case study of the University of Denver’s decision to implement a video game console lending service. The initiative was driven by the need to support the Emergent Digital Practices program, an interdepartmental curriculum focused on video game design and creation. Funded partially by a grant awarded to a professor in the program, the project aimed to provide students with access to hardware necessary for analyzing games across different platforms and generations, addressing a gap where traditional library resources failed to meet the specific technical needs of game design students.

The article details the decision-making process that led to choosing circulation over establishing a dedicated gaming space within the library. While a dedicated space would have offered greater control over equipment and eliminated barriers for students lacking televisions, the University of Denver’s limited physical space in its Academic Commons model made this impractical. Furthermore, the academic goal required students to have extended access to hardware for comparative analysis, which circulation facilitated more effectively than restricted in-library use. The library purchased current and previous generation consoles from Sony, Microsoft, and Nintendo, along with GAEMS Personal Gaming Environments to ensure portability and protection, allowing students to use the devices off-campus with built-in screens.

Implementation presented several challenges, primarily concerning patron accountability and software security. To address accountability, the library developed extensive check-in forms with images of all components to ensure complete returns and maintained detailed serial number records for internal hardware parts to prevent component swapping or theft. Software security was managed by creating default library accounts that prevented users from locking out other patrons or storing credit card information on the devices. Staff verified that all user profiles were logged out upon return, a process that slowed circulation but ensured system integrity and patron privacy.

The article also addresses internal resistance from library staff who questioned the appropriateness of lending non-traditional materials. This was mitigated by framing the service as a direct response to demonstrated curricular needs rather than a general attempt to attract patrons. The outcome has been positive: circulation numbers met expectations without formal marketing, and feedback led to the addition of extra controllers. The project is cited as evidence that academic libraries can successfully expand their collections to include hardware, thereby reinforcing their role as active partners in supporting evolving academic disciplines.

citation: The Reign of Children: The Role of Games and Toys in American Public Libraries, 1876–1925
target_sections: Games as Cultural Media|Service Ecology|Learning and Literacy|Community and Inclusion
summary_text:
Jennifer Burek Pierce’s 2016 article, "The Reign of Children: The Role of Games and Toys in American Public Libraries, 1876–1925," provides a historical analysis of the integration of nonbook media into early American public library youth services. The study challenges the traditional historiography that portrays libraries primarily as repositories for serious literature, arguing instead that games, toys, and visual aids were foundational to the development of children's librarianship during this period.

The methodology relies on an examination of professional library literature from the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, alongside archival artifacts such as dissected maps, sliced animals, scrapbooks, and stereopticon slides. Pierce situates these materials within the broader context of the child study movement and Froebelian kindergarten education, which emphasized tactile learning and the distinct developmental needs of children. Early librarians, influenced by figures like G. Stanley Hall and Friedrich Froebel, believed that young patrons required engaging, multimodal resources to foster interest and comprehension, particularly given the limited literacy skills of many children at the time.

Key findings indicate that children's rooms were designed as active, "homelike" spaces filled with colorful pictures, puzzles, and creative supplies. Dissected maps, for instance, were not merely novelties but educational tools that taught geography, morality, and national identity while requiring manual dexterity and cognitive engagement. Similarly, scrapbooks and sliced animal games promoted literacy and concentration. These resources were intended to attract children who might otherwise be intimidated by traditional library environments, thereby expanding the library's reach to diverse socioeconomic groups.

However, the article highlights a significant professional tension. While youth services librarians championed these experimental methods, senior administrators and adult services professionals often viewed them with suspicion. Critics argued that games and amusements detracted from the library's dignity and its core mission of providing "good books" for self-improvement. This conflict culminated in a shift toward standardization and efficiency in the 1920s. Influenced by calls for professional uniformity, the profession largely abandoned the playful, experimental approaches of the earlier era. The "reign of children," characterized by peer interaction and creative expression, was curtailed in favor of book-centered services that aligned with adult library norms.

Pierce concludes by drawing parallels between this historical period and contemporary debates over digital media and games in libraries. She suggests that current librarians facing skepticism about nonbook resources can find validation in the historical precedent of early youth services professionals who successfully advocated for the educational value of play. The article serves as a reminder that the tension between institutional tradition and innovative, user-centered services is a recurring theme in library history, urging modern practitioners to recognize the enduring relevance of engaging, multimodal approaches to youth engagement.

citation: Burgess, J., & Wallace, A. (2021). Using Investigative Video Games to Teach Clinical Intuition and Metacognition in Reference Transactions. Journal of Education for Library and Information Science, 62(4), 477–491.
target_sections: Service Purposes and Library Functions|Learning and Literacy|Service Conditions and Institutional Context|Game Modalities in Library Contexts
summary_text:
Burgess and Wallace (2021) address a critical pedagogical challenge in Library and Information Science (LIS) education: how to effectively teach "clinical intuition" and metacognition for reference transactions, particularly within the growing context of online Master of Library and Information Science (MLIS) programs. The authors argue that traditional reference instruction relies heavily on real-time interaction and immediate feedback, elements that are often diminished in asynchronous online environments. To address this, the article employs a concept analysis method to define clinical intuition in reference work and proposes investigative video games as a viable pedagogical tool for developing these skills.

The authors begin by defining clinical intuition not as a vague "gut feeling," but as a form of professional judgment refined through experience, metacognition, and the iterative testing of ad hoc rules. Drawing parallels with nursing practice, they posit that reference workers must possess self-awareness and situational awareness to adapt their approaches during fluid, unscripted patron interactions. This intuition is developed through metacognitive reflection—thinking about one’s own thinking processes—and requires opportunities to test hypotheses and receive feedback on decision-making outcomes.

The study identifies a specific limitation in current online LIS education: while asynchronous courses can effectively deliver domain knowledge (e.g., database features, search syntax), they often fail to provide the dynamic, interactive practice necessary for refining clinical judgment. Without live, real-time feedback, students may struggle to internalize the complex social, cognitive, and behavioral skills required for effective reference interviews. The authors contend that this gap risks leaving new librarians to learn these critical skills on the job, potentially compromising service quality.

To bridge this gap, Burgess and Wallace introduce a conceptual model linking three types of reasoning—abductive, deductive, and inductive—to specific phases of the reference transaction. Abductive reasoning involves forming hypotheses about patron needs; deductive reasoning applies known principles to select resources; and inductive reasoning evaluates evidence from search results. The authors then analyze three investigative video games—*Her Story*, *Return of the Obra Dinn*, and *Subserial Network*—as case studies demonstrating how game mechanics naturally engage these reasoning skills. For instance, *Her Story* requires players to use inductive trial-and-error with keyword searches to uncover narrative clues, mirroring the iterative process of refining search strategies. *Return of the Obra Dinn* demands abductive inference to determine character fates from fragmented evidence, paralleling the need to infer tacit patron questions.

The authors argue that these games offer three key advantages for LIS pedagogy: they allow for autonomous, self-paced learning; they provide immediate, tangible feedback on hypothesis testing; and they deliver this feedback in the same mode as the activity, reinforcing the connection between action and outcome. This aligns with Experiential Learning Theory (ELT), which emphasizes the dialectic of action/reflection and experience/abstraction. By engaging with these games, students can practice rule-testing and metacognitive reflection in a low-stakes environment.

However, the authors acknowledge several limitations. Investigative games are not educational tools designed for LIS; thus, instructors must explicitly connect game mechanics to reference competencies. Additionally, some games contain violent or unsettling content, suggesting they should be offered as optional supplements rather than mandatory assignments. The article concludes that while the current research is conceptual, it highlights a promising avenue for enhancing online reference instruction. The authors call for future empirical studies to validate whether using investigative video games significantly improves students’ reasoning skills and clinical intuition in professional practice.

citation: Capdarest-Arest, N., Opuda, E., & Stark, R. K. (2019). “Game on!” Teaching gamification principles for library instruction to health sciences information professionals using interactive, low-tech activities and design-thinking modalities. *Journal of the Medical Library Association*, 107(4), 566–571.
target_sections: Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Learning and Literacy|Programming and Facilitation|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern
summary_text:
This case report by Capdarest-Arest, Opuda, and Stark (2019) details the design, implementation, and evaluation of an interactive workshop aimed at teaching health sciences librarians how to incorporate low-tech gamification into library instruction. The authors argue that while high-tech games are prevalent in literature, they often require resources (time, money, coding skills) that many librarians lack. In contrast, low-tech games offer accessibility and foster face-to-face collaboration. The study’s primary objective was to determine if a short, hands-on session using design-thinking principles could increase librarians' confidence and intent to use gamification in their instructional settings.

The methodology centered on a 90-minute special content session at the 2018 Medical Library Association (MLA) annual meeting, titled “Design, Play, Learn.” The pedagogical framework integrated multimodal instruction, including pre-session readings (flipped classroom), a brief didactic lecture, and active learning through game prototyping. The session utilized the design-thinking process—empathize, define, ideate, prototype, and test—to guide participants in creating tangible, low-tech games using accessible office supplies. A sample card game focused on database searching was provided as a reference model. Data were collected via anonymous five-item Likert scale surveys administered before and after the session to measure changes in confidence, experience, and future usage intent.

The population consisted of health sciences librarians attending MLA ’18. The session format was informal, with no mandatory registration, allowing participants to join or leave at will. This flexibility introduced a limitation in tracking exact attendance numbers; however, 43 participants completed the pre-session survey, and 35 completed the post-session survey. The authors acknowledge that the "ebb and flow" of attendees made it difficult to calculate an accurate return rate for the surveys.

The findings demonstrated substantial positive shifts in participant attitudes. Confidence in using gamification increased from 28% (12 out of 43 respondents) pre-session to 83% (29 out of 35 respondents) post-session. Similarly, the intent to incorporate session learnings into future instruction rose from 77% to 94%, and the likelihood of using the specific game created during the workshop increased from 46% to 86%. Qualitative feedback reinforced these quantitative results, with participants describing the session as highly helpful and noting that it facilitated professional networking and immediate application of skills in their own institutions.

The authors conclude that brief, hands-on workshops are effective for building librarian confidence in game design. They identify several implications for future practice, including the need for extended time for debriefing and sharing, the value of allowing participants to play sample games before designing their own, and the potential benefit of splitting sessions into design-focused and assessment-focused components. The study underscores the viability of low-tech gamification as a resource-efficient strategy for enhancing library instruction engagement.

citation: Casucci, T., Locke, A. B., Henson, A., & Qeadan, F. (2020). A workplace well-being game intervention for health sciences librarians to address burnout. Journal of the Medical Library Association, 108(4), 605–617.
target_sections: Service_Conditions_Addressed|Service_Area|Conceptual_Theme|Intended_Outcome
summary_text:
This study investigates the prevalence of burnout among health sciences librarians at the Spencer S. Eccles Health Sciences Library and evaluates the efficacy of a serious game intervention designed to enhance workplace well-being and collegiality. The authors define burnout as a psychological syndrome characterized by emotional exhaustion, depersonalization, and reduced personal accomplishment, noting that while burnout is well-documented in healthcare, it has been less frequently studied specifically within health sciences librarianship.

The methodology involved a longitudinal survey design using a modified American Medical Association (AMA) Mini-Z burnout instrument. Surveys were administered to library faculty in 2016 and to both faculty and staff in 2017 and 2018. Between the 2017 and 2018 surveys, the researchers implemented a three-month "Wellness Game." The game was designed using design box methodology to target personal resilience and social connection, focusing on four dimensions: physical, mental, social, and appreciation. Participants were organized into teams and earned points for wellness activities, with bonus points for collaborative efforts. The intervention aimed to give employees control over their participation while fostering a sense of community.

The population consisted of library faculty and staff. Response rates varied across the three survey periods, ranging from 33% to 93%. Demographic analysis revealed no significant differences in sex, age, or race between faculty and staff groups, although reporting on Latino/Hispanic origin differed significantly. The sample size was small, necessitating the use of exact tests for statistical comparisons and Cohen’s h for effect size estimation.

Key findings indicate that burnout levels were initially high, particularly among faculty, with 38%–73% reporting emotional exhaustion and 54%–91% reporting job-related stress. Staff also reported significant burnout (62% in 2017). Post-intervention analysis showed that while there were non-significant trends toward reduced burnout symptoms and increased perceived control over workload, the game intervention did not produce statistically significant improvements in overall burnout or job satisfaction. However, qualitative data from post-game surveys highlighted positive impacts on social dynamics. Seventy percent of respondents indicated the game encouraged socialization with colleagues. Thematic analysis identified sociability, motivation, gameplay, and fun as the most enjoyable aspects of the intervention. Conversely, participants cited tedious record-keeping, low team engagement, and excessive duration as drawbacks.

The authors conclude that while the serious game successfully improved collegiality and recognition among employees, it failed to significantly mitigate burnout or enhance job satisfaction. They argue that games alone cannot address the root organizational causes of burnout, such as workload demands and leadership alignment. The study suggests that effective well-being strategies require a multifactorial approach, combining individual-focused interventions like games with broader organizational changes. This research contributes to the literature on Games-Based Library Services by demonstrating the potential of gamification for team building while highlighting its limitations in addressing deep-seated professional stressors.

citation: Halo ‘n’ Horns by Connie Chang (2024)
target_sections: Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Programming Formats and Service Models|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Game Design Workshops, Jams, and Participatory Creation|Fandom Festivals and Pop-Culture Events
summary_text:
The provided source text is not an empirical research article but rather a descriptive resource and session summary associated with a live-streamed tabletop role-playing game (TTRPG) event. The central subject is *Halo ‘n’ Horns*, a comedy micro RPG created by Connie Chang. The text explicitly defines the game’s core mechanic: it is designed for two Game Masters, who assume the roles of an angel and a devil, and one player, who portrays a mortal that the GMs are attempting to "win over." This structure suggests a competitive yet collaborative narrative framework focused on persuasion and character interaction within a supernatural context.

The document serves multiple informational functions relevant to Games-Based Library Services (GBLS). First, it provides bibliographic and contextual metadata, identifying the creator and offering links to social media profiles. Second, it situates *Halo ‘n’ Horns* within a broader ecosystem of similar games and media. The "Play Alikes" section lists other TTRPGs with thematic or mechanical parallels, such as *Heaven Has No Taste*, *Heaven Nor Hell*, and *Blazing Hymn*. The "Read/Watch Alikes" section connects the game to popular culture touchstones including *Good Omens*, *The Devil is a Part-Timer*, *Lucky Penny*, *Sweet Paprika*, *Heaven’s Design Team*, *The Good Place*, *The City We Became*, and *American Gods*. These comparisons help librarians or facilitators understand the tone, genre, and audience appeal of the game, suggesting it aligns with comedic, supernatural, and morally ambiguous narratives.

A significant portion of the text is dedicated to safety and moderation protocols, which are critical considerations for library-based gaming programs. The document lists specific "Safety Tools" recommended for use during play, including "Script Change" by Beau Jágr Sheldon and "The Door is Open" by Eirik Fatland, along with references to the TTRPG Safety Toolkit. These tools are designed to help participants manage content boundaries and ensure a comfortable environment for all players. Furthermore, the text outlines the "Game RT Twitch Policy," which establishes clear behavioral expectations for online audiences: participants must be kind and respectful, hate speech is prohibited, moderators reserve the right to remove disruptive individuals, and channels for reporting harassment or private communication are provided. These policies highlight the importance of structured moderation in public or semi-public gaming events, a key lesson for libraries hosting similar programs.

The text also summarizes the structure of the specific streaming event hosted by GameRT, an initiative associated with the American Library Association (ALA), as indicated by the contact email `gamert@ala.org`. The session was divided into three parts: an introduction and rules explanation, the actual play demonstration, and a post-game discussion. The "Actual Play" featured participant Aabria Iyengar, a well-known figure in the TTRPG community. While the text does not provide empirical data or analysis of player engagement, it offers a model for how such events can be structured: beginning with clear instruction, moving to demonstration, and concluding with reflective discussion.

In the context of a GBLS literature review, this document contributes by illustrating the practical components of facilitating a TTRPG event. It underscores the necessity of selecting appropriate games based on thematic alignment, integrating safety mechanisms to protect participants, and establishing clear codes of conduct for both players and observers. The absence of empirical findings means the text cannot inform statistical trends or measured outcomes regarding library gaming programs. However, it serves as a valuable case study in event planning and resource curation, demonstrating how libraries can leverage existing TTRPG titles while prioritizing community safety and inclusive practices. The reference to GameRT resources further points librarians toward established networks and support systems for implementing games-based services.

citation: Chapman, A. (2021). Trials of Metadata: Emerging Schemas for Videogame Cataloguing. Journal of Library Metadata, 21(3-4), 63–103. DOI: 10.1080/19386389.2021.2007729
target_sections: Conceptual Terrain: Games, Play, and Library Service|Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Analog, Digital, and Hybrid Affordances|Libraries as Playful Spaces and Service Environments|Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services
summary_text:
Abigail Chapman’s 2021 article, "Trials of Metadata: Emerging Schemas for Videogame Cataloguing," addresses the pressing need for standardized metadata practices as videogames enter library and museum collections. The study conducts a comparative analysis of five distinct metadata schemas to evaluate how they handle the unique complexities of the medium compared to traditional standards like Resource Description and Access (RDA). The selected schemas include two academic initiatives—the Core Metadata Schema (CMS) by GAMECIP and the Video Game Metadata Schema (VGMS) by the GAMER Group—one fan-community database with formal standards (MobyGames), and two commercial distribution platforms (Steam and Itch.io).

The methodology involves comparing these schemas against eleven core RDA elements identified by Online Audiovisual Cataloguers (OLAC) and analyzing their approaches to genre classification. The study finds that traditional cataloguing rules struggle with the hybrid physical-digital nature of games, particularly regarding title sources, edition definitions, and carrier types. For instance, determining the primary source for a title is complicated by discrepancies between packaging, menus, and digital storefronts. While RDA prioritizes the title screen, videogame-specific schemas often allow for broader sourcing to capture variant or colloquial titles used by players.

A significant finding is the universal inclusion of a distinct "platform" element across all analyzed schemas, separate from edition or system requirements. This reflects the critical role hardware and software environments play in defining the game experience. Similarly, the concept of "publisher" is often decoupled from "developer," with schemas emphasizing the developer’s creative role, which is frequently obscured in traditional bibliographic records. MobyGames demonstrates high granularity by offering controlled vocabularies for nearly forty contributor roles, whereas academic schemas like VGMS use broader agent elements but provide detailed faceted descriptions for other attributes.

The analysis of genre reveals that single-term classification is inadequate for videogames. Commercial platforms utilize user-generated tags, which offer rich, emergent terminology regarding gameplay mechanics and visual style but lack the consistency required for formal library cataloguing. In contrast, academic schemas decompose genre into multiple facets: gameplay (ludic elements), narrative (plot/themes), and visual style (artistic presentation). VGMS, for example, employs separate vocabularies for mood, dimension, and point of view, acknowledging that these visual aspects are integral to the user experience.

Chapman concludes that while RDA provides a foundational structure, it lacks the medium-specific granularity necessary for effective videogame cataloguing. The emerging schemas from academic and fan communities offer valuable models for enhancing bibliographic records, particularly through detailed controlled vocabularies for platforms, media types, and multifaceted genre terms. These developments are essential for supporting scholarly research, digital preservation, and improved user discovery in library environments.

citation: Chen, C.-H., Law, V., & Huang, K. (2023). Adaptive scaffolding and engagement in digital game-based learning. *Educational Technology Research and Development*, 71, 1785–1798. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11423-023-10244-x
target_sections: Games for Learning, Health and Well Being|Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern|Service Purposes and Library Functions
summary_text:
Chen, Law, and Huang (2023) present a quasi-experimental study investigating the efficacy of adaptive scaffolding versus fixed scaffolding within a digital game-based learning (DGBL) environment. The primary objective was to determine how adaptive instructional support influences students' conceptual understanding of physics (specifically Newton’s laws) and their multidimensional engagement, defined as cognitive (self-regulation and deep strategy), emotional, and behavioral. This study addresses a gap in the literature where previous meta-analyses have shown inconsistent results regarding DGBL effectiveness, particularly in science education, and where few studies have examined adaptive scaffolding through a comprehensive engagement framework.

The participants were 61 seventh-grade students from a secondary school in Taiwan, randomly assigned by class to either a fixed scaffolding group ($N=30$) or an adaptive scaffolding group ($N=31$). The intervention utilized "Summon of Magicrystal," a computer-based interactive game designed to teach physics concepts through gameplay mechanics involving time, distance, and velocity. In the fixed condition, students received pre-planned scaffolds in a static sequence regardless of their individual progress. In the adaptive condition, an automated system analyzed student log data, game progress, and answer results to trigger scaffolds only when specific behavioral thresholds indicated a need for support, thereby tailoring assistance to individual cognitive levels.

Data were collected via pre- and post-tests measuring conceptual understanding and validated questionnaires assessing four engagement subscales: self-regulation, deep strategy, behavioral engagement, and emotional engagement. The study employed a 2x2 factorial ANCOVA with pre-test scores as covariates to analyze performance and moderation effects, and a one-way MANOVA to compare engagement levels between groups.

The results indicated that adaptive scaffolding significantly improved learning performance compared to fixed scaffolding, demonstrating a large effect size ($\eta^2=0.263$). Students in the adaptive group achieved higher post-test scores than their counterparts in the fixed group. Regarding engagement, the adaptive scaffolding group exhibited significantly higher levels of self-regulation, deep strategy use, and emotional engagement compared to the fixed group. However, no significant difference was found in behavioral engagement between the two conditions. The authors hypothesize that while adaptive scaffolds reduced unnecessary cognitive load and allowed for deeper cognitive processing, they did not necessarily increase observable participation or effort metrics captured by behavioral engagement scales.

Furthermore, the study tested whether engagement moderated the relationship between scaffolding type and learning performance. Contrary to expectations, none of the four engagement dimensions showed a significant interaction effect with scaffolding type on learning outcomes. This suggests that while adaptive scaffolding enhances both performance and certain engagement types, the mechanism by which it improves learning is not statistically mediated or moderated by these specific engagement measures in this context.

The authors conclude that adaptive scaffolding offers substantial benefits for DGBL by supporting diverse learner needs without disrupting game flow or imposing excessive cognitive load. They recommend future research explore different methods for generating adaptive support, investigate the impact on intrinsic motivation more thoroughly, and develop more inclusive measures for emotional engagement. The study provides robust empirical evidence supporting the integration of adaptive technologies in educational games to enhance both academic achievement and specific facets of learner engagement.

citation: Chen et al., "Development of Game-Based Augmented Reality Navigation System for Makerspace User Education," 2023 14th IIAI International Congress on Advanced Applied Informatics (IIAI-AAI), pp. 218-223, DOI: 10.1109/IIAI-AAI59060.2023.00052
target_sections: Learning and Literacy|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services
summary_text:
This study investigates the efficacy of game-based augmented reality (AR) tools in facilitating user education within library makerspaces. As libraries evolve into creative hubs providing access to advanced technologies like 3D printers and laser cutters, traditional instructional methods often struggle to engage users or ensure retention of complex operational knowledge. The authors argue that while web-based tutorials and librarian-led tours are common, they may lack the interactivity required for deep learning. Consequently, this research develops and evaluates a "Game-Based AR Navigation System" (ARNS) called Mini-Universe, designed to teach makerspace equipment usage through an alternative reality puzzle game format.

The methodology employed a quasi-experimental design with 36 first-year graduate students from a high school in Taiwan, all of whom were novice users of the library’s makerspace. Participants were randomly assigned to one of three groups: (1) an experimental group using the ARNS via handheld mobile devices; (2) a control group using a web-based navigation system; and (3) a control group participating in a traditional narrative guided tour led by a librarian. The ARNS was developed using Mini-Universe software, leveraging cognitive scaffolding theory. It required users to scan physical "stickers" placed on makerspace equipment to collect clues, solve puzzles, and complete missions related to machine operation. This gamified approach included elements of challenge, peer competition, and immediate feedback via an "energy stone" mechanic that prevented random guessing and encouraged careful analysis of information.

Learning effectiveness was assessed using pre-tests and post-tests comprising knowledge-based, combination, and sorting questions regarding four specific machines: 3D printers, stereolithography units, thermal transfer printers, and laser cutter machines. Statistical analyses included Wilcoxon signed-rank tests for within-group changes and Kruskal-Wallis tests with Conover’s post-hoc comparisons for between-group differences.

The quantitative findings revealed that all three groups demonstrated significant improvement in learning outcomes from pre-test to post-test, indicating that each method was effective in transmitting basic knowledge. However, comparative analysis showed no statistically significant difference in learning effectiveness between the ARNS group and the traditional librarian-guided tour group, nor between the web navigation group and the librarian group. Crucially, the ARNS group performed significantly better than the web navigation group (p < 0.05). This suggests that while gamified AR is not superior to direct human instruction in this context, it is markedly more effective than passive, text-heavy digital tutorials.

Qualitative interviews supplemented these findings. Participants in the ARNS group reported higher engagement due to the game’s challenge elements and the necessity of actively analyzing clues rather than passively reading text. They noted that the process of combining stickers and solving puzzles helped them retain information about machine functions more effectively. In contrast, users of the web-based system found the content convenient but criticized the excessive amount of text, which led to impatience and poor retention. The librarian tour group appreciated the direct interaction but acknowledged that oral explanations alone made it difficult to grasp detailed technical specifications of each machine.

The study concludes that game-based AR is a valuable tool for makerspace education, particularly in overcoming the limitations of web-based tutorials. The authors recommend a blended learning approach: using web resources or librarian tours for initial orientation and basic familiarity, followed by the ARNS for interactive, deep-dive learning of equipment operations. This hybrid model aims to balance convenience with engagement, ensuring users are both informed and motivated to utilize makerspace resources effectively. Future research directions include enhancing the AR system with 3D object modeling rather than static images to further improve immersion and detail retention.

citation: Chen, K.-F., Hwang, G.-J., & Chen, M.-R. A. (2024). Knowledge check‑based concept mapping in digital games: impacts on students’ learning performance and behaviors. Education Tech Research Dev, 72, 2297–2324.
target_sections: Learning_and_literacy|Service_Conditions_Addressed|Conceptual_Theme
summary_text:
This quasi-experimental study investigates the efficacy of a knowledge check-based concept map-guided gaming (KC-CMG) strategy in secondary chemistry education. The primary objective was to compare this novel approach against a conventional concept map-guided gaming (CMG) strategy, assessing impacts on learning achievement, problem-solving awareness, critical thinking tendency, germane cognitive load, and specific learning behaviors.

The study involved 48 seventh-grade students divided into an experimental group (n=24) and a control group (n=24). Both groups utilized a self-developed digital game focused on the classification of substances. The control group engaged with concept maps and answered first-tier factual questions. The experimental group followed the same structure but additionally completed second-tier conceptual questions designed to probe reasoning and correct misconceptions, constituting the "knowledge check" mechanism. The intervention spanned approximately five weeks, including pre- and post-tests, questionnaires, behavioral logging, and interviews.

Quantitative results demonstrated that the KC-CMG strategy significantly outperformed the CMG strategy across all measured variables. ANCOVA analyses revealed significant differences in learning achievement (F = 7.17, p < 0.01), problem-solving awareness (F = 6.49, p < 0.01), and critical thinking tendency (F = 5.10, p < 0.05) favoring the experimental group. Furthermore, independent samples t-tests indicated that the KC-CMG group experienced significantly higher germane cognitive load (t = 2.73, p < 0.05), suggesting more effective mental model construction.

Behavioral analysis provided insight into these outcomes. Sequential analysis of game logs showed that students in the experimental group who failed second-tier conceptual checks were significantly more likely to revisit and re-read concept maps (F2→RE behavior) compared to the control group. This iterative process allowed learners to identify gaps in their understanding and reinforce conceptual connections. Consequently, the experimental group demonstrated higher success rates in subsequent learning challenges.

Qualitative data from interviews supported these findings. Experimental group students reported that the two-tier testing forced them to reflect on *why* answers were correct or incorrect, enhancing their motivation and conceptual clarity. In contrast, control group students appreciated the engagement and efficiency of the game but did not report the same depth of reflective practice. The authors conclude that the knowledge check mechanism serves as a critical scaffold, transforming passive consumption of concept maps into active validation of understanding.

The study acknowledges several limitations. The sample size was small (N=48), limiting generalizability. The findings are specific to chemistry and may not transfer to other subjects. The intervention duration was relatively short (approximately five weeks). Additionally, the development of such integrated game systems is resource-intensive. The authors recommend creating basic templates to facilitate broader adoption by educators. Overall, the study provides empirical evidence that combining concept maps with rigorous knowledge checks in digital games can significantly enhance cognitive engagement and learning outcomes in science education.

citation: Cho et al. (2023). The use of an escape room as an immersive learning environment for building resilience to misinformation. Journal of Librarianship and Information Science, 57(2), 524–538.
target_sections: Games as Cultural Media and Heritage|Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Ground Services in Purpose, Audience, and Institutional Context|Evidence Quality and Outcome Gaps
summary_text:
This article presents an exploratory pilot study evaluating "The Euphorigen Investigation," a misinformation-themed escape room designed to enhance resilience against misinformation through immersive, collaborative gameplay and facilitated reflection. The authors argue that conventional information literacy approaches often fail to address the psychological dimensions of misinformation, such as cognitive biases and emotional triggers. To address this gap, they developed an escape room that avoids didactic skills-building in favor of experiential learning, allowing players to "fail safely" by encountering and sharing misinformation within a fictional context.

The study was conducted across five public libraries in Washington State between February and March 2021. A total of 80 participants engaged in 17 sessions, working in teams of 3–6 players. The intervention consisted of approximately 45–60 minutes of gameplay followed by a 15–20 minute post-game debrief discussion facilitated by trained librarians. Data collection methods included audio-video recordings of gameplay and debriefs, participant surveys, librarian session reports, and a librarian focus group. Qualitative data from video transcripts were analyzed using thematic analysis with paired coders in ATLAS.ti, while quantitative survey data (n=50) were used descriptively.

The findings indicate that the escape room successfully increased participants' awareness of specific misinformation techniques, particularly deepfake images and videos, which many players found novel or alarming. The game’s design intentionally elicited feelings of vulnerability by having players unwittingly share a deepfake video, thereby challenging the "third-person effect" (the belief that others are more susceptible to misinformation than oneself). Participants frequently reflected on their own cognitive biases, such as confirmation bias, during the debrief sessions. Many reported a shift in attitude toward greater caution when encountering and sharing information on social media, with some describing new strategies for verification, such as "vibe checking" or cross-referencing sources.

Librarian feedback highlighted the value of the collaborative format and the post-game debrief in fostering peer learning and civil discourse about controversial topics. However, librarians also noted logistical challenges associated with the online delivery model, including technical difficulties and recruitment issues. They suggested that an in-person version would be more appealing and recommended incorporating real-world examples into future iterations to strengthen the connection between game mechanics and daily life.

The authors conclude that immersive, collaborative games facilitated by trusted community figures like librarians offer a promising avenue for misinformation education. The study underscores the critical role of the post-game debrief in transforming subjective game experiences into transferable knowledge. Limitations include the small sample size, which precludes statistical generalization, and the potential for social desirability bias in self-reported behaviors. The authors recommend further large-scale and longitudinal studies to assess long-term behavioral impacts and to explore adaptations for diverse communities and formats.

citation: The Information Needs of Virtual Users: A Study of Second Life Libraries
target_sections: Games as Mainstream Cultural Media|Games for Learning, Health and Well Being|Libraries, Public Knowledge, and the Contemporary Information Environment|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Ground Services in Purpose, Audience, and Institutional Context|Gaps in the Literature and Future Research Agenda
summary_text:
This 2012 study by Chow et al. investigates the viability and usability of library services within Second Life, a prominent social virtual world. The research addresses the proliferation of virtual libraries and the uncertainty surrounding their utility for both traditional patrons and native virtual users. Utilizing a mixed-method design, the authors examined the Olathe Public Library in Kansas and its corresponding virtual branch, alongside a broader sample of Second Life librarians and users. The study collected data from 366 participants through interviews, focus groups, and surveys administered via online platforms and in-world kiosks.

The primary objective was to determine if information needs differ between traditional and virtual users, how virtual libraries are managed, the usability of these services, and their future trajectory. The findings reveal a stark divergence in user profiles and expectations. Traditional patrons surveyed (n=59) were predominantly interested in borrowing physical materials like books and videos. They demonstrated low technological engagement with gaming or chat interfaces and were largely unaware (84.7%) that their library maintained a Second Life presence. Interest in virtual services among this group was minimal, though a focus group indicated that exposure to the technology could increase appreciation for its potential in education and remote access.

In contrast, virtual users (n=285 general library patrons; n=15 Olathe specific) were characterized as "digital neophytes" or explorers. The majority were first-time visitors to the virtual libraries they surveyed, with 73% of Olathe virtual patrons stating they were simply exploring rather than seeking specific information. These users rarely engaged with librarians (79% never spoke to one) and did not utilize library resources during their visits. Instead, their primary goals were social interaction and entertainment. The study found that virtual libraries often fail because they attempt to replicate traditional, object-oriented information services in an environment where users seek experience-oriented interactions.

Regarding management, the study highlights significant organizational challenges. Virtual library initiatives are largely driven by individual librarians acting as volunteers, performing work during personal time due to lack of institutional support or recognition of Second Life as a professional duty. This reliance on unpaid labor creates sustainability issues and low efficiency. The authors note that "born virtual" libraries (those not tied to a physical branch) often see higher engagement than branches of traditional libraries, likely because they are more adept at fostering community rather than replicating physical services.

The implications for library science are clear: to succeed in virtual worlds, libraries must abandon the model of mirroring physical services. Instead, they should leverage the 3D environment for social programming, collaborative projects, and unique experiential learning opportunities that cannot be replicated in two-dimensional digital spaces or physical buildings. Furthermore, libraries must address internal cultural barriers by formally supporting staff involvement in virtual worlds to ensure long-term viability. The study concludes that without a shift toward community-centric services and adequate organizational backing, virtual library branches will remain underutilized and unsustainable.

citation: Clark, D., Tanner-Smith, E., & Killingsworth, S. (2014). Digital Games, Design and Learning: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis (Executive Summary). Menlo Park, CA: SRI International.
target_sections: Games for Learning, Health and Well Being|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Learning and Literacy|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services
summary_text:
This executive summary presents a systematic review and meta-analysis of 69 study samples involving 6,868 K-16 students, examining the efficacy of digital games for learning. Published in 2014 by Clark, Tanner-Smith, and Killingsworth, the study addresses the sparseness of systematic evidence regarding game-based learning by synthesizing research from 2000 to 2012. The authors employed random-effects meta-analysis with robust variance estimates to analyze two primary comparisons: media comparisons (digital games vs. non-game controls) and value-added comparisons (augmented game designs vs. standard game designs).

The overarching finding for media comparisons was a significant positive effect size of 0.33 standard deviations, indicating that digital games enhanced student learning relative to traditional non-game instructional conditions. Crucially, the value-added analysis revealed an effect size of 0.37 for augmented designs over standard ones. The authors note that these magnitudes are similar, suggesting that the design of the intervention is as critical to learning outcomes as the medium itself. This challenges debates focused solely on whether games are "better" than traditional media, shifting the focus to *how* games are designed.

The study explored several moderating variables. Regarding general study characteristics, multiple gameplay sessions were associated with significantly better learning outcomes than single sessions, though total duration did not show a consistent linear correlation with gains. Contrary to prior meta-analyses by Sitzmann (2011) and Wouters et al. (2013), this study found that supplemental non-game instruction did not significantly increase effect sizes, possibly due to differences in how "supplemental instruction" was coded or integrated. In terms of player structure, single-player games without competition yielded higher gains than both collaborative competitive games and single-player competitive games, suggesting that competitive structures may undermine self-efficacy for some learners.

Regarding game mechanics, the analysis found that both simple gamification and sophisticated mechanics were effective, though higher levels of scaffolding were consistently associated with better learning outcomes. Visual and narrative characteristics presented complex results; an aggregate measure of contextualization (including visual realism, anthropomorphism, and narrative depth) showed a small negative relationship with immediate learning gains. The authors interpret this not as a rejection of situated learning theory, but as a reflection of the challenges in designing rich contexts that do not obscure key relationships or overwhelm cognitive capacity during immediate posttests. They argue that current assessments may fail to capture the deeper, transferable understanding that contextualized games aim to foster.

The authors highlight significant limitations in the existing literature, particularly regarding research quality and commensurability. Few studies met rigorous quality standards, and there is substantial heterogeneity in how game and control conditions are implemented and described. The study calls for more detailed reporting of design features, including screenshots and theoretical bases, to facilitate better synthesis. Furthermore, they advocate for a shift away from proof-of-concept studies toward value-added research that isolates specific design elements. Finally, the authors emphasize the need for improved assessment tools capable of measuring higher-order cognitive, intrapersonal, and interpersonal skills, which are central to 21st-century learning but difficult to capture with standard immediate posttests.

citation: Clarke, R. I., Lee, J. H., Jett, J., & Sacchi, S. (2014). Exploring Relationships Among Video Games. In *Joint Conference on Digital Libraries* (pp. 1-2). IEEE.
target_sections: Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Existing Library Information Systems|Description, Discovery, and Games Advisory|Challenges to Cataloging Games|Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern
summary_text:
This poster presentation by Clarke, Lee, Jett, and Sacchi (2014) addresses the inadequacy of current bibliographic conceptual models, specifically Functional Requirements for Bibliographic Records (FRBR), in describing the complex relationships inherent to video games. The authors argue that as video games gain cultural and scholarly significance, the challenges of describing them—particularly regarding digital distribution and non-physical components—require a user-centered approach to metadata. The primary objective was to explore these relationship types to inform the development of a robust metadata schema for the Seattle Interactive Media Museum (SIMM) and broader digital library contexts.

The methodology combined qualitative consultation with video game experts and enthusiasts alongside a direct examination of specific game sets and their existing metadata structures. The sample population was selected to ensure diversity in genre, platform, creation date, and distribution method. Key case studies included the *Shin Megami Tensei: Persona*, *Disgaea*, *Final Fantasy*, *Bejeweled*, and *Civilization V* series. These "seed games" were chosen specifically because they exhibit complicated relational structures with other titles, editions, and additional content.

The analysis revealed four distinct categories of relationships that current standards fail to capture effectively. First, **edition relationships** are problematic because traditional bibliographic definitions rely on print-centric concepts (e.g., copies from the same typographic image). In video games, "editions" may differ significantly in source code, visual presentation, and gameplay mechanics across platforms (arcade, console, digital network), leading users to perceive them as distinct entities rather than mere variations. Second, **series relationships** are characterized by sprawling, aggregate structures. The *Persona* series, for instance, functions as a sub-series of *Shin Megami Tensei*, containing entries with unrelated narratives, shared settings, and numerous spin-offs, anime adaptations, and merchandise. This complexity extends to other major franchises like *Final Fantasy* and *Civilization*.

Third, the study highlighted **relationships between games and additional content**. The rise of digital distribution has introduced downloadable content (DLC), expansions, and patches that vary in impact. Some additions, such as the *Gods & Kings* expansion for *Civilization V*, introduce substantial new gameplay features, while others merely fix security issues or update software. Current metadata lacks the granularity to distinguish between these types of additions, which is critical for fulfilling user information needs. Fourth, the authors identified **inspirational and lineage relationships**. Many games are explicitly "based on" or "inspired by" predecessors, such as *Candy Crush Saga* deriving from *Bejeweled*, which itself was inspired by the 1984 game *Shariki*. Tracing these genealogical links is valuable for scholars and designers but remains unrepresented in standard cataloging.

The implications of this work are significant for library science and digital preservation. The authors conclude that FRBR’s Group I entities (work, expression, manifestation, item) are insufficient for alternative media like video games. To improve user access and description, future work must clearly define these complex relationships and integrate them into a new conceptual model and metadata schema. This research serves as a preliminary step toward creating a standardized, user-centered framework for describing interactive media in digital libraries.

citation: Coonan, C. (2019). ‘The best way to learn is just to play’. *Journal of Information Literacy*, 13(1), 1–2. http://dx.doi.org/10.11645/13.1.2648
target_sections: Games as Mainstream Cultural Media|Games for Learning, Health and Well Being|Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Community, Belonging, Wellness, and the Public Commons|Game Modalities in Library Contexts
summary_text:
This editorial by Coonan (2019) introduces an issue of the *Journal of Information Literacy* dedicated to expanding the conceptual boundaries of information literacy (IL) beyond traditional academic settings. The overarching objective is to demonstrate how IL manifests in diverse contexts, including public libraries, school environments, higher education professional development, and informal digital spaces. The text serves as a curated guide to the issue’s contents, emphasizing a shift toward understanding IL as a practice embedded in everyday life rather than solely a formal educational outcome.

The editorial categorizes the issue’s contributions into several key areas. First, it addresses professional learning and development for library staff, citing reports from the LILAC and ALDinHE conferences. These sections highlight themes such as professional identity, imposter syndrome, and the synergy between learning development and IL. Second, the text outlines pedagogical innovations in higher education. Notable examples include a flipped workshop designed for creative arts students to understand the attribution of non-textual artifacts, and an evaluation of asynchronous online tutorials using learning diaries. The latter method is highlighted for its ability to capture individual reflections and avoid the consensus bias often found in focus groups.

Third, the editorial discusses empirical research on academic perceptions of IL, specifically within nursing and business disciplines at Anglia Ruskin University. This work connects disciplinary practices to IL, framing information evaluation as a means of cultivating social capital for future professional workplaces. Fourth, it examines public library services through the lens of the 'Philosophy for Children' (P4C) approach. This section argues for repositioning libraries as sites of civic discourse and inquiry-based learning, where facilitators help children develop independent critical thinking strategies rather than simply providing information.

Most relevant to games-based library services is the editorial’s discussion of informal learning in videogaming. Coonan highlights a paper by Scolari and Contreras-Espinosa that investigates transmedia literacy among game players. The text notes that this research identifies significant overlaps between gaming practices and IL competencies, including problem-solving, identity performance, navigating complex media environments, and networking. The editorial emphasizes the authors' conclusion that skills often viewed as merely technical or contextual within gaming possess broader social and cognitive applications. This section underscores a critical philosophical point: learning is not merely preparation for living but is an active part of living itself.

The implications drawn in this editorial suggest that library professionals should recognize and integrate these informal literacies into their practice. By acknowledging the IL skills developed through gaming and other everyday interactions, libraries can better support users across all contexts. The text concludes by encouraging practitioners to adopt the innovative ideas presented in the issue, reinforcing the journal’s mission to develop deep, critical understandings of IL that are applicable both within formal institutions and in the broader social sphere. The editorial thus frames games-based learning not as a peripheral activity, but as a significant domain where information literacy is actively constructed and performed.

citation: Copeland, T., Henderson, B., Mayer, B., & Nicholson, S. (2013). Three Different Paths for Tabletop Gaming in School Libraries. Library Trends, 61(4), 825-835.
target_sections: Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Libraries as Playful Spaces and Service Environments|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy|Community, Belonging, Wellness, and the Public Commons
summary_text:
This article, titled "Three Different Paths for Tabletop Gaming in School Libraries," presents a qualitative examination of how three school library professionals have implemented tabletop gaming programs in diverse educational settings. The authors—Teresa Copeland, Brenda Henderson, and Brian Nicholson (with contributions from Scott Nicholson)—argue that nondigital games, including board, card, and role-playing games, offer unique advantages over digital counterparts, such as lower cost, portability, and the ability for players to manually manipulate game mechanics, thereby enhancing understanding. The article is structured around three case studies that illustrate distinct approaches: curriculum integration, recreational club formation, and systemic support for game-based learning.

The first case study, presented by Teresa Copeland from Tesseract School in Arizona, focuses on integrating games into the academic curriculum across PK-12 grades. Copeland describes using card games like *Apples to Apples* for vocabulary building and role-playing games (RPGs) for deep historical engagement. A primary example is "Trial of Socrates," an adaptation of a college-level RPG where students assume roles in ancient Athens, requiring them to study primary sources like Plato’s works to argue their character’s case. Copeland reports that this method generated unprecedented enthusiasm and critical thinking, with students investing significant effort into research and speechwriting. The narrative highlights the flexibility of live-action RPGs, allowing facilitators to adjust scenarios on the fly to encourage creativity. Additionally, simpler games are used for foundational skills, such as alphabetical order and online safety, demonstrating that gaming can be tailored to various developmental stages.

The second case study, by Brenda Henderson from Trinity High School in the UK, details the establishment of "Unplugged," an after-school board game club. This program targets students aged 13–18, many of whom do not participate in traditional sports or other extracurriculars. Henderson notes that the club provides a safe, social environment where students develop soft skills such as turn-taking, conflict resolution, and handling winning and losing gracefully. An informal survey of 13 regular attendees revealed that most did not participate in other clubs, suggesting the library fills a gap in social engagement for these students. The club also fosters peer-to-peer learning, with older students teaching rules to younger ones, thereby reinforcing their own understanding. Henderson emphasizes the importance of the librarian’s role in facilitating these interactions and maintaining game quality, noting that the program has helped expand social circles for isolated students.

The third case study, from Brian Mayer at the Genesee Valley Educational Partnership in New York, describes a regional initiative supporting 22 school districts with a collection of over 200 games aligned with state and national learning standards. Mayer outlines a selection process based on engagement, curricular connection, time efficiency, and return on investment. He reports that games serve as a "nonthreatening environment" for students to explore complex ideas, particularly benefiting those who struggle with traditional instruction. A key finding is the "20-minute drop point," where students transition from viewing the activity as a chore to engaging with it as a challenge, provided the game is authentically fun rather than overtly didactic. Mayer also highlights the expansion into game design workshops, where students create their own games to demonstrate mastery of curricular concepts. This approach leverages diverse learning styles within groups, promoting collaboration and practical application of skills.

Collectively, these cases suggest that tabletop gaming in school libraries can effectively bridge education and recreation. The authors conclude that such programs enhance student engagement, support social development, and provide alternative pathways for learning, particularly for students who may be disengaged from conventional academic or extracurricular structures. The article underscores the librarian’s critical role in curating collections, facilitating play, and integrating games into broader educational goals.

citation: Cormier, J., & Fail Faster. (2024). Design Your Destiny.
target_sections: Service Purposes and Library Functions|Game Design Workshops, Jams, and Participatory Creation|Libraries as Playful Spaces and Service Environments|Outreach, Participation, and New Audiences|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Fandom Festivals and Pop-Culture Events
summary_text:
The provided source text functions as a promotional and informational document regarding "Design Your Destiny: Running Out of Time," an interactive educational product created by Jay Cormier and Fail Faster. The text is directed primarily at teachers and librarians, positioning the product as a resource for integrating game design principles into library services and classroom settings. The core premise of the product is described as an interactive time-travel story that encapsulates multiple games and an activity book. Within this narrative framework, participants encounter games that are intentionally "broken" or flawed. The text asserts that the accompanying book provides insights and guidance on how to repair these mechanical issues, requiring users to implement their own solutions and playtest their revised versions to assist the main characters in the story. This structure suggests a pedagogical approach focused on iterative design, problem-solving, and critical analysis of game mechanics.

The document does not present empirical data, research findings, or statistical analysis. Instead, it serves as a hub for resources related to the product and its creator. It references a Video on Demand (VOD) session hosted on Twitch, indicating that the content was likely presented in a webinar format aimed at library professionals. The text explicitly lists resources for libraries to promote the title, including posters and image files. Furthermore, it connects "Design Your Destiny" to Jay Cormier’s broader educational initiatives, specifically the "Fail Faster" online game design course, suggesting that this product is part of a larger ecosystem of game development education.

A significant portion of the text is dedicated to curating related materials and tools, which may serve as supplementary resources for librarians implementing games-based services. These include references to Cormier’s other works (e.g., "MIND MGMT," "Harrow County"), articles on setting up tabletop role-playing games (TTRPGs) in libraries, and guides on game jams. The text also lists specific game design software such as Twine, RPG Maker, Unity, and Godot, alongside tutorials and reference guides. This curation implies a recommendation for librarians to familiarize themselves with these tools to support patrons interested in game creation.

Additionally, the source text emphasizes community standards and safety within gaming environments. It includes links to TTRPG safety toolkits, such as "Script Change" and "Lines and Veils," which are protocols used to ensure participant comfort and consent during role-playing activities. The inclusion of a Twitch chat policy for "GameRT" (likely the Game Round Table or similar library gaming initiative) underscores the importance of respectful interaction and moderation in digital and physical gaming spaces.
