The provided source text does not constitute a single academic article, research study, or empirical report suitable for standard scientific summarization. Instead, it appears to be a raw export or a structured list of bibliographic resources related to games-based library services (GBLS). The content is organized into four primary categories: Articles, Books, Lists & Libguides, and Video Resources. Within the "Articles" section, there is a further subdivision for "Analog Games" and "TTRPG & Kids," followed by a list of titles, authors, publication dates, and sometimes sources (e.g., PBS, The Telegraph, Dicebreaker).

The listed articles cover topics such as the benefits of tabletop role-playing games (TTRPGs) during the COVID-19 pandemic, the development of social-emotional skills through gaming, inclusivity in gaming communities, and the use of RPGs for mental health support. Specific titles include "5 Science-Backed Benefits of Playing Tabletop RPGs During (and After) COVID-19" by The Pop Mythologist (May 2020), "Games Build Social-Emotional Skills" by Rachel Pollari (Dec 2022), and a white paper by foundry10 evaluating Game to Grow's TTRPG programming for youth (Feb 2023). Other entries discuss the cultural rise of Dungeons & Dragons, the therapeutic potential of fictional worlds, and practical tips for adjusting RPG mechanics for children.

The "Books" section lists two titles: "Playing Games in the School Library: Developing Game-Based Lessons and Using Gamification Concepts" and "Libraries Got Game: Aligned Learning through Modern Board Games." These titles suggest a focus on pedagogical integration and curriculum alignment within library settings.

The "Lists & Libguides" section references "Meaningful Play (children and teens): General Resources" and "Gaming in Libraries: An Annotated Bibliography," indicating that the source text itself may be part of or derived from a larger annotated bibliography or libguide designed to assist librarians in navigating the landscape of gaming resources.

Finally, the "Video Resources" category is listed but contains no specific entries in the provided text.

Because the source text is a collection of references rather than a standalone study, it lacks an objective research question, methodology, sample population, or empirical findings. It does not present data on participant outcomes, statistical analyses, or qualitative interviews. Consequently, no claims regarding efficacy, causality, or correlation can be extracted from this text itself. The text serves only as a pointer to other works that may contain such information. For the purposes of a Games-Based Library Services literature review, this entry represents a curated resource list rather than primary evidence. It highlights the existence of literature concerning social-emotional learning, mental health benefits, and practical implementation strategies for TTRPGs and board games in library contexts, but it does not provide the substantive content required to evaluate those claims. Any analysis of the benefits or methods described in the cited titles would require accessing the original sources listed, which are not included in this text. Therefore, the summary is limited to describing the structure and scope of the provided bibliographic list.

citation: Giles, K. (2015). No Budget, No Experience, No Problem: Creating a Library Orientation Game for Freshman Engineering Majors. *The Journal of Academic Librarianship*, 41(1), 86-93. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.acalib.2014.12.005
target_sections: Service Purposes and Library Functions|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy|Community, Belonging, Wellness, and the Public Commons|Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services
summary_text:
This article details the creation and evaluation of "Mystery at the Library," an Alternate Reality Game (ARG) style orientation activity designed for freshman engineering students at James Madison University (JMU). The primary objective was to familiarize students with the Rose Library’s physical space, resources, and services in a manner that was engaging and cost-effective, addressing challenges associated with traditional library tours and scavenger hunts. The author, Kelly Giles, notes that engineering freshmen often lack awareness of discipline-specific resources housed in the science library, despite receiving general information literacy instruction.

The methodology involved developing a narrative-driven game where students solved the mystery of a stolen fictional notebook belonging to Nikola Tesla. The game required players to search the library building and online databases for clues, thereby practicing essential skills such as using the online catalog, locating books by call number, accessing reserve items, finding reference materials, and utilizing RefWorks for citation management. The development process was iterative; initial pilot versions included complex narrative elements like a detective character via email, which proved confusing and time-consuming. Feedback from library staff and students led to simplifications, removing non-instructional puzzles and streamlining the narrative to focus on learning objectives. The final version used minimal materials—primarily photocopies in red folders—costing less than a dollar per participant.

The study employed a quasi-experimental design during the fall of 2012. Two sections of a required engineering course (N=44) participated in the game, while a third section (N=24) served as a control group. An online survey was administered to all groups after the experimental group completed the activity but prior to any formal library instruction. The survey measured self-reported familiarity with six specific library tasks using a 5-point Likert scale and assessed student perceptions of the game’s enjoyment and utility.

Key findings revealed that the experimental group reported higher familiarity with all listed library tasks compared to the control group. The most significant difference was observed in familiarity with RefWorks, a tool many students had not previously encountered. Control group responses indicated a strong preference for hands-on learning styles, supporting the rationale for an experiential game-based approach. Among the experimental group, 88% agreed or strongly agreed that the activity was fun, and 74% found it helpful. Additionally, 79% recommended the activity for future classes. Qualitative comments highlighted improved understanding of library layout, catalog usage, and Library of Congress call numbers.

The author acknowledges several limitations. Primarily, the study relied on self-assessment of familiarity rather than objective measures of learning or skill retention. Consequently, the results do not definitively prove educational efficacy but suggest increased confidence and awareness. The sample size was limited to one specific cohort of engineering students at a single institution. Despite these constraints, the article demonstrates that effective library instruction games can be developed without significant budgets or technical expertise. The narrative structure provided a scaffold for subsequent in-class instruction, allowing librarians to connect game experiences to broader information literacy concepts such as source evaluation and ethical use of information. The author concludes that while the specific theme was tailored to engineering students, the framework is adaptable for other disciplines by modifying the thematic content and resource targets.

citation: Giles, K., Shuyler, K. S., Evans, A., & Reed, J. (2019). Creating a library orientation card game to reach new transfer students. *Public Services Quarterly*, 15(3-4), 187–201. https://doi.org/10.1080/15228959.2018.1488643
target_sections: Service Purposes and Library Functions|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy|Community, Belonging, Wellness, and the Public Commons|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Ground Services in Purpose, Audience, and Institutional Context
summary_text:
This article details the creation and evaluation of a library orientation card game designed specifically for new transfer students at James Madison University (JMU). The authors, Kelly Giles, Kristen Shuyler, Andrew Evans, and Jon Reed, sought to address the unique challenges faced by transfer students, including social integration difficulties and gaps in information literacy instruction. Traditional lecture-based orientations were deemed insufficient due to logistical constraints, such as short session durations (approximately 30 minutes) and limited staff availability. Furthermore, literature suggests that transfer students benefit significantly from activities that foster a sense of community, which correlates with academic success.

The methodology involved adapting an existing *Apples to Apples*-style card game previously developed for student employee training. The original game required moderators to explain correct answers, which was impractical for the large, unmoderated groups expected at orientation. To resolve this, the development team revised the cards to focus on library policies, services, and resources relevant to patrons rather than staff. Crucially, they added the correct answer to the back of each question card, allowing players to self-correct after judging rounds without constant librarian intervention. The final deck consisted of 27 question cards and 144 answer cards, featuring a mix of factual library information and humorous pop culture references.

The game was first implemented in 2015 with approximately 100 transfer students. Facilitators observed that the activity successfully prompted conversation and laughter, serving as an effective icebreaker. Students were seen exchanging contact information, indicating successful social bonding. In 2016, a formal assessment was conducted using an IRB-approved survey distributed to 99 attendees. Eighty-five completed surveys were analyzed. The quantitative results were highly positive: 93% of respondents found the game enjoyable, 92% reported it helped them learn about the library, 86% felt more comfortable in the library environment, and 89% recommended the activity for future orientations. Qualitative feedback described the game as "awesome" and superior to traditional pamphlets, though some students requested more humor or additional interactive elements like scavenger hunts.

The authors argue that the *Apples to Apples* format is particularly suitable for this context because it does not require players to know factual answers to win; rather, winning depends on subjective judgment of humorous or creative responses. This design reduces "library anxiety" and allows students with varying levels of prior knowledge to participate equally. The study highlights that while the game may not deeply teach information literacy skills due to time constraints, it effectively introduces library resources in a low-stakes environment and facilitates peer connections.

The implications for games-based library services are significant. The project demonstrates that tabletop card games can be cost-effective, scalable, and adaptable tools for outreach. The authors note that the game has since been used with other populations, including student athletes and new faculty, suggesting broad applicability. They propose future developments such as subject-specific expansion packs or a version containing only humorous answers to further reduce frustration when factual cards are not selected. However, they acknowledge the need for further assessment to measure the long-term impact of the game on information literacy outcomes and social integration. The study serves as a practical case study for librarians seeking to engage transfer students through playful, interactive design.

summary_text:
The provided source text is a structured summary and outline for a live-streamed gaming session titled "Summer Break!" hosted by @gnomedic as part of the GameRT initiative. Consequently, it lacks traditional research components such as hypotheses, data collection methods, or statistical analysis. Instead, it documents the procedural flow of a Tabletop Role-Playing Game (TTRPG) session designed for a library audience.

The primary purpose of the text is to outline the three-part structure of the broadcast: an introduction to the game rules, an actual playthrough, and a post-game discussion. The game featured, *Summer Break!*, is identified as a micro-RPG found in *The Ultimate Micro-RPG Book* edited by James D’amato. The stream begins with a technical check and an introduction to the hosts, Russell and Danielle, who guide the audience through character creation, fort creation, and danger creation mechanics. This segment serves to educate viewers on the specific rules of the micro-RPG format, which is characterized by its brevity and simplicity compared to traditional TTRPGs.

The second phase involves the actual gameplay, described in the text as "Fun Stuff." While no specific narrative outcomes are detailed, this section represents the practical application of the game mechanics in a live setting. The third and most significant section for library service implications is the discussion phase. Here, the hosts address several key themes relevant to Games-Based Library Services (GBLS). First, they discuss "Figuring out Rules & Playtesting," indicating an interest in the iterative nature of game design and adaptation. Second, they explore "Playing at different life stages" and "What childhood means to different people." This suggests that the session aimed to leverage the nostalgic themes of *Summer Break!* to facilitate intergenerational or reflective dialogue among adult participants, a common goal in community library programming.

A critical component of the discussion is "Programing Potential," which directly addresses the utility of such games in library settings. The text implies that micro-RPGs offer accessible entry points for patrons due to their low barrier to entry and short duration. Furthermore, the session explicitly incorporates "Safety Tools Considerations." The source text lists several specific resources, including "Script Change" by Beau Jágr Sheldon, "The Door is Open" by Eirik Fatland, and the "TTRPG Safety Toolkit" collected by Kienna Shaw and Lauren Bryant-Monk. It also reiterates the Twitch policy emphasizing kindness, respect, and zero tolerance for hate speech, reinforcing the safety framework mentioned earlier. It highlights the intersection of nostalgia, game mechanics, and community engagement in library services.

citation: Goddard, J. (2020). Public Libraries Respond to the COVID-19 Pandemic, Creating a New Service Model. Information Technology and Libraries, 39(4), 1–4. https://doi.org/10.6017/ital.v39i4.12847
target_sections: Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Community, Belonging, Wellness, and the Public Commons|Outreach, Participation, and New Audiences|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Ground Services in Purpose, Audience, and Institutional Context|Institutional Capacity and Sustainable Service Models
summary_text:
Jon Goddard’s 2020 editorial, "Public Libraries Respond to the COVID-19 Pandemic, Creating a New Service Model," examines the rapid transformation of public library services during the early stages of the pandemic. The article posits that librarians successfully rewrote service protocols to maintain community engagement and educational support through virtual means, thereby demonstrating the enduring value of libraries as technology-driven hubs. The piece serves as both a report on these adaptations and an advocacy tool for preserving the hybrid service model post-pandemic.

The methodology relies on aggregated data from library vendors, specific case studies of library systems, and industry reports rather than original empirical research. Goddard highlights significant surges in electronic resource usage. Data from Overdrive, utilized by nearly 90% of U.S. public libraries, indicates that daily ebook checkouts remained consistently 52% higher than pre-COVID averages, with new user registrations doubling or tripling previous highs. In Suffolk County, New York, new patron registrations to Overdrive increased by 72%. The shift was particularly pronounced among younger demographics; youth and juvenile ebook readership saw an 87% increase. Educational support services also expanded, with Brainfuse online tutoring usage in Suffolk County rising nearly 50%, and Gale’s virtual preschool program, Miss Humblebee's Academy, experiencing a 100% increase in user sessions. Adult learners similarly engaged with digital resources, evidenced by a 50% increase in enrollments for Gale Courses between March and July compared to the previous year.

Community engagement transitioned entirely to virtual platforms. Libraries utilized tools such as Zoom, Discord, Facebook Live, and YouTube to deliver programming. The San José Public Library (SJPL), which had no prior virtual programming, launched over 2,000 virtual events by October 2020, including storytimes and reading initiatives. Similarly, the Oceanside Library in New York scaled its offerings from zero to eight daily programs within a week of closure, eventually opening participation to out-of-state residents due to high demand. Reference services also shifted online; at SJPL, chat reference conversations increased by nearly 400%, rising from approximately 40 to 160 sessions per day, with support provided in multiple languages including Spanish, Vietnamese, and Chinese.

The article addresses the digital divide, noting that approximately 25% of households lack broadband internet. Libraries responded by maintaining outdoor Wi-Fi signals, lending hotspots, and partnering with educational institutions. SJPL collaborated with city departments to distribute approximately 16,000 high-speed hotspots to students and the public. Additionally, libraries implemented contactless curbside pickup services, utilizing tools like LibCal for appointment scheduling and LibAnswers for SMS notifications upon arrival.

Goddard concludes that these adaptations should not be temporary measures but rather a permanent "new normal." He argues that libraries must continue to offer hybrid services, combining physical spaces with robust virtual platforms for reference, programming, and material distribution. The editorial underscores the importance of federal advocacy, noting the success in securing $50 million through the CARES Act and calling for further support via the Library Stabilization Fund Act. Ultimately, the article asserts that the pandemic revealed libraries' critical role in providing equitable access to information and technology, a function that must be sustained through continued investment and strategic planning.

citation: Public Library Association. 2023 Public Library Technology Survey: Summary Report. Chicago: Public Library Association, 2024.
target_sections: Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy|Community, Belonging, Wellness, and the Public Commons|Outreach, Participation, and New Audiences|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services
summary_text:
The *2023 Public Library Technology Survey: Summary Report*, authored by Sara S. Goek and colleagues for the Public Library Association (PLA), presents a comprehensive empirical analysis of technology integration within U.S. public libraries. The primary objective is to assess the current landscape of library technology resources, digital literacy services, infrastructure capabilities, and staff support structures, while tracking evolution from the 2020 baseline amidst post-pandemic recovery and federal funding initiatives.

The methodology relies on a nationwide survey administered in fall 2023 via the Benchmark platform. The PLA invited all U.S. public libraries at the administrative entity level to participate. A total of 1,511 libraries completed the survey, yielding a 16.4% response rate. To ensure national representativeness, the results were weighted in post-processing to account for differences between respondents and the universe of all U.S. public libraries. The data is stratified by locale type: city (6% of entities), suburban (25%), and town/rural (69%).

Key findings indicate a significant expansion in technology access and services since 2020. Notably, hotspot lending has increased substantially, with 46.9% of libraries now offering internet hotspots for checkout, compared to 32.6% in 2020. Digital content provision is nearly universal, with 95% of libraries offering e-books and/or e-audiobooks. Furthermore, 95.3% of libraries provide some form of digital literacy training, ranging from basic computer skills to coding and maker technology. However, significant disparities exist by locale. City libraries are far more likely to possess advanced on-site technologies, such as maker production equipment (74.8% in cities vs. 33.5% in town/rural) and digital media production tools (58.9% in cities vs. 18% in town/rural). Similarly, city libraries are more likely to offer streaming media (92.9%) and language learning resources (85.9%) compared to their town/rural counterparts (46.7% and 40.2%, respectively).

Infrastructure analysis reveals persistent gaps in broadband connectivity. While 74.8% of libraries have fiber optic connections, speed disparities remain pronounced. City libraries subscribe to average download speeds over three times higher than town/rural libraries. Critically, 28.4% of all libraries subscribe to internet connections that fall below the Federal Communications Commission’s (FCC) revised broadband standard of 100 Mbps download and 20 Mbps upload; this figure rises to 35.4% for town/rural libraries. Barriers to improving connectivity include contractual lock-ins (40.1%), lack of funding (30.5%), and unavailability of faster speeds in the service area (30.4%).

Staffing and budget constraints emerge as critical limitations. Only 20.7% of libraries employ full-time IT staff, with many relying on consortia or contracted support. Open-ended responses identified limited staff capacity (54%) and insufficient funding (39%) as the primary challenges to providing digital literacy assistance. Additionally, 86% of libraries cited subscription costs as the top factor influencing their ability to offer digital content. Despite these constraints, 60.8% of libraries maintain dedicated budget lines for public access technology, with grants (61.9%) and local government funds (48.9%) being the primary sources.

The report implies that public libraries serve as essential hubs for digital equity, particularly for underserved populations lacking home broadband or digital skills. The authors emphasize the importance of federal funding mechanisms like E-rate and the Digital Equity Act in sustaining these services. They also highlight the emerging need for staff training in new technologies, such as artificial intelligence, noting that 61% of respondents planning future training focus on AI. The data underscores that while libraries have successfully expanded technological reach, systemic inequities in infrastructure and resources between urban and rural settings require continued advocacy and targeted investment.
