Schema testing was conducted by having students create metadata records for 65 sample games, including console titles, digital apps, and flash-based games. Each game was cataloged twice by different individuals to assess inter-cataloger consistency and identify ambiguities in the schema.

User evaluation comprised two streams. First, 32 semi-structured interviews were conducted with a diverse range of stakeholders, including casual and avid gamers, parents, collectors, industry professionals, curators/librarians, and scholars. Participants were recruited via snowball sampling from various communities and professional networks. Second, an online survey was administered to a broader population of gamers. Of 2,163 respondents, 1,257 completed the questionnaire, which assessed the perceived usefulness of specific metadata elements for both currently played games and new game discovery.

Key findings from the survey indicated that <price> and <platform> were the most highly rated elements, with over 80% of respondents finding them useful. Subject metadata, such as <genre>, <style>, <plot/narrative>, and <theme>, was also valued, as was relational data like <series> and <franchise>. Visual metadata, particularly <gameplay videos>, was deemed highly informative for assessing gameplay mechanics and visual appeal, whereas static images like screenshots were viewed with skepticism regarding their veracity.

Schema testing revealed significant challenges in cataloging consistency. Discrepancies frequently arose regarding the conceptual level of description (work vs. manifestation), particularly for games available across multiple platforms. Subjective elements requiring personal play experience, such as <mood> and <theme>, led to inconsistent descriptions when catalogers relied on secondary sources. Consequently, the researchers developed a conceptual model to clarify entity distinctions and established preferred source lists for subjective data.

Several elements were removed or modified from the recommended schema due to complexity or low utility. <Characters>, <controls>, <achievements>, and <credits> were excluded because they introduced excessive workload, inconsistency, or ambiguity without proportional benefit to user discovery. Conversely, new elements were added to address emerging needs in digital distribution. A <DRM> (Digital Rights Management) element was included to capture restrictions relevant to both players and scholars. The <box art/cover> element was renamed <representative art> to accommodate digitally distributed games lacking physical packaging. Additionally, <online capabilities> was split into <networked features> and <connectivity> to distinguish between gameplay interactions and technical requirements.

The study concludes that the revised metadata schema (Version 2.0) better reflects user information needs and the unique characteristics of video games. The authors emphasize the necessity of media-specific standards to support the preservation, organization, and access of video game collections in cultural heritage institutions. The work provides a foundational framework for future developments in video game metadata, highlighting the importance of balancing descriptive richness with cataloging efficiency.

citation: Lee, J. H., Jett, J., & Perti, A. (2015). The Problem of “Additional Content” in Video Games. In Proceedings of the 15th ACM/IEEE Joint Conference on Digital Libraries (JCDL '15), 237–240.
target_sections: Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Description, Discovery, and Games Advisory|Cultural Stewardship and Preservation|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern
summary_text:
This article addresses the growing complexity of managing video game collections in cultural heritage institutions, specifically focusing on the challenges posed by "additional content" such as downloadable content (DLC) and modifications (mods). The authors define additional content as material that expands or supplements a game beyond its initial point of purchase, explicitly excluding ordinary software patches and bug fixes. They distinguish between mods, typically developed by fan communities, and DLC, produced by corporate entities, noting that both require the original game to function but differ in origin and often in cost.

The authors identify significant philosophical challenges related to the bibliographic concept of a "work." Traditional definitions view a work as a distinct intellectual creation, but additional content can substantially alter gameplay mechanics and narrative structures. This raises questions about whether a game with extensive mods or DLC remains the same work or constitutes a new one. The optional nature of this content further complicates matters, as different players may experience vastly different versions of the same title. The authors note that there are no established criteria for determining when a modification becomes a distinct work, suggesting that community usage and popularity might serve as informal benchmarks.

Practically, the article outlines hurdles in acquisition, characterization, description, access, and preservation. Acquisition is complicated by the ephemeral nature of digital distribution platforms, the dependency on original game copies, and budget constraints given the volume of available content. Description is hindered by the lack of standardized controlled vocabulary within the gaming industry and the difficulty of attributing creation to diverse sources, ranging from large corporations to individual fans. Furthermore, describing system requirements, compatibility, and inter-mod dependencies is critical for usability but currently lacks standardization. Access and preservation face technical barriers due to varied distribution methods, platform-specific architectures, copy protection measures on physical media, and encryption in digital files that obscure the underlying code.

To address these issues, the authors propose a new conceptual model that treats additional content as a separate entity from the base game. This model assigns specific attributes to each piece of additional content, including title, type (mod, DLC, expansion), and version/configuration requirements. By doing so, it facilitates clearer bibliographic control and allows for the creation of formal metadata records. The authors illustrate this with examples from *Civilization V*, showing how the model can capture detailed information about expansions and mods, including their dependencies on other content.

The authors acknowledge that this approach diverges from the Functional Requirements for Bibliographic Records (FRBR) framework, arguing that FRBR is poorly suited for non-text media and objects with multiple versions, such as serials. They posit that video games are increasingly analogous to serials due to their continuous evolution. A significant limitation of the proposed model is that it does not resolve the philosophical question of when a mod becomes a new work; this remains a matter for community best practices. Additionally, the model introduces the challenge of "perpetual cataloging," as the constant creation of new content requires ongoing maintenance of bibliographic records. The authors suggest future research should focus on refining the model through practical application and exploring linked data ontologies like BIBFRAME or schema.org as alternative frameworks for representing video game collections.

citation: Lee, J. H., Clarke, R. I., & Rossi, S. (2016). A qualitative investigation of users’ discovery, access, and organization of video games as information objects. Journal of Information Science, 42(6), 833–850.
target_sections: Conceptual Terrain: Games, Play, and Library Service|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Description, Discovery, and Games Advisory|Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services
summary_text:
This qualitative study by Lee, Clarke, and Rossi (2016) addresses a gap in library and information science literature regarding how users discover, access, and organize video games as information objects. While previous research has focused on player behavior within games or general information seeking, little empirical data existed on the metadata needs of diverse stakeholders outside of gameplay contexts. The authors conducted 56 semi-structured interviews with a stratified sample of participants over 18 years old, including gamers, parents, collectors, industry professionals, librarians, educators, and scholars. Recruitment utilized snowball sampling through various digital and physical venues. Data were analyzed using an iterative coding process akin to grounded theory, resulting in a codebook covering gaming experience, collection behavior, information needs, metadata types, appeal factors, and user characteristics.

The study’s primary finding is that user roles are not static; individuals often embody multiple personas (e.g., a gamer who is also a parent or scholar), leading to fluid information needs depending on the context. Consequently, the authors argue against rigid user profiling in information systems. Instead, systems should accommodate different "user modes," such as seeking information for oneself versus acting as a proxy for others. Proxy users, including parents and librarians, exhibited distinct needs from direct players. They prioritized extrinsic information such as content appropriateness (violence, profanity), educational value, technical compatibility (hardware requirements, pause functionality), and cost. Direct players, conversely, sought detailed content information regarding mechanics, narrative, and visual style.

Regarding metadata, genre was identified as the most frequently discussed element but also a source of confusion, often conflated with theme or subject. Participants expressed difficulty with broad genre labels and desired more nuanced subject metadata, such as themes (e.g., cyberpunk, female protagonists) and tropes. Visual metadata, including screenshots and gameplay videos, was deemed critical for decision-making, often providing more accurate insights than textual descriptions. Furthermore, relationship-based metadata—such as series connections, influences, and modifications—was highly valued by collectors, scholars, and industry professionals for tracing the history and impact of games.

The authors also explored the "four Rs": reviews, ratings, rankings, and recommendations. Participants triangulated these sources to make purchase decisions, with proxy users preferring professional reviews (e.g., Common Sense Media) and direct players valuing user-generated content. However, concerns regarding bias and reliability in both professional and user reviews were noted. Additionally, the study identified specific "appeal factors" that drive game selection, such as fellowship, narrative, challenge, visual stimulation, and fantasy. The authors suggest that recommendation systems should leverage these appeal factors rather than relying solely on genre matching.

Finally, the study highlights challenges in organizing digital game collections, which often become cluttered due to impulse buys and sales. Participants expressed a need for flexible organization tools that allow sorting by status (e.g., played, to-play) or other custom criteria. The authors conclude that effective video game information systems must be robust enough to handle complex metadata relationships, support diverse user roles and modes, and integrate visual and appeal-based discovery features to meet the varied needs of all stakeholders.

citation: Lee, Jin Ha, Rachel Ivy Clarke, Hyerim Cho, and Travis Windleharth. "Understanding Appeals of Video Games for Readers’ Advisory and Recommendation." *Reference & User Services Quarterly* 57, no. 2 (Winter 2017): 127–139.
target_sections: Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern|Discovery and Advisory|Learning and Literacy|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access
summary_text:
This article addresses the gap in library readers' advisory (RA) services regarding video games, which are increasingly present in collections but rarely recommended due to a lack of standardized frameworks for understanding user preferences. Building on established RA concepts such as "appeals" and "doorways," the authors define appeals as elements of media that evoke emotional and intellectual experiences. The primary objective is to empirically identify these appeals from user data and determine their relationship with existing video game genres, thereby creating a complementary access point for recommendations that transcends traditional genre classifications.

The methodology involved a large-scale survey distributed through various online and physical channels, including gaming forums and museum exhibitions. Of the 2,163 participants, 1,257 completed the survey. The data collection focused on two areas: an open-ended question asking users to describe their favorite games and the reasons for their appeal, and quantitative questions linking preferred genres to specific appeal categories. The open-ended responses were analyzed using a consensus coding model by two independent coders, while descriptive statistics were used for the genre-appeal correlations.

The study identified sixteen distinct appeals: Narrative, Challenge, Sensation, Fellowship, Nostalgia, Fantasy, Exploration, Mood, Depth, Creativity/Innovation, Expression, Accomplishment, Competition, Submission, Mastery, and Learning. Narrative emerged as the most dominant appeal, cited by 628 respondents, emphasizing the importance of story, character relatability, and integrated gameplay. Challenge was the second most common, encompassing both mechanical difficulty and intellectual problem-solving. Sensation included visual, auditory, and physical aspects, with visual aesthetics being the most frequently mentioned. Social appeals were divided into Fellowship (cooperative play) and Competition (striving against others or oneself). Other significant appeals included Exploration (discovering new worlds), Depth (player agency and consequences), and Submission (relaxation or "turning off the brain").

A critical finding was the correlation between these appeals and eleven standard video game genres. The data revealed that while some genres align with expected appeals (e.g., Narrative in RPGs, Physical Interaction in Music/Dance games), others showed unexpected overlaps. For instance, "Submission" was a top appeal for both Puzzle and Simulation games, despite their differing themes. Similarly, "Fellowship" was prominent in Shooter, Sports, and Driving/Racing games, though secondary appeals differed (Challenge for Shooters, Competition for Racing). These patterns suggest that appeals offer a nuanced layer of metadata that can facilitate cross-genre recommendations, allowing librarians to suggest diverse titles based on shared user motivations rather than superficial genre labels.

The authors conclude that integrating these appeals into library catalogs—potentially through crowdsourced metadata or proportional appeal displays—can enhance the quality of RA services. They advocate for a "whole-collection" approach, where video games are treated as equal partners in media recommendation alongside books and films. Future research will expand this framework to other media types, particularly investigating how appeals like mood and narrative manifest across different formats to support comprehensive, cross-media advisory services. The study provides a robust, user-centered foundation for improving video game discoverability and recommendation in library settings.

citation: Lee, J. H., Schmalz, M., Keating, S., & Ha, J. (2020). Challenges in Organizing and Accessing Video Game Development Artifacts. In A. Sundqvist et al. (Eds.), iConference 2020 (LNCS 12051, pp. 630–637). Springer Nature Switzerland AG.
target_sections: Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern|Cultural Heritage and Research|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Conceptual Terrain: Games, Play, and Library Service
summary_text:
Lee et al. (2020) address the critical gap in the preservation and organization of video game development artifacts, which are often overlooked compared to finished game products. While digital games are increasingly recognized as cultural heritage, the "digital ephemera" generated during their creation—such as game design documents, technical specifications, art bibles, and test builds—remain poorly organized and at risk of loss. The study investigates the challenges faced by stakeholders in organizing and accessing these materials, aiming to inform better preservation practices for libraries, archives, museums, and game developers.

The researchers employed a qualitative, user-centered methodology, conducting semi-structured interviews with 29 participants recruited via snowball sampling. The sample included 12 game industry professionals (developers and producers), 6 game historians, and 11 workers from memory institutions (libraries, archives, and museums). Data were analyzed using inductive coding following a consensus model to ensure reliability.

The findings reveal that stakeholders face significant hurdles in organizing and retrieving development artifacts, driven by three main factors: organizational culture, technical work environments, and a lack of standard vocabulary. First, organizational culture within the game industry often discourages documentation. Developers frequently view documentation as unproductive or tedious, prioritizing rapid product delivery over record-keeping. This is exacerbated by agile development methodologies that value "working software over comprehensive documentation." Consequently, early design documents are rarely maintained as living records, and changes are not systematically tracked. Second, the technical environment contributes to fragmentation. The use of diverse tools (e.g., Confluence, Dropbox, wikis) without consistent naming conventions makes retrieval difficult. Participants noted that while storage is easy, finding specific assets later is akin to "finding a needle in a stack of needles." Third, there is a profound lack of standardized terminology. Both industry professionals and memory institution workers struggle with inconsistent naming practices. Memory institutions often rely on inadequate general-purpose vocabularies or create isolated, homegrown controlled vocabularies, resulting in a fractured linguistic landscape that hinders interoperability.

Additionally, the study highlights a disconnect between different types of collections within memory institutions. Libraries, archives, and special collections operate on separate systems (e.g., WorldCat vs. Argus) without explicit links between related materials. This siloing prevents users from accessing a comprehensive view of a game’s history, as development artifacts may be scattered across unconnected repositories.

The authors conclude that addressing these challenges requires organizational interventions. They emphasize the need for a standardized vocabulary and naming conventions to describe video game development artifacts accurately. To this end, they are developing a taxonomy to extend existing metadata schemas like VGMS and GAMECIP. Furthermore, they advocate for exploring methods to connect related items across different collection types within institutions. The study suggests that these solutions could also benefit other digital media sectors, such as film and animation, which face similar preservation challenges with born-digital artifacts. Ultimately, the authors argue that without industry-wide drives toward documentation and standardized organizational practices, the historical context and development processes of video games will remain critically endangered.

citation: Lee, K. J., King, W. E., Dahya, N., & Lee, J. H. (2020). Librarian perspectives on the role of virtual reality in public libraries. Proceedings of the Association for Information Science and Technology, 57, e254. https://doi.org/10.1002/pra2.254
target_sections: Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Libraries as Playful Spaces and Service Environments|Outreach, Participation, and New Audiences|Gaps in the Literature and Future Research Agenda
summary_text:
This article presents a qualitative case study examining librarians' perspectives on implementing virtual reality (VR) programs in public libraries. Conducted in partnership with Washington State Libraries and Oculus, the research focused on seven diverse library sites across urban, regional, and rural settings. The primary data source for this paper consists of 36 semi-structured interviews with 23 librarians, conducted both before and after they facilitated VR programming using Oculus Rift headsets. The study aims to understand how librarians perceive VR as a tool for informal learning and social engagement, and to identify practical implications for successful implementation.

Prior to implementation, librarians held high expectations for VR’s educational potential, citing its immersive and interactive qualities as superior to traditional media. They envisioned VR as a means to enhance memory retention, foster empathy through perspective-taking, and allow for safe experimentation in subjects like science. However, post-implementation findings revealed a disconnect between these expectations and the available content. Librarians reported that patrons primarily learned how to operate the VR hardware rather than acquiring specific knowledge from the applications. The limited library of educational VR content often replicated traditional text-based learning models without leveraging immersive affordances, leading librarians to view the experience more as technological exposure than substantive learning.

A significant finding concerns the challenge of reaching marginalized communities. While VR provided free access to expensive technology for financially disadvantaged patrons, librarians expressed skepticism about its impact on other marginalized groups, such as immigrants or those with different cultural backgrounds. Recruitment hurdles, including complex waiver processes for minors and a lack of targeted outreach, resulted in participation skewed toward existing library users, particularly young males interested in gaming. Librarians noted that older adults and women were less likely to volunteer initially but often engaged positively once they observed others participating.

The physical setup of VR stations emerged as a critical factor in user engagement. Librarians faced a tension between privacy and visibility. Private setups offered comfort for users but reduced spontaneous participation, while public setups in main library areas encouraged observation and "peer coaching," where spectators actively guided users. This social dynamic transformed single-user VR experiences into communal events, fostering interaction among diverse age groups. However, accessibility remained a concern; standard VR hardware excluded patrons with mobility or cognitive disabilities due to the requirement for hand controllers and standing positions, highlighting that technology is not neutral.

The authors conclude that the success of VR programming depends heavily on librarian strategy rather than the technology alone. They recommend integrating VR with existing library programs, such as STEM or arts initiatives, to broaden appeal. Furthermore, they advocate for shifting from passive consumption to active content creation, encouraging libraries to involve community members in designing VR experiences relevant to their local contexts. This approach aims to address content limitations and promote inclusivity, ensuring that VR serves as a meaningful tool for informal learning and social engagement in public libraries.

citation: Supporting Youth Mental Health through Co-designing a Virtual Reality Experience: A Recipe-Based Co-design Model. Jin Ha Lee, Elin Björling, Ryan McLean, Anna Moreno, and Kung Jin Lee. The Library Quarterly: Information, Community, Policy 94(2): 134–158. April 2024.
target_sections: Service Purposes and Library Functions|Libraries as Playful Spaces and Service Environments|Community, Belonging, Wellness, and the Public Commons|Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Ground Services in Purpose, Audience, and Institutional Context
summary_text:
This qualitative study investigates the experiences of librarians participating in a multi-site initiative to co-design virtual reality (VR) experiences aimed at supporting teen mental health. Conducted across three public libraries—Seattle Public Library, DC Public Library, and Fayette Public Library in Texas—the project involved eight librarians who were interviewed before and after the program. The research addresses two primary questions: what opportunities and challenges librarians perceive in offering such programs, and what tensions emerge during implementation that inform future practices.

The study context is framed by the exacerbation of youth mental health issues during the COVID-19 pandemic and the growing role of libraries as community hubs for well-being. The program utilized a co-design approach where teens aged 14–18 collaborated with librarians and undergraduate interns to create VR environments focused on stress reduction and mindfulness.

Findings indicate a significant shift in librarian attitudes regarding mental health support. Initially, many participants expressed anxiety about "saying something wrong" or causing harm due to a lack of formal training in mental health. However, through the program, librarians reframed mental health from a binary crisis state to a spectrum of everyday well-being. This perspective change increased their confidence in facilitating discussions and creating safe spaces for youth expression. The co-design process itself was identified as a critical enabler of engagement, fostering rapport and allowing teens to feel valued. Librarians noted that the collaborative nature of design helped normalize mental health conversations.

However, the implementation revealed tensions regarding structure versus flexibility. Librarians with little prior co-design experience initially desired highly detailed lesson plans to mitigate uncertainty. As the program progressed, some found rigid structures restrictive when adapting to local contexts or technical issues. To address this, the authors propose a "recipe-based co-design model." This framework analogizes co-design to cooking: it identifies "main ingredients" (core principles like partnership and inquiry) that must be preserved, "substitutes" (tools or activities that can be swapped), and "local ingredients" (community-specific strengths). This model aims to provide enough structure for novice facilitators while allowing necessary improvisation.

The role of VR technology elicited mixed reactions. While some librarians found VR immersive and effective for social engagement, others highlighted significant barriers. These included technical setup times, the potential for motion sickness, and privacy concerns related to platform requirements (e.g., Facebook accounts for Oculus Quest). Crucially, some librarians observed that VR might exclude teens who do not identify as "techies" or those from marginalized groups less familiar with gaming culture. One site reported that VR was not the primary draw for their community, suggesting that the technology itself may not be universally suitable for all library contexts.

The study concludes that while co-design is a robust method for engaging youth and supporting mental health, the integration of VR requires careful consideration of accessibility and inclusivity. The proposed recipe-based model offers a practical tool for libraries to adapt co-design programs to their specific resources and community needs without compromising the core benefits of participatory design. Future work will involve testing this model across additional library sites to assess its effectiveness in building librarian confidence and program quality.

citation: Leenaraj, B., Arayaphan, W., Intawong, K., & Puritat, K. (2023). A gamified mobile application for first-year student orientation to promote library services. Journal of Librarianship and Information Science, 55(1), 137–150.
target_sections: Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Ground Services in Purpose, Audience, and Institutional Context
summary_text:
This study by Leenaraj et al. (2023) investigates the efficacy of a gamified mobile application, "CMU Journey," for orienting first-year students to library services at Chiang Mai University in Thailand. The research addresses the limitations of traditional orientation methods, which are often passive and fail to sustain student engagement or long-term knowledge retention. The primary objective was to compare the gamified app against traditional e-learning modules regarding knowledge acquisition, knowledge retention, and intrinsic motivation.

The methodology utilized an explanatory sequential mixed-methods design. A total of 60 first-year students were randomly assigned to either an experimental group (using the CMU Journey app) or a control group (using e-learning). The CMU Journey app employed mechanics inspired by *Pokémon Go*, requiring students to physically navigate the library campus, locate virtual monsters via GPS, and answer questions about library services to earn points and rewards. Data collection involved pre-tests, immediate post-tests, and delayed post-tests (one month later) to assess knowledge of library rules and resources. Intrinsic motivation was measured using the Intrinsic Motivation Inventory (IMI), focusing on perceived competence, interest, and effort. Qualitative data were gathered through open-ended surveys analyzing user feedback.

The quantitative results revealed distinct differences between the two interventions. Regarding immediate knowledge acquisition, there was no statistically significant difference between the gamified group and the e-learning group (p=0.036), indicating that both methods were equally effective for short-term learning. However, in terms of knowledge retention, the gamified group significantly outperformed the control group after one month (p<0.001). This suggests that the active, location-based engagement facilitated by the game helped solidify memory of library services over time.

Concerning intrinsic motivation, the e-learning group showed no significant changes in any IMI subscales. In contrast, the gamified group demonstrated a significant increase in "Interest" (p<0.001), though no significant changes were observed in "Perceived Competence" or "Effort." This indicates that while the game made the orientation more enjoyable and engaging, it did not necessarily alter students' perceptions of their ability to use the library or their perceived effort required.

Qualitative feedback from the experimental group was predominantly positive, with students describing the experience as "fun," "addictive," and "innovative." Many noted that the game motivated them to explore the library even if they initially lacked interest. However, some participants reported physical fatigue from walking and technical issues related to GPS accuracy and battery consumption on Android devices. Suggestions for improvement included adding multiplayer features, diverse question types, and deeper content challenges. The control group found e-learning helpful but often "boring" or too lengthy, with some experiencing login difficulties.

The authors conclude that gamified mobile applications are a valuable tool for library orientation, particularly for enhancing long-term knowledge retention and fostering intrinsic interest. They acknowledge limitations, including the small sample size (n=60) and single-site context, which restricts generalizability. Future research should involve larger, multi-institutional samples and incorporate additional game elements to sustain engagement beyond initial orientation events. This study contributes to the literature on Games-Based Library Services by providing empirical evidence that gamification can effectively bridge the gap between immediate instruction and long-term user adoption of library resources.

citation: Lester, D., Skulmoski, G. J., Fisher, D. P., Mehrotra, V., Lim, I., Lang, A., & Keogh, J. W. L. (2023). Drivers and barriers to the utilisation of gamification and game-based learning in universities: A systematic review of educators' perspectives. British Journal of Educational Technology, 54, 1748–1770.
target_sections: Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Libraries as Playful Spaces and Service Environments|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services
summary_text:
This systematic review by Lester et al. (2023) investigates the factors influencing university educators' adoption of gamification and game-based learning (GBL). Despite evidence suggesting educational benefits, many educators do not routinely use these approaches. The study aims to identify specific drivers and barriers to inform institutional support strategies.

The authors conducted a systematic search of Web of Science, Scopus, and EBSCO databases, identifying 1,330 articles. After removing duplicates and screening titles, abstracts, and full texts against strict eligibility criteria (peer-reviewed, English, published post-2011, focusing on educator perceptions), 17 studies were included for analysis: 11 quantitative, 2 mixed-methods, and 4 qualitative. The Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool was used to assess the quality of these studies.

The findings reveal that drivers and barriers can be categorized into attitudinal, design-related, and administrative domains. Key drivers include educators' beliefs that gamification/GBL fosters student interaction, collaboration, fun, and engagement. Quantitative data indicated that perceived usefulness and ease of use significantly predict an educator's intention to adopt these methods. Conversely, significant barriers were identified. Administrative challenges, such as lack of time, insufficient institutional support, and financial constraints, were prominent. Design-related barriers included difficulties in aligning game mechanics with learning outcomes and a lack of technical expertise. Attitudinal barriers involved skepticism about the proven benefits of GBL and concerns that games might be perceived as childish or distracting.

The review notes that while gamification is often viewed as easier to implement and more readily accepted by students, GBL faces greater resistance due to higher development costs and complexity. The authors acknowledge limitations, including potential bias in the included studies (e.g., over-representation of existing gamification users) and variability in sampling methods across different countries and disciplines.

In conclusion, Lester et al. argue that universities must adopt a multi-faceted approach to support educators. This includes disseminating robust evidence to shift attitudes, providing flexible training programs to address design skills gaps, and fostering administrative collaboration to remove structural barriers. The study suggests a phased implementation strategy, encouraging educators to begin with gamification before attempting more resource-intensive GBL initiatives.

citation: Lewallen, Kate. "Curating a Digital Collection." *Knowledge Quest* 48, no. 2 (November/December 2019): 42-46.
target_sections: Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy|Programming Formats and Service Models|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern
summary_text:
This article by Kate Lewallen, published in *Knowledge Quest* (2019), serves as a professional feature advocating for the adoption of breakout boxes within school library services. The primary objective is to align the use of these gamified educational tools with the American Association of School Librarians (AASL) Standards Framework for Learners, specifically highlighting their capacity to foster inquiry, collaboration, and resilience. Lewallen argues that while traditional academic merits are important, soft skills such as those outlined in the AASL Shared Foundations are critical for student success but often difficult to teach in conventional classroom settings. Breakout boxes, described as portable versions of escape rooms, offer a mechanism to gamify curriculum, thereby increasing student engagement and investment in learning outcomes.

The methodology of the article is descriptive and anecdotal rather than empirical. Lewallen draws upon her professional experience as the head librarian at the Webb School of Knoxville to illustrate practical applications. There is no formal data collection, statistical analysis, or controlled study design. Instead, the text functions as a guide, combining theoretical justification with practical implementation strategies. The population referenced includes students (notably sixth and ninth graders during library orientations), classroom teachers, and school staff. The "data" consists of the author’s observations regarding teacher adoption rates and student responses to breakout activities in various contexts, including classrooms, the library media center, and summer day camps.

Key findings reported by the author indicate that breakout boxes are highly adaptable resources. Physically, they consist of reprogrammable locks, boxes, and puzzle materials that can be customized for any subject area or grade level. Lewallen notes that while initial teacher adoption was slow, usage has increased over time as educators recognized the utility of these kits for introducing new units, reviewing content, or building team cohesion among faculty. In the library setting, breakout games proved effective for orientation, transforming a potentially passive introduction into an interactive exploration of the space. Furthermore, the author highlights the accessibility of digital breakout options via platforms like BreakoutEDU and Google Sites, which allow libraries with limited budgets to participate in gamified learning without purchasing physical kits.

The implications for library practice are significant. Lewallen posits that curating breakout boxes supports the librarian’s role as a teacher leader and collaborator. By providing resources that require students to work in teams, communicate effectively, and think critically under time pressure, librarians can directly support AASL standards related to inquiry and collaboration. The article provides actionable recommendations for other librarians, including creating free accounts on BreakoutEDU to access pre-made games, joining online professional communities for peer support, and starting with simple games to acclimate students to the format. Lewallen emphasizes that the success of these activities relies on a student-centered approach where the librarian acts as a "guide on the side," allowing students to take control of the problem-solving process. Ultimately, the article concludes that breakout boxes are a valuable addition to school library collections, offering a versatile, engaging method to develop essential soft skills and prepare learners for future challenges in college, career, and life.

citation: Litsey, R., Harris, R., & London, J. (2018). CTRL “C” – CTRL “V”; using gaming peripherals to improve library workflows and enhance staff efficiency. Journal of Access Services, 15(1), 34–43.
target_sections: Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Professional Capacity|Ground Services in Purpose, Audience, and Institutional Context
summary_text:
This article by Litsey, Harris, and London (2018) investigates the integration of computer gaming peripherals into library workflows to mitigate repetitive stress injuries (RSI) and enhance staff efficiency. The authors posit that traditional ergonomic interventions in libraries often fail to address the psychosocial dimensions of workplace stress, focusing instead on physical posture and tissue damage. By drawing parallels between the repetitive tasks performed by library staff and those undertaken by computer gamers, the study proposes that gaming-specific hardware can provide a more holistic solution.

The theoretical framework rests on two pillars: the tripartite model of RSI development (tissue damage, individual parameters, and psychosocial factors) and Csikszentmihalyi’s concept of "flow." The authors argue that while standard ergonomics address the first two factors, gaming peripherals can also influence psychosocial well-being by reducing cognitive load and increasing task control, thereby facilitating a state of flow characterized by immersion and enjoyment. This psychological benefit is hypothesized to improve job satisfaction and overall productivity.

Methodologically, the study examines the Document Delivery department at Texas Tech University Libraries, which processes over 100,000 requests annually. The intervention involved replacing standard input devices with gaming mice (specifically Logitech G300s, G600, and G13 models) and keypads. These devices were selected for their customizable buttons, high sensitivity (DPI), and ergonomic design. Staff members programmed frequently used commands, such as copy-paste functions, to easily accessible buttons, thereby minimizing the need for repetitive keyboard-mouse transitions and reducing physical strain.

To evaluate the impact of this intervention, the authors conducted a comparative analysis of transaction data. They selected article requests for analysis due to their labor-intensive nature. Random samples of 800 requests were drawn from two six-month periods: June 1, 2015, to December 1, 2015 (pre-implementation), and June 1, 2016, to December 1, 2016 (post-implementation). The results indicated a reduction in the average turnaround time from 1.77 days to 1.68 days, representing a gain of 2.1 hours per request on average. The authors interpret this finding as evidence that gaming peripherals enhance speed and accuracy while reducing the physical and psychological burden on staff.

The study concludes that gaming peripherals are an underutilized resource in library management. By optimizing tools for repetitive tasks, libraries can address the full spectrum of RSI risks. The authors emphasize that this approach is not limited to interlibrary loan services but is applicable to any library function involving repetitive data entry or mouse usage. The integration of such technology is presented as a cost-effective strategy to improve employee well-being and operational efficiency in an environment where staff are often required to do more with fewer resources.

citation: Littman, D. M., Milligan, T., Berry, R., Holloway, B., & Lawrence Scott, M. (2024). What do recently housed young people imagine for the future of third places? Using game‐based inquiry to (re)imagine afﬁrming, youth‐centered third places. American Journal of Community Psychology, 74, 127–141.
target_sections: Community_and_Inclusion|Service_ecology|Conceptual_Terrain: Games, Play, and Library Service|Cross-Cutting_Conditions_for_Effective_Games-Based_Library_Services|Ground_Services_in_Purpose,_Audience,_and_Institutional_Context
summary_text:
This article reports on a qualitative study utilizing game-based inquiry to explore how recently housed young people envision the future of "third places"—community settings such as libraries, parks, and community centers. The authors argue that while third places are theoretically important for social connection, they often fail marginalized youth, particularly those with histories of homelessness, due to exclusionary practices and hostile architecture. To address this gap, the researchers partnered with 21 young adult residents (ages 18–24) from a permanent supportive housing program in Grand Junction, Colorado. The study employed an original board game, the "Future of Third Places Game," designed through an iterative co-creation process involving residents and design experts.

The methodology centered on six game sessions where participants responded to "community request cards" depicting common pain points experienced by unhoused youth (e.g., lack of safe resting spots, exclusion from age-restricted venues). Using craft supplies, participants designed physical or social interventions to address these needs. The researchers analyzed transcripts of these sessions using thematic analysis, guided by Oldenburg and Brissett’s classic tenets of third places but adapted to capture constructivist perspectives on power and inclusion.

The findings revealed that participants prioritized three core tenets for affirming third places: agency and individualization, explicit inclusion, and the meeting of everyday needs. Unlike traditional theory which views third places as neutral social hubs, participants envisioned spaces that actively subvert oppressive hierarchies and provide essential resources. Specific qualities identified included multifunctional spaces that allow for various activities (from dancing to quiet rest), portable and adaptable infrastructure (such as mobile community centers or pods with retractable roofs), and freedom to choose how to engage in play. Participants also emphasized the importance of privacy options, the presence of supportive but non-authoritative adults, and free amenities like food and hygiene items.

The authors discuss these findings as a necessary expansion of third place theory, arguing that for marginalized youth, third places must function as sites of survival and safety, not just socialization. The study highlights the potential of game-based inquiry as a tool for participatory community planning, enabling stakeholders to co-design inclusive environments. Limitations noted include the small sample size (N=21), the lack of systematic demographic data collection which limits intersectional analysis, and the possibility that the game’s design cards may have primed certain responses related to rest and safety. The authors recommend future research explore these tenets across diverse geographic and identity groups and suggest that libraries and community centers adopt these youth-centered principles to better serve vulnerable populations.

citation: Low Stress High Mayhem Resources: Venture Society (GameRT, 2024)
target_sections: Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy|Community, Belonging, Wellness, and the Public Commons|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services
summary_text:
The provided text is a resource document associated with a 2024 GameRT (Games Round Table) session titled "Low Stress High Mayhem Resources: Venture Society," hosted by the American Library Association. The document functions as a curated bibliography and guide for librarians and educators interested in integrating tabletop role-playing games (TTRPGs) into library services, with a specific emphasis on low-stress, non-violent, and child-friendly options.

The primary purpose of the text is to provide practical resources for facilitating TTRPG sessions that prioritize safety and inclusivity. It begins by defining key terminology, including "tabletop roleplaying game," "cozy game," "roleplaying focused game," "non-violent ttrpg," and "kids ttrpg." This terminological framing establishes the scope of the resource list, which aims to move beyond traditional combat-heavy RPGs toward more wholesome and accessible experiences.

The document categorizes recommended games into three distinct groups based on their thematic content and target audience. For children and tweens, it suggests titles such as *Magical Kitties Save the Day* by Atlas Games, *Amazing Tales* by Martin Lloyd, and *Quest* by The Adventure Guild. For players seeking "Redwall Vibes" (implying gentle, nature-focused fantasy), it recommends *Wanderhome* by Possum Creek Games and *Cozy Town* by Rae Nedjadi. For those interested in "Fantastical World Vibes," it lists *Monster Care Squad* by Sandy Pug Games. These recommendations are presented as alternatives to high-stress gaming environments, aligning with the session's title.

A significant portion of the text is dedicated to safety resources, reflecting a critical component of modern library programming involving interactive play. It provides links to the "TTRPG Safety Toolkit" collected by Kienna Shaw and Lauren Bryant-Monk, including a guide and Google Drive access. This underscores the importance of establishing clear boundaries and consent mechanisms in gaming groups, particularly when working with younger or vulnerable populations.

Furthermore, the text bridges recreational gaming with academic research by listing several scholarly articles that examine the benefits of TTRPGs. These include Ruff (2021) on social and emotional learning competencies; Cao et al. (2018) on interpersonal communication; Parks (2022) on social learning among autistic young adults; Taylor (2019) on emotional resiliency; Daniau (2016) on the transformative potential of role-playing games; Glickman (2022) on therapeutic use for LGBTQIA+ individuals; and Bawa (2022) on motivation in educational settings. This collection of literature supports the argument that TTRPGs can serve as effective tools for social-emotional development, therapy, and education, providing a theoretical foundation for their inclusion in library services.

The document also outlines the structure of the associated Twitch stream, which included an introduction to rules, actual play demonstration, and discussion. It concludes with standard community guidelines for the online session, emphasizing kindness, respect, and zero tolerance for hate speech, along with contact information for GameRT. Overall, the text serves as a comprehensive starting point for librarians seeking to implement safe, inclusive, and educationally beneficial TTRPG programs, supported by both practical game recommendations and relevant academic literature.

citation: Madray, A., & Catalano, A. (2010). The Curriculum Material Center’s Vital Link to Play and Learning: What’s the Connection? Education Libraries, 33(2), 11-17.
target_sections: Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Libraries as Playful Spaces and Service Environments|Learning and Literacy|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services
summary_text:
Madray and Catalano (2010) present a comprehensive overview of the intersection between play-based learning and the operational role of Curriculum Material Centers (CMCs) within academic libraries. The authors, serving as education liaisons and CMC librarians, argue that play is not merely recreational but a fundamental component of cognitive, social, and emotional development in children. Drawing on the works of educational theorists such as Friedrich Froebel, Jean Piaget, and Lev Vygotsky, the article establishes that learning occurs when the brain processes experiences from play, which enhances neurological and motor skills. The central thesis is that CMCs serve as vital links between theoretical child development research and practical classroom application for pre-service and in-service teachers.

The article details the historical mandate of CMCs by the National Council for Accreditation of Teacher Education (NCATE) and guidelines from the Association of College and Research Libraries (ACRL), which require collections to support P-12 curricula across various subjects and learning styles. The authors emphasize that games, manipulatives, and toys are among the most utilized materials in these centers. They provide subject-specific examples of how play materials facilitate learning:
*   **Mathematics:** The authors cite Kamii and Kato (2006) to argue that games offer intrinsic motivation and immediate feedback, superior to repetitive worksheets. While Ke’s (2008) research showed that computer gaming camps improved students' attitudes toward math without significantly raising test scores, the authors suggest that positive engagement is a precursor to learning. They recommend manipulatives like Unifix blocks and commercial games like Monopoly for teaching basic concepts.
*   **Science:** Play encourages natural inquiry. The authors note that Severeide and Pizzini (1984) found play integration improves problem-solving and achievement in science. Simple items like thermometers, rocks, and blocks teach empirical concepts such as physics and measurement.
*   **Social Studies:** Role-playing software and board games are highlighted for teaching complex historical and economic concepts. For instance, Monopoly is used to illustrate market exchange and financial management, while games like *Civilization* and *Oregon Trail* contextualize history.
*   **Music and Physical Education:** Music aids in gross motor skill development and memory, while PE games foster cooperation and physical health. The authors suggest CMCs direct teachers to specialized curricula rather than housing bulky sports equipment.
*   **Special Education:** The article stresses the importance of inclusive materials. Pretend play toys (e.g., doctor kits) and textured items support social and language development for students with disabilities, allowing them to express themselves and engage in sociodramatic play.

A significant portion of the text is dedicated to the practical challenges of collection development. Madray and Catalano warn against selecting materials that are too time-consuming for standard class periods (20–40 minutes) or overly fragile/technical. They advocate for "authentic" games that are fun first and educational second, citing Harris (2009). The authors also highlight the importance of collaboration with faculty to ensure materials align with state standards and developmental appropriateness.

Finally, the article outlines strategies for promoting CMC collections. Librarians are encouraged to move beyond passive housing of materials by conducting workshops, creating displays, and integrating demonstrations into library instruction sessions. The authors conclude that by guiding teachers in the selection and use of play-based materials, CMC librarians directly contribute to effective pedagogy and improved student outcomes. The piece serves as a professional guide for academic librarians seeking to optimize their CMC collections to support evidence-based, play-integrated teaching practices.

citation: Maluck, T. (2013). Tales from Play It Loud. *Library Trends*, 61(4), 779-789.
target_sections: Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern|Libraries, Public Knowledge, and the Contemporary Information Environment|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Fandom Festivals and Pop-Culture Events|Programming Formats and Service Models
summary_text:
Thomas Maluck’s 2013 article, "Tales from Play It Loud," presents a reflective case study on the implementation of multiplayer gaming programs at the Northeast Regional Branch of the Richland Library in Columbia, South Carolina. The primary objective is to document the operational experiences and social impacts of two distinct programs: "Play It Loud," focused on Nintendo Wii video games, and "Go Your Own Way," centered on the board game Go. Maluck argues that these programs successfully fostered positive social interactions among patrons and aligned with the Search Institute’s "40 Developmental Assets" for youth.

The methodology is qualitative and observational, relying on the author’s role as the supervising young adult librarian. The design of "Play It Loud" was informed by Scott Nicholson’s S.N.A.K.S. model (Strategy, Narrative, Action, Knowledge, Social), aiming to provide a variety of game archetypes to engage diverse players. The program utilized multiplayer Wii titles such as *Mario Kart*, *Super Smash Bros. Brawl*, and *Just Dance*, selected for their accessibility and appropriateness for teens and younger children. Attendance averaged 21 participants per monthly session, primarily aged 9–16, though it functioned as a family event. A secondary program, "Go Your Own Way," emerged from patron interest in board games during video game sessions. It utilized a simplified rule set ("First Capture Go") to lower the barrier to entry, attracting smaller groups of up to ten participants.

Key findings indicate that the programs facilitated significant social integration and behavioral development. Maluck reports that gaming bridged gender gaps; for example, teenage boys who initially mocked dance games eventually participated after observing peers enjoying them, while girls successfully competed in tournaments dominated by boys. The author maps observed behaviors to specific developmental assets, noting instances of "Positive Family Communication" where parents and children learned games together, and "Service to Others" where teen volunteers assisted with setup and tutoring. Cultural competence was also observed, with diverse groups interacting positively during play. However, the study notes limitations in managing younger children (around age 5), whose tantrums and lack of impulse control disrupted sessions for older participants. Consequently, an age limit of 10+ was enforced for "Play It Loud." Additionally, attempts to introduce educational games like *Minecraft* faced resistance from teens who preferred pure entertainment, highlighting a tension between recreational and educational goals in library programming.

The implications drawn by Maluck suggest that gaming programs can serve as effective engagement tools for libraries, building trust with youth and encouraging participation in other services. The collaboration between "Go Your Own Way" and a "Book Bingo" program demonstrated how cross-promotion could boost attendance for both initiatives. The article concludes that while challenges exist in balancing different age groups and educational objectives, the core goal of providing shared, positive gaming experiences was achieved. Maluck advocates for continued expansion of such programs, emphasizing the need for clear boundaries, staff supervision, and a diverse selection of games to accommodate various player preferences and intelligences.

citation: Manso Rodríguez, R.A. (2022). Gamificación en bibliotecas para la alfabetización mediática e informacional, tendencias, buenas prácticas y recomendaciones. Bibliotecas. Anales de Investigacion;18(3), 1-15.
target_sections: Games as Participatory and Expressive Media|Learning and Literacy|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern
summary_text:
This article by Ramón Alberto Manso Rodríguez (2022) presents a descriptive bibliographic review analyzing trends, good practices, and recommendations for applying gamification in Media and Information Literacy (MIL) programs within library settings. The study aims to synthesize existing literature to reinforce the application of game-based strategies in libraries and provide actionable guidelines for implementation.

The methodology follows the PRISMA statement for systematic reviews. The author conducted a search in Scopus and Web of Science databases using the keywords “gamification,” “media and information literacy,” and “library” for publications between 2011 and 2021. Additionally, a snowball sampling technique was employed to identify further relevant sources. From an initial pool of 233 records, 49 documents were selected based on inclusion criteria, which required peer-reviewed articles or conference proceedings focusing on gamification in library-based MIL contexts. The final corpus consisted primarily of studies from academic libraries (89.7%), with minor representation from public and school libraries.

The findings reveal a stable trend in research output over the decade, with 2011 being the most productive year. The analysis categorizes the literature into theoretical characterizations and practical implementations. Key themes identified include "Information literacy" and "Library escape rooms." The review highlights that gamification serves as an effective tool to increase user motivation, engagement, and autonomy in learning. It facilitates the development of critical skills such as information evaluation, combating misinformation (fake news), and mastering database usage. Common gamification mechanics observed in successful implementations include role-playing, cooperation, rewards, points, levels, badges, and immediate feedback. The article distinguishes between two primary application areas: library orientation, which familiarizes users with institutional resources, and MIL instruction, which focuses on deeper skill acquisition related to accessing, using, and producing information.

Despite the benefits, the author notes challenges in implementation, such as the tendency of some librarians to use gamification merely as a vehicle for exposition or passive quizzes, lacking interactive elements. Successful practices often involve simple games, mini-games, or digital platforms like Moodle, Kahoot, Unity3D, and Minecraft. The review emphasizes that failure should be integrated as part of the learning process, and that co-creation with users and faculty enhances the effectiveness of these programs.

Based on the synthesis of literature and the author’s expertise, the article proposes a six-step framework for implementing gamified MIL programs: (1) defining the target audience and their informational needs; (2) establishing a competency tree; (3) evaluating existing projects to identify best practices; (4) developing a game prototype that integrates specific learning objectives with game mechanics; (5) implementing the program; and (6) conducting systematic evaluation and feedback. The conclusion asserts that gamification is a valuable strategy for promoting lifelong learning and deep understanding of complex concepts, provided that design considerations prioritize interaction, accessibility, and meaningful engagement over mere entertainment.

citation: Manzano-León, A., Rodríguez-Rivera, P., Raposo-Rivas, M., & Rodríguez-Ferrer, J. M. (2024). Digital game-based learning for transgender identity awareness: a qualitative study in Spanish social education degree. Current Psychology, 43, 18065–18073. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12144-023-05556-3
target_sections: Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Learning_and_Literacy|Community_and_Inclusion|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Gaps in the Literature and Future Research Agenda
summary_text:
This qualitative study by Manzano-León et al. (2024) investigates the efficacy of Digital Game-Based Learning (DGBL) in raising awareness about transgender and gender diverse (TGD) individuals among undergraduate Social Education students in Spain. The research addresses the broader context of transphobia and stigma, which are rooted in rigid binary conceptions of gender and contribute to significant mental health challenges for TGD populations. The authors posit that traditional educational methods, such as lectures and debates, may be insufficient for changing deep-seated prejudices, whereas immersive methodologies like video games can foster empathy and understanding through narrative engagement.

The primary objective was to evaluate whether the narrative video game "A Normal Lost Phone" could enhance students' sensitivity to TGD issues and develop relevant professional competencies. The study employed a qualitative design involving five focus groups and participant observation. The sample consisted of 35 undergraduate students (29 female, 5 male, 1 non-binary) with a mean age of 20.52 years, selected via convenience sampling from a mandatory Social Education course. Participation in the specific game-based activity was voluntary. The intervention comprised two 2-hour sessions where students played the game cooperatively in teams, followed by a critical essay and optional focus groups to discuss their experiences.

The video game "A Normal Lost Phone" was selected for its trans-friendly narrative, which follows a transgender protagonist navigating identity concealment, family intolerance, and social support systems. The researchers conducted a thematic analysis of the focus group data using ATLAS.ti software, establishing a threshold of at least three instances for including sub-themes to ensure relevance.

The findings were categorized into three main themes: narrative and gameplay elements, training through the video game, and raising awareness towards TGD people. Quantitative summaries of qualitative responses indicated high levels of student engagement; 97.3% of participants reported high interest and motivation, describing the experience as fun, creative, and distinct from traditional learning methods. Regarding professional development, 91.9% of students perceived the activity as beneficial for their Social Education training, with 73.0% noting specific improvements in understanding how to treat TGD individuals professionally. Furthermore, 94.6% of participants reported a heightened awareness of the social realities faced by TGD people, citing the game's ability to simulate lived experiences and foster empathy. Additionally, 59.5% highlighted the game's potential in preventing violence and harassment against gender minorities.

The authors discuss these results as evidence that video games can serve as effective pedagogical tools for promoting sensitivity and reducing stigma. The immersive nature of the game allowed students to internalize perspectives different from their own, which is crucial for future social educators who will work with vulnerable populations. The study suggests that such methodologies can enhance student engagement and learning outcomes more effectively than traditional instruction.

However, the authors acknowledge several limitations. The sample size was relatively small and demographically homogeneous (predominantly young women), limiting the generalizability of the findings to other age groups, genders, or professional sectors. The voluntary nature of the participation may have introduced selection bias, as students with pre-existing positive attitudes toward gaming or TGD issues might have been more likely to participate. Additionally, the study did not measure participants' prior knowledge or experiences with TGD topics, which could have influenced their responses. Finally, the short duration of the intervention (four hours) prevented the assessment of long-term retention or behavioral changes. The authors recommend future longitudinal studies and quasi-experimental designs to further validate the effectiveness of DGBL in educational settings.

citation: Maranchak, M., & Maranchak, N. (2023). Gamification mechanics usage for UGC promotion in online library marketing. Ukrainian Journal on Library and Information Science, 11, 21–33.
target_sections: Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern|Outreach, Participation, and New Audiences
summary_text:
This article by Maranchak and Maranchak (2023) explores the application of gamification mechanics in internet marketing for libraries, specifically focusing on stimulating User-Generated Content (UGC). The authors posit that in the digital environment of Web 2.0, traditional advertising is often ignored by users fatigued by direct promotion. Consequently, UGC serves as a critical tool for building trust and organic reach, functioning as a form of digital word-of-mouth marketing.

The study utilizes qualitative methods, including bibliographic analysis, content analysis, and selective observation of internet resources. The data comprises an examination of official social media accounts of Ukrainian libraries and comparative case studies from international commercial brands such as Starbucks, Lay’s, and Mercedes-Benz. The authors do not conduct a quantitative experiment with human subjects; instead, they analyze digital artifacts—posts, comments, videos, and campaigns—to derive best practices for library marketing.

The findings categorize UGC into various formats, including comments, reviews, creative designs, photographs, videos, ratings, podcasts, and NFTs. The authors argue that gamification—the application of game thinking and dynamics to non-game contexts—can effectively motivate users to create this content. They propose a five-component strategy for launching successful gamified UGC campaigns: (1) an idea and format that align with the target audience’s interests; (2) a thoughtful reward system, which can be material (gifts, discounts) or immaterial (reposting on official pages, emotional satisfaction); (3) a clear call-to-action (CTA) with visual cues and deadlines; (4) an appropriate platform for content placement; and (5) rigorous selection criteria to ensure quality and reputation management.

The article highlights several types of gamification mechanics applicable to libraries: interactive (contests, challenges), visual (AR filters, stickers), inquisitive (quizzes, polls), competitive (status awards), and creative/social (co-creation). It cites the Starbucks #WhiteCupContest as a prime example, which generated nearly 4,000 entries in three weeks through a simple gamified prompt. Similarly, it references Ukrainian examples like Lay’s #SmileUA campaign. The authors also discuss offline-to-online gamification, such as art installations in libraries (e.g., the Foucault pendulum at NTUU "Igor Sikorsky Kyiv Polytechnic Institute") that encourage users to take photos and tag the institution.

The implications for library services are significant. The authors recommend that librarians adopt these marketing strategies to enhance user engagement and attract new patrons. By leveraging gamification, libraries can transform passive users into active brand ambassadors who voluntarily share positive experiences. The study concludes that while gamification is one of many tools, its proven effectiveness in commercial sectors makes it a necessary component of modern library internet marketing strategies, helping to strengthen relationships with existing clients and expand the user base through social proof.

citation: Martín-Martín, Óliver; Manero, Borja; Romero-Hernández, Alejandro (2022). “El videojuego como herramienta de alfabetización informacional en estudiantes universitarios”. Profesional de la información, v. 31, n. 4, e310402. https://doi.org/10.3145/epi.2022.jul.02
target_sections: Learning_and_Literacy|Service_Purposes_and_Library_Functions|Service_Area|Cross-Cutting_Conditions_for_Effective_Games-Based_Library_Services|Ground_Services_in_Purpose_Audience_and_Institutional_Context
summary_text:
This article by Martín-Martín, Manero, and Romero-Hernández (2022) investigates the efficacy of serious video games as tools for fostering information literacy (IL) among undergraduate students. The study addresses the challenge libraries face in engaging students with traditional IL instruction, proposing game-based learning as a motivational alternative aligned with the European DigComp framework.

The methodology centers on a quasi-experimental pilot study involving 14 university students, predominantly first-year Nursing majors. Prior to the experiment, the authors analyzed 1,639 library queries from a two-year period to identify common information needs, which informed the design of "El último examen" (The Last Exam), a custom 3D video game developed in Unity. The game tasks players with navigating a virtual university environment to complete research-related objectives, such as using library catalogs, searching bibliographic databases with Boolean operators, and utilizing reference management software.

Data collection employed a pre-test/post-test design using an anonymous questionnaire validated by four library experts. The instrument measured three dimensions: library usage/perception (Likert scale), IL competencies (12 multiple-choice questions), and intervention satisfaction. Statistical analysis confirmed normality via Shapiro-Wilk tests, allowing for paired Student’s t-tests to evaluate changes in IL scores.

The findings demonstrate a statistically significant improvement in IL competencies following the intervention. The mean score on IL questions rose from 53.75% to 85.71% (p < .001), indicating that the game effectively taught targeted skills such as database searching and catalog navigation. Participant feedback was overwhelmingly positive, with 92.85% reporting satisfaction and all participants expressing willingness to repeat the activity. Interestingly, while objective knowledge increased, students' subjective confidence in their information skills decreased from 89.86% to 64.28%, suggesting the game helped them recognize previous knowledge gaps. However, the intervention did not significantly alter actual library usage frequency or general perceptions of the library as a dynamic space.

The authors acknowledge several limitations. The small sample size and demographic skew toward Nursing students limit generalizability. Furthermore, despite positive outcomes, voluntary participation was low, raising questions about the ability of such games to attract students spontaneously without institutional support. The study concludes that while video games are valuable for enhancing IL competencies and motivation, they should be integrated as complementary elements within broader instructional frameworks rather than standalone solutions. Future developments should aim for higher production standards, incorporate marketing strategies, and address local library-specific queries to maximize engagement and utility.

citation: Mattson, J. L. (2021). Review of the book Games and Gamification in Academic Libraries edited by Stephanie Crowe and Eva Sclippa. Communications in Information Literacy, 15(1), 158–163.
target_sections: Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy|Community, Belonging, Wellness, and the Public Commons|Outreach, Participation, and New Audiences
summary_text:
This article is a professional book review of *Games and Gamification in Academic Libraries*, edited by Stephanie Crowe and Eva Sclippa, published in the Association of College and Research Libraries series. Reviewed by Janna L. Mattson from George Mason University, the text evaluates the volume’s relevance to academic librarians aiming to integrate gaming into library services. The review situates the book within the context of a "cultural renaissance" in traditional board games and the broader educational shift toward active and experiential learning.

The reviewed book is structured into four primary sections. Section I provides an overview of games and gamification, featuring case studies on hosting game nights and training student workers through rewards-based systems like "library flair." The reviewer notes that while these chapters offer best practices for collaboration with campus partners and local businesses, some content regarding escape rooms feels repetitive when compared to later sections. Section II addresses collection development, detailing strategies for acquiring and maintaining both electronic and tabletop game collections. Contributors from institutions such as the University of North Texas and Lycoming College provide practical advice on accessibility, circulation policies, and using databases like BoardGameGeek.com for assessment.

Section III focuses on information literacy instruction. The review highlights chapters that utilize games to address challenges in high-volume one-shot sessions and for-credit courses. Notable examples include the use of *Pandemic Legacy* to teach disinformation identification and escape rooms to foster problem-solving skills. Mattson observes that while these pedagogical approaches are innovative, not all chapters explicitly connect their methods to the ACRL Framework for Information Literacy, a linkage that is present in Chapters 11 and 12 but absent elsewhere. Section IV, the largest section, covers programming and outreach. It includes case studies on transforming library fairs into mystery games, which reportedly tripled attendance at Texas Tech University, and initiatives targeting casual gamers to reduce library anxiety and build community.

Critically, Mattson identifies several limitations in the edited volume. The primary critique concerns structural inconsistency; some chapters include helpful features like best practices and key takeaways, while others do not, leading to a lack of cohesion. Additionally, there is significant repetition regarding specific resources and game types, particularly escape rooms. The absence of a concluding chapter is noted as a missed opportunity to reflect on the future trajectory of gaming in libraries.

Despite these flaws, the review concludes that the book’s greatest strength is its wide appeal and practical utility for librarians across various roles. It serves as a comprehensive guide for implementing game-based services, offering adaptable models for institutions of different sizes. The reviewer affirms that games and gamification are effective tools for enhancing student engagement and learning, recommending the volume as a worthy addition to contemporary professional development literature in library science.

citation: Maxwell, L. (2023). Let's Roll: A Guide to Setting up Tabletop Role-Playing Games in your School or Public Library. London: Facet Publishing.
target_sections: Service Purposes and Library Functions|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy|Community, Belonging, Wellness, and the Public Commons|Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services
summary_text:
Lucas Maxwell’s 2023 guide, *Let’s Roll*, serves as a practical manual for librarians and educators seeking to implement Tabletop Role-Playing Games (TTRPGs), particularly Dungeons & Dragons (D&D), in school and public library environments.
