Furthermore, the inclusion of safety tools and a strict Twitch policy emphasizing respect and zero tolerance for hate speech underscores the importance of creating inclusive and safe environments for gaming activities in public libraries. The document serves as a practical example of how librarians and gaming professionals can structure playtest sessions, integrate safety protocols, and engage community members through interactive storytelling.

In conclusion, this text provides a faithful record of a specific GBLS-related event. It highlights the procedural aspects of hosting a TTRPG stream, the importance of participant diversity in play styles, and the necessity of safety considerations. While it lacks empirical rigor in terms of data collection, it offers valuable insights into the practical implementation of games-based services in library settings.

citation: Pan, Y., & Ke, F. (2023). Effects of game‑based learning supports on students’ math performance and perceived game flow. *Education Tech Research Dev*, 71, 459–479. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11423-022-10183-z
target_sections: Learning_and_literacy|Service_Conditions_Addressed
summary_text:
This study by Pan and Ke (2023) examines the efficacy of different types of learning supports within a game-based learning (GBL) environment, specifically focusing on their impact on sixth-grade students' mathematical performance and perceived game flow. The research addresses a central challenge in educational gaming: facilitating the transformation of intuitive learning into generative learning without disrupting the immersive experience of gameplay. The authors utilized a value-added approach, embedding modeling supports into an existing 3D architecture game called "ERebuild," which focuses on ratios, proportional reasoning, and geometry.

The methodology employed a pretest–posttest experimental design with repeated measures. Forty-one sixth-grade students from a middle school in the western United States were randomly assigned to one of three conditions: learning support with conceptual knowledge only (LSCK), procedural knowledge only (LSPK), or a combination of both (LSCPK). The supports were delivered as interactive completion tasks prior to engaging with specific game levels. Conceptual support involved creating relationship charts and answering multiple-choice questions to map semantic structures, while procedural support provided step-by-step computational instructions via fill-in-the-blank exercises. Math achievement was assessed using isomorphic test items aligned with Common Core State Standards, and game flow was measured using a customized seven-item Likert scale administered after gameplay.

The empirical results revealed significant differences in learning outcomes based on the type of support provided. A mixed-design ANOVA indicated a significant interaction between time and support type. While all groups demonstrated improved math scores from pretest to posttest, the LSPK group (procedural knowledge only) achieved significantly higher overall performance than both the LSCPK and LSCK groups. The LSCPK group outperformed the LSCK group, making conceptual-only support the least effective condition. The authors attribute this hierarchy to cognitive load theory; procedural knowledge may be acquired more rapidly within the limited intervention time (20 minutes per level), whereas conceptual mapping may impose higher cognitive demands or require more extensive training for transfer to conventional assessments. Additionally, the interactive nature of the supports in this study likely promoted active processing compared to passive review methods used in prior research.

Regarding game flow, the study found no statistically significant differences among the three conditions ($p = 0.58$). All participants reported positive flow experiences, suggesting that providing pre-task learning supports did not disrupt immersion or enjoyment. This finding aligns with previous literature indicating that scaffolding provided before gameplay can facilitate learning without interrupting the flow state during active play. Descriptively, the LSPK group reported the highest overall flow scores, though this difference was not statistically significant.

The authors acknowledge several limitations, including the small sample size, the absence of a control group (no-support condition), and the extrinsic nature of the learning supports. They recommend future studies employ larger samples, include control groups, and utilize evidence-centered design to intrinsically embed supports within game mechanics. Furthermore, they suggest using in-situ measurement tools like Flowometer to capture dynamic changes in flow rather than relying solely on post-game reflections. Overall, the study provides empirical evidence that procedural modeling supports are particularly effective for immediate math performance gains in GBL contexts and that such supports can be integrated without compromising the player's flow experience.

citation: Pan, Y., Adams, E. L., Ketterlin-Geller, L. R., Larson, E. C., & Clark, C. (2024). Enhancing middle school students’ computational thinking competency through game‑based learning. Education Tech Research Dev, 72, 3391–3419.
target_sections: Learning_and_Literacy|Service_Conditions_Addressed
summary_text:
This study by Pan et al. (2024) investigates the efficacy of game-based learning (GBL) in enhancing computational thinking (CT) competency, self-efficacy, and engagement among middle school students. Recognizing that traditional CT instruction often focuses heavily on programming syntax rather than broader problem-solving skills, the authors designed an intervention using a modified Minecraft environment called "Factory Craft." The study aimed to determine if GBL could improve CT outcomes and how individual differences such as gender, grade level, and prior gaming experience moderated these effects.

The methodology employed a one-group pretest-posttest design with 31 middle school students (52% male, 48% female; 94% Hispanic) recruited from a summer camp in Texas. Participants engaged in six 50-minute sessions involving gameplay focused on data analysis, algorithms, and the impacts of computing, aligned with CSTA standards. Data were collected via demographic surveys, CT competency tests (adapted from NAEP items), CT self-efficacy surveys, and repeated measures of game-based engagement using the User Engagement Scale.

The results indicated that while there was no statistically significant overall improvement in CT competency scores (p = .111), male students showed a significant increase in competency (p = .041). In contrast, CT self-efficacy improved significantly for the entire group (p = .004), with female students demonstrating a notable gain in self-efficacy (p = .028) despite not showing significant gains in competency scores. This divergence suggests that while boys may have performed better on objective measures of CT skills, girls experienced greater confidence in their problem-solving abilities through the GBL intervention.

Regarding engagement, students reported high levels of satisfaction and aesthetic appreciation but showed a significant decrease in perceived usability across lessons (p = .046). This decline indicates increasing cognitive load or frustration as tasks became more complex. Gender differences were observed in engagement patterns; females initially reported lower usability but improved in later stages involving sorting tasks, whereas males experienced consistent declines in perceived usability during challenging content-specific tasks.

The authors acknowledge several limitations, including the small sample size, lack of a control group, and reliance on self-reported data which may be subject to response biases. They conclude that GBL is effective for boosting CT self-efficacy, particularly among female students, and recommend that educators provide additional practice time for girls to bridge the gap between self-efficacy and actual competency. Future studies should incorporate control groups, larger diverse samples, and unobtrusive analytics to better understand the transfer of implicit knowledge to explicit skills in GBL environments.

citation: Patino, D.M., Summerlin, E., Benn, S., Roldan, W., Joya, A., Dahya, N., Luke, J., Lee, J.H., & Yip, J. (2021). Virtual Reality Art & Design Curriculum. Published by the University of Washington Information School Digital Youth Lab.
target_sections: Service Purposes and Library Functions|Cultural Stewardship and Preservation|Outreach, Participation, and New Audiences|Programming Formats and Service Models|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services
summary_text:
This document outlines the development, implementation, and outcomes of a six-module Virtual Reality (VR) Art & Design Curriculum designed for incarcerated youth in Washington State. Authored by a team from the University of Washington Information School Digital Youth Lab, the report details an educational intervention delivered in May and June 2019 at Echo Glen, a juvenile rehabilitation center. The primary objective was to introduce VR technology and digital art making to marginalized youth through an anti-oppressive framework, culminating in a co-curated public library exhibit.

The methodology centered on a participatory design approach, prioritizing safety, trauma-informed practices, and inclusivity. The curriculum avoided personal storytelling to prevent triggering past trauma, focusing instead on abstract world-building and art creation. The program involved two cohorts of 12 participants each, aged 13–21. Despite active recruitment efforts for female participants, the demographic was predominantly male, with only one regular female attendee. The setting presented unique challenges, including strict security protocols, limited access to students prior to the program, and difficulties in obtaining parental consent for research components due to family separation. Consequently, empirical data collection was limited; while 13 educators and librarians were interviewed, only three youth participated in a focus group due to consent barriers.

The curriculum consisted of six two-hour sessions covering VR introduction, world-making (using *Tiny Town VR*), future building (*Fantastic Contraption*), music visualization (*Fantasynth*, *Beat Saber*), art creation (*Tilt Brush*, *SculptVR*), and exploration. Each session combined VR experiences with non-VR activities such as drawing, block building, and discussion to ensure all participants remained engaged. The physical setup was carefully designed to mitigate vulnerability; VR users faced the room with their backs to bookshelves rather than other students, and small groups spectated via computer screens.

Findings were primarily qualitative and anecdotal. The school principal reported notable increases in student engagement and positive social behavior. Youth feedback highlighted VR’s ability to lower artistic barriers; one participant noted that individuals who could not draw traditionally produced "reasonably good" work in VR. The program concluded with the development of "Here But Not Here," an exhibit installed at Snoqualmie Library for four weeks. This exhibit utilized a co-curation model, sharing curatorial authority with the youth participants. Themes derived from student feedback, such as VR being "realistic" and "trippy," guided the exhibit’s narrative.

The authors conclude that this curriculum offers a replicable model for library services in restrictive or marginalized settings. They emphasize the importance of anti-oppressive principles, including acknowledging power dynamics and centering community voices. While the research component faced significant limitations, the educational program succeeded in fostering creativity and engagement. The report suggests that VR can serve as a valuable tool for social-emotional learning and inclusive programming in libraries, provided that safety, accessibility, and ethical considerations are rigorously addressed.

citation: Steinerová, J. (2015). Looking for Creative Information Strategies and Ecological Literacy. In S. Kurbanoğlu et al. (Eds.), *Information Literacy: Moving Toward Sustainability* (pp. 3–12). Springer International Publishing.
target_sections: Conceptual Terrain: Games, Play, and Library Service|Service Purposes and Library Functions
summary_text:
This article, titled "Looking for Creative Information Strategies and Ecological Literacy," presents a theoretical framework for understanding creativity within the domain of information literacy. Authored by Jela Steinerová and published in the proceedings of the Third European Conference on Information Literacy (ECIL 2015), the work addresses the gap in information science research regarding the role of creativity in information behavior. The primary objective is to identify creative information strategies and define "ecological information literacy" as a holistic approach to information use that considers the environment and context.

The methodology employed is a meta-analysis of three existing empirical studies, combined with a review of phenomenographic literature on information use patterns. The first dataset analyzed involves semi-structured interviews with 19 doctoral students from diverse disciplines, revealing variations in information styles such as monitoring key sources in sciences versus deeper categorization in social sciences. The second dataset comprises interviews with 17 information managers at Slovak universities, highlighting the significance of values, people, and creativity as knowledge assets. The third dataset is a survey of 255 scholars, artists, and information professionals in Slovakia, which compared analytical processing strategies used by professionals with holistic creative strategies employed by artists.

The findings synthesize these sources to propose that information strategies can be divided into traditional analytic strategies and intuitive strategies, with the latter providing greater scope for human creativity through uncertainty, heuristics, and serendipity. The author identifies three main patterns of information use: evolutionary, interactive, and sequential. Based on this analysis, a conceptual model of creative information strategies is developed, structured around three dimensions: problem-oriented, knowledge-oriented, and interactive. Within these dimensions, the article outlines specific supportive strategies, including conceptual structuring, conceptual exploration, and conceptual navigation. The model posits that creative information behavior relies on sources such as metaphors, analogies, intuition, and empathy, rather than solely on logical structures.

A significant portion of the article is dedicated to defining "ecological information literacy." This concept is described at two levels: a macro level involving environmental awareness and sustainability in library operations (e.g., green libraries), and a micro level concerning individual information behavior. At the micro level, ecological information literacy involves cultivating the information environment, assessing relevance, making informed decisions, and developing resilience against information overload. The article argues that users build their own "ecological information spaces" through mixed strategies of orientation and creative exploration.

The implications of this research suggest a shift in information literacy instruction and service design. The author recommends that digital libraries and information services should be designed to support implicit knowledge discovery, visualization, and collaboration. By integrating intuitive and synthetic thinking into information strategies, institutions can foster more creative and innovative information use. The proposed conceptual model serves as a guide for developing digital spaces that enhance information literacy by accommodating the complex, contextual, and creative nature of human information behavior. The article concludes by emphasizing the need for further research to better understand how communities of practice can be supported in becoming more creative in their information use.

citation: Pinedo, R., García-Martín, N., Rascón, D., Caballero-San José, C., & Cañas, M. (2022). Reasoning and learning with board game-based learning: A case study. Current Psychology, 41, 1603–1617.
target_sections: Learning_and_Literacy|Community_and_Inclusion|Service_Ecology
summary_text:
This qualitative case study, conducted by Pinedo et al. (2022), investigates the perceptions and impacts of Game-Based Learning (GBL) using board games within a public primary school in Spain. The research aims to understand how the educational community—comprising teachers, students, and families—interprets GBL and its effects on the teaching-learning process. The study is situated in a context where GBL was progressively introduced over two academic years (2017–2019) following teacher training, with the goal of fostering twenty-first-century skills such as critical thinking, cooperation, and self-regulation.

The methodology employed an exploratory, holistic, and intrinsic case study design. Data collection involved four techniques: document analysis of school projects, observation of 24 game sessions involving 45 students (ages 11–12), a focus group with five form tutors, and semi-structured interviews with two students, one teacher, and one family volunteer. The sample included students with special educational needs (16% in both observed grades). Data were analyzed using constant comparison and thematic coding via ATLAS.ti software until category saturation was reached, with high inter-rater reliability (Cohen’s Kappa = 0.92).

The findings are organized into three primary themes: GBL characteristics, participation and inclusion, and quality learning and reasoning. First, regarding characteristics, participants identified motivation, positive emotions, and flexibility as key attributes of GBL. Teachers and students reported that games foster both intrinsic and extrinsic motivation, creating a "flow state" where failure is perceived as part of play rather than academic incompetence. This environment reduces anxiety and encourages engagement, particularly among shy or struggling students.

Second, the study highlights GBL’s role in promoting participation and educational inclusion. The flexible nature of board games allows for adaptation to diverse learning levels, enabling students with special needs to remain in mainstream classrooms rather than being segregated. Furthermore, GBL facilitates family involvement; volunteers assist in game sessions, which strengthens the home-school connection and provides parents with strategies to support learning at home. Teachers noted that family presence allowed them to observe student interactions more closely and provide individualized attention.

Third, the research addresses quality learning and reasoning. Participants associated GBL with the acquisition of curricular content (e.g., mathematics, language) and the development of executive functions (EFs), such as working memory, inhibition, cognitive flexibility, and planning. Teachers observed that games require strategic thinking and problem-solving, thereby enhancing high-level cognitive abilities. However, a significant challenge identified was the difficulty in assessing specific curricular competencies gained through GBL, as traditional evaluation methods do not easily capture improvements in strategy or executive functions.

The authors conclude that GBL is a valuable active methodology for fostering inclusive education and developing essential cognitive and social skills. They recommend further research to generalize these findings and emphasize the need for teacher training to effectively integrate GBL into the curriculum. The study acknowledges limitations related to the single-case design, which restricts generalizability, but suggests the derived category map can serve as a foundation for future longitudinal or quantitative studies.

citation: Ponto, K., Tredinnick, R., Verbeke, M., Kopp, K., Swanson, L., & Gagnon, D. (2023). Waddle: using virtual penguin embodiment as a vehicle for empathy and informal learning. In 29th ACM Symposium on Virtual Reality Software and Technology (VRST 2023), October 09–11, 2023, Christchurch, New Zealand. ACM, New York, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.1145/3611659.3617211
target_sections: Games for Learning, Health and Well Being|Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Analog, Digital, and Hybrid Affordances|Libraries as Playful Spaces and Service Environments|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Learning and Literacy
summary_text:
This article presents an empirical study evaluating "Waddle," a virtual reality (VR) application designed to promote informal learning and empathy through the virtual embodiment of an Adélie penguin. The research addresses gaps in previous VR animal embodiment studies, which often failed due to a lack of visuomotor alignment between the user and the avatar. By leveraging hand-tracking technology on Oculus Quest 2 headsets, the developers created an experience with intentional gesture congruence, allowing users to see themselves as penguins—complete with beaks and flippers—and perform species-specific actions such as nest building, mating dances, and defending eggs from predators.

The primary objective was to determine if this embodied VR experience could facilitate informal learning, induce a sense of virtual embodiment, and increase empathy toward the animal. The study hypothesized that users would accept their penguin identity, that embodiment would enhance learning outcomes in informal settings, and that embodiment would correlate with increased empathy.

The methodology involved a pre-test/post-test design with a sample of 43 students aged 8 to 13 (grades 3–8). Participants were recruited from informal learning organizations attending a science festival at a university campus. The VR experience lasted approximately 20 minutes. Data collection utilized three instruments: the Dispositional Empathy with Nature scale for empathy, a modified Avatar Embodiment Questionnaire for embodiment, and two open-ended questions to assess informal learning regarding penguin life cycles and challenges. Statistical analyses included paired t-tests for pre/post comparisons and Pearson correlations to examine relationships between variables.

The findings supported all primary hypotheses. First, the study demonstrated significant gains in informal learning, with participants showing improved ability to identify major life events and challenges faced by Adélie penguins after the experience. Second, embodiment scores were positive (Mean = 0.986), indicating that participants successfully experienced a sense of ownership over the virtual penguin body. Third, and most notably, there was a statistically significant increase in empathy scores from pre-experience (M = 0.85) to post-experience (M = 1.65), with p = 0.005. Furthermore, a strong positive correlation was observed between embodiment scores and post-experience empathy ratings (r = 0.53, p < .001), suggesting that the degree to which users felt embodied as penguins directly influenced their empathetic response.

The authors conclude that direct animal embodiment in VR is not only possible but effective for educational purposes. The significant correlation between embodiment and empathy underscores the potential of VR as a tool for fostering social engagement with ecology and environmental appreciation. The study highlights the importance of visuomotor synchrony in achieving these outcomes, distinguishing it from prior failed attempts at animal embodiment. While the results are promising, the authors note that future research is needed to isolate specific design elements that contribute most to embodiment and empathy, and to further explore how these constructs interact to support informal learning. This work contributes to the broader field of games-based library services by demonstrating how immersive, embodied simulations can serve as effective vehicles for STEM education and affective learning outcomes in informal settings.

citation: Post, C., Henry, T., Nunnally, K., & Lanham, C. (2023). A Colossal Catalog Adventure: Representing Indie Video Games and Game Creators in Library Catalogs. *Cataloging & Classification Quarterly*, 61(7-8), 792-814.

summary_text:
This article presents an action research study examining the cataloging challenges associated with digitally distributed independent (indie) video games, specifically focusing on text-based narrative titles. As digital distribution supplants physical media and indie game production expands, library cataloging practices must adapt to represent these diverse cultural artifacts accurately. The authors argue that while existing guidelines from the Online Audiovisual Catalogers (OLAC) provide a necessary foundation, they often prioritize mainstream commercial games produced by large corporate entities. This study seeks to bridge that gap by analyzing how descriptive cataloging, subject access, and name authority control can be tailored for indie narrative games, which share characteristics with both traditional literature and interactive software.

The methodology involved the creation of 18 original MARC bibliographic records for games published by Choice of Games, a small publisher specializing in text-based interactive fiction. Two co-authors performed the cataloging using standard library systems (Connexion Client and WorldShare Management Services), while the full research team engaged in semi-structured interviews to reflect on the process. The resulting records were compared against existing practices at the University of Michigan Library, which holds a significant collection of digital narrative games.

In terms of descriptive cataloging, the authors found that digital indie games resemble other electronic resources more than physical media. They utilized MARC fields to designate these titles as computer programs with text-based content, distinguishing them from mainstream games typically coded as moving images. The study highlights the complexity of version control in digital distribution; while minor patches do not require new records, significant expansions or platform-specific releases may necessitate distinct bibliographic entries. The authors note that indie publishers like Choice of Games often provide clear bibliographic metadata similar to book copyright pages, facilitating accurate description compared to the opaque credit structures of many mainstream titles.

Subject cataloging presented a hybrid challenge. Because narrative games are driven by story and choice, they require subject headings that reflect their literary content (e.g., "Cyberpunk culture," "Detectives") alongside genre terms that capture their interactive mechanics (e.g., "Adventure games," "Role-playing games"). The authors observed that existing controlled vocabularies, such as LCSH and the OLAC Video Game Genre Vocabulary, often use terminology out of sync with the gaming community. For instance, terms like "Plot-your-own stories" fail to convey the interactive depth of digital narrative games. Consequently, the researchers supplemented controlled headings with uncontrolled terms used by players, such as "interactive fiction" and "text-based games," to enhance discoverability.

A significant finding pertains to name authority control. Mainstream video games are often attributed to corporate entities due to large, specialized development teams. In contrast, indie narrative games frequently involve smaller teams or individual authors with distinct creative roles. The study revealed a lack of existing Library of Congress Name Authority File (LCNAF) records for many indie creators. To address this, the authors created 33 new name authority records for individuals involved in writing, editing, and programming. This granular attribution is argued to be essential for accurately representing the production model of indie games and integrating these creators into the broader bibliographic universe.

The implications for library practice are substantial. The authors recommend that catalogers adopt a flexible approach, mixing controlled vocabularies with community-derived terminology to support user discovery. They emphasize the importance of creating name authority records for indie game developers to ensure consistent access points across institutions. Furthermore, the study suggests that as digital distribution evolves, cataloging standards may need further refinement to handle dynamic updates and diverse release models characteristic of independent game development. Ultimately, the research advocates for recognizing indie games not merely as software, but as significant cultural works deserving of rigorous and nuanced bibliographic representation.

citation: Public Library Association. (2023). *Public Library Services for Strong Communities Report: Results from the 2022 PLA Annual Survey*. Chicago: Public Library Association.
target_sections: none
summary_text:
The Public Library Association’s 2023 report, *Public Library Services for Strong Communities Report*, presents findings from the 2022 PLA Annual Survey. The primary objective was to establish a baseline of data regarding how U.S. public libraries address community needs through services, programs, partnerships, and facilities. The survey was administered in fall 2022 to all U.S. public libraries at the administrative entity level. Of the invited population, 1,167 libraries responded, yielding a 12.6% response rate. The data was weighted to reflect the national universe of public libraries and analyzed by locale: city, suburban, and town/rural.

The findings indicate that libraries continue to prioritize core services related to literacy, education, and civic engagement. Specifically, 84.7% of libraries are currently addressing literacy and educational achievement, while 66.6% focus on digital equity. Summer reading programs remain a cornerstone service, with 99.1% of libraries offering them for children, teens, or adults. In the realm of teen programming, games and gaming programs (65.6%) and STEAM programs (59.1%) are prominent alongside summer reading. For adult audiences, book clubs (84%) and art/music programs (68.5%) are most common. Notably, workforce development is an emerging focus; 77.5% of libraries offer job and career services, and 23.8% have specific workforce development programs.

Partnerships are nearly universal, with 97.5% of libraries reporting at least one partnership. However, these collaborations are predominantly informal (95%) rather than formal (59%). The most common partners are K-12 schools (89.7%) and local governments (85.2%). The report emphasizes that partnerships allow libraries to expand their reach despite resource constraints.

Significant challenges were identified regarding infrastructure and capacity. Approximately 40% of library buildings have not undergone major renovations since 2000 or earlier, with town/rural facilities being the oldest on average. Staffing shortages (cited by 73.4% of respondents) and funding limitations are the primary barriers to expanding services. Consequently, many libraries provide support services informally rather than formally, often due to a lack of trained staff or partners.

Regarding evaluation practices, while 91.3% of libraries track output measures such as circulation and attendance, only 29% engage in outcome measurement to assess the impact on patron learning and behavior. The report concludes that while libraries are adapting to post-pandemic realities and expanding into areas like food insecurity support (addressed by 31.6% of libraries) and sustainability initiatives, sustained investment in staff, funding, and physical infrastructure is critical for maintaining their role as resilient community hubs.

summary_text:

The document serves a dual purpose: it acts as a curated bibliography of queer media recommended by round table members and provides logistical details for a series of online events broadcast via Twitch.

The methodology is descriptive and curatorial. There is no empirical research, statistical analysis, or experimental design involved. Instead, the content relies on crowd-sourced recommendations from professionals within these specific library domains. The document is structured to facilitate easy navigation for librarians and patrons seeking inclusive materials, featuring a table of contents that directs users to sections on books, comics, film/TV, games, maps, and other content such as zines and podcasts.

A significant portion of the text is dedicated to the "Schedule of Events," which outlines specific programming intended to highlight queer representation in media. These events include a roundtable discussion on queer creators, a dramatic reading of the comic *Matchmaker* by Cam Marshall, a film screening of *Brother to Brother* directed by Rodney Evans, a presentation on queer geographies by MAGIRT, and a liveplay of the tabletop RPG *Yazeba’s Bed & Breakfast* led by GameRT. The core data presented consists of extensive lists of media titles categorized by format. In the "Games" section, notable titles include *Celeste*, *Undertale*, *Stardew Valley*, *Night in the Woods*, and various tabletop RPGs such as *Thirsty Sword Lesbians* and *Legacy of Dragonholt*. The "Comics" list features works like *Heartstopper*, *Gender Queer*, and *Lumberjanes*. The "Books" section includes titles such as *The House in the Cerulean Sea* by T.J. Klune and *Cemetery Boys* by Aiden Thomas. Additionally, the document lists resources for maps and geospatial information, such as "Everywhere Is Queer" and historical gay guides, as well as other content like the LGBTQ Video Game Archive and various queer-focused zines and podcasts.

The implications of this document for library services are centered on collection development and inclusive programming. By providing a vetted list of queer media, the Round Tables offer librarians actionable resources to diversify their collections and better serve LGBTQ+ patrons. The emphasis on live-streamed events demonstrates a model for virtual community engagement and professional learning, particularly relevant in the context of games-based library services where interactive media and digital platforms play a crucial role. The document also serves as an administrative tool, providing contact information, social media links, and calls for volunteerism and board nominations for each Round Table, thereby supporting the organizational sustainability of these specialized groups within the ALA. Overall, the text functions as both a practical guide for resource acquisition and a promotional instrument for queer-inclusive library programming.

summary_text:
The provided text is a curated resource document titled "Resources for Understanding D&D's New OGL," dated 2023. Its primary objective is to compile and organize a variety of external materials that elucidate the changes and controversies surrounding the Open Gaming License (OGL) for Dungeons & Dragons. The document does not present original empirical research, data analysis, or theoretical arguments. Instead, it functions as a bibliographic guide, aggregating links and references to help readers navigate the complex information ecosystem generated by the license updates.

The methodology employed is purely curatorial. The author has categorized resources into several distinct sections: Articles, Forum Discussions, News Reports, Podcasts, Videos, and Miscellaneous items. Under the "Articles" section, the document lists sources from diverse perspectives, including the Electronic Frontier Foundation (likely providing a legal or consumer rights perspective), Gizmodo (reporting on the announcement of changes), the original WotC OGL version, Wizards of the Coast's response to a leaked update, a Medium article by Noah Downs, a Reddit AMA conducted by a third-party creator, Dicebreaker, and ARS Technica. This selection indicates an attempt to balance official corporate communications with independent journalism, legal advocacy, and community voices.

The "Forum Discussions" section specifically highlights a thread from the Foundry Virtual Tabletop Reddit community, pointing to the relevance of digital tabletop platforms in this discourse. The sections for "News Reports," "Podcasts," and "Videos" are present but contain no specific entries in the provided text, suggesting either an incomplete draft or a placeholder structure. The "Miscellaneous" section includes significant items such as the #OpenDND petition, indicating organized community resistance or advocacy. It also references historical and analytical content, including a discussion by Ryan Dancey, identified as one of the architects of the original OGL, regarding its creation, and a PhD thesis titled "Can't Revoke!" associated with Roll For Combat. Additionally, it mentions content related to "One D&D," linking the license changes to broader product line updates.

In terms of findings, the document does not report statistical results or measured outcomes. However, it implicitly finds that the OGL changes have generated substantial multi-modal discourse. The inclusion of legal resources (EFF, PhD thesis), corporate statements (WotC), and community actions (petition, Reddit AMA) suggests that the issue is perceived as having legal, creative, and social dimensions. The document serves as a map to these various facets rather than an analysis of them.

For a literature review on Games-Based Library Services (GBLS), this document has limited direct relevance regarding pedagogical methods or library service models. However, it may be relevant in the context of understanding the legal and licensing frameworks that govern tabletop role-playing games (TRPGs) used in library settings. Libraries often utilize TRPGs for community engagement, literacy programs, and social skill development. Understanding the OGL is crucial for libraries and educators who create or distribute derivative materials, as it affects their ability to legally use and adapt game content. The document highlights the volatility of these licenses and the importance of staying informed about legal changes that could impact library programming involving Dungeons & Dragons. It underscores the need for librarians to be aware of the broader industry context, including community sentiment and legal precedents, when integrating commercial TRPGs into their services. The compilation serves as a snapshot of the information landscape at a specific point in time (2023), reflecting the heightened attention on intellectual property rights within the gaming community.

citation: Robson, Diane, Jessica Phillips, and Steven Guerrero. "Don’t Just Roll the Dice: Simple Solutions for Circulating Tabletop Game Collections Effectively in Your Library." *Library Resources & Technical Services* 62, no. 2 (April 2018): 80–90.
target_sections: Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Ground Services in Purpose, Audience, and Institutional Context|Fandom Festivals and Pop-Culture Events
summary_text:
This article presents an empirical study conducted by librarians at the University of North Texas (UNT) Media Library to establish best practices for circulating tabletop game collections. Recognizing a lack of professional literature on this topic, the authors designed an experiment to evaluate the efficacy of various preservation and processing methods against the costs in staff time and resources. The study focused on two popular titles, *Ticket to Ride* (TR) and *Betrayal at House on the Hill* (BHH), using four copies of each game. Each copy underwent a different level of intervention, ranging from no treatment (Copy 1) to minimal processing (Copy 2), moderate preservation/processing (Copy 3), and extensive preservation/processing (Copy 4).

The primary phase involved an eight-month circulation period (July 2015–May 2016). The authors tracked circulation frequency, damage severity (graded on a 0–3 scale), piece loss, and the time required for processing. Results indicated that damage across all copies was minimal to minor, with no game becoming unusable. Crucially, the loss rate was negligible across all conditions, remaining below 1% regardless of the intensity of preservation or processing. For instance, TR Copy 1 (minimal processing) lost one piece after 25 circulations, while TR Copy 4 (full processing) lost zero pieces after 15 circulations. However, the labor cost disparity was stark: fully processing BHH Copy 4 took approximately 26 hours, costing roughly twelve times the price of a new game, whereas minimal processing took only seven minutes. The authors concluded that the marginal gain in preservation from full processing did not justify the significant expenditure of staff time.

To further assess durability, the authors conducted controlled stress tests exposing game components to water, soda, cheese dip, and salsa for varying durations, as well as physical impact tests on boxes and instruction booklets. These tests revealed material-specific vulnerabilities. Cardstock warped when exposed to liquids but remained usable; sleeving cards mitigated liquid damage, though open sleeves could trap contaminants if not properly cleaned. Chipboard tokens and tiles were highly susceptible to delamination from moisture, with contact paper offering complete protection but requiring prohibitive application time. Box corners reinforced with cloth tape showed superior resilience compared to untreated boxes or those with clear tape. Instruction booklets bound in pamphlet binders or Velo binding sustained no damage from impact, outperforming sewn or sleeved alternatives.

Based on these findings, the authors recommend a targeted approach for library collections. They advocate for minimal processing—specifically, a single barcode on the box and bagging game pieces to prevent loss—as sufficient for most items. Preservation efforts should be prioritized for high-use games: card sleeves are recommended to extend lifespan and prevent wear-based advantages in gameplay; cloth tape should be used to reinforce box corners; and durable binding methods should be employed for instruction manuals. The study concludes that tabletop games can be circulated effectively without extensive preservation protocols, as the cost of full protection outweighs the benefits given the inherent durability of modern game components and low loss rates.

citation: Robson, Diane, Catherine Sassen, Jason Thomale, and Kevin Yanowski. "Enhancing the Discovery of Tabletop Games." *Library Resources & Technical Services* 63, no. 3 (July 2019): 199–214.
target_sections: Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Discovery_and_advisory|Service_ecology
summary_text:
This article by Robson, Sassen, Thomale, and Yanowski presents a case study from the University of North Texas (UNT) Libraries regarding the enhancement of discovery tools for a collection of over 600 tabletop games. The authors identify a broader problem in library science: three-dimensional materials are often poorly cataloged or left uncataloged due to perceived complexity and a lack of standardized guidance, rendering them invisible to users in digital catalogs. To address this, UNT librarians implemented a multi-faceted approach involving rigorous descriptive cataloging, the creation of local genre terms, and the technical customization of their discovery layer.

The methodology begins with adherence to Resource Description and Access (RDA) standards for creating core bibliographic records. The authors detail specific MARC field applications for tabletop games, including recording identifiers (ISBN/UPC), physical descriptions (carrier type, extent, dimensions), and access points for designers and publishers. They emphasize that while RDA provides a baseline, local enhancements are necessary for optimal discovery. Consequently, the team developed a controlled vocabulary of 50 genre terms specific to tabletop games, drawing from the crowd-sourced database Board Game Geek but tailored to reflect both novice and expert terminology. These terms were assigned as authorized access points in MARC field 655.

A significant portion of the article focuses on the implementation of faceted search capabilities. Recognizing that standard metadata fields did not capture key user decision factors—specifically duration of play, number of players, and recommended age—the authors created a custom coding system. These attributes were encoded into MARC field 590 (Local Note) using standardized tokens (e.g., "d30t59" for 30-59 minutes). This data was then indexed into Apache Solr, an open-source search engine, where the codes were mapped to human-readable labels. The Blacklight discovery interface was configured to present these as filterable facets, allowing users to narrow search results by game characteristics.

The authors highlight that this technical achievement relied heavily on interdisciplinary collaboration between catalogers, systems librarians, and IT staff. They argue that catalogers must understand technological constraints to create useful metadata, while technologists must understand metadata structures to build effective interfaces. The article notes that the system was deployed in a prerelease version at the time of writing, so empirical user data on search success rates is not provided. However, the authors assert that the implementation successfully addressed the discoverability issues inherent in three-dimensional collections.

The implications for games-based library services are significant. The study demonstrates that effective access to game collections requires more than mere acquisition; it demands robust metadata strategies and discovery interface customization. The authors caution that such customizations require sustained institutional support and resources, which may not be available to all libraries. Nevertheless, they advocate for the principle of enhancing metadata to meet user needs, suggesting that even libraries without custom development capabilities can benefit from improved cataloging practices and genre term assignments. The article serves as a technical guide for other institutions seeking to improve access to non-traditional library materials through integrated cataloging and systems work.

citation: Rossi, S., Lee, J. H., & Clarke, R. I. (2014). Mood Metadata for Video Games and Interactive Media. In 2014 IEEE International Conference on Multimedia and Expo Workshops (ICMEW) (pp. 1-6). IEEE.
target_sections: Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Analog, Digital, and Hybrid Affordances|Collections, Circulation, and Equitable Access|Description, Discovery, and Games Advisory|Game Evaluations are Not Without Concern|Conceptual Terrain: Games, Play, and Library Service
summary_text:
This article presents the development and preliminary evaluation of a Controlled Vocabulary (CV) for mood metadata within the domain of video games. Recognizing video games as significant cultural artifacts in digital library collections, the authors argue that existing metadata schemas lack robust mechanisms for representing the emotional tone or "mood" of games, despite its importance to gamers. The primary objective was to create a standardized set of mood terms to facilitate search and collocation functions in library systems.

The methodology began with a review of scholarly literature on mood models from psychology and music information retrieval, specifically citing works by Russell, Thayer, and Watson and Tellegen. These theoretical frameworks provided the structural basis for organizing mood dimensions. The authors then harvested existing mood taxonomies from sources such as AllMusic.com, the Movie Genome Project, gamerDNA.com, and Amazon customer reviews. Through an iterative refinement process, redundant and genre-specific terms were removed, resulting in a CV containing 17 defined mood terms, each with equivalent terms and illustrative game examples. These terms include "Adventurous," "Aggressive," "Cute," "Dark," "Horror," "Humorous," "Imaginative," "Intense," "Light-hearted," "Mysterious," "Peaceful," "Quirky," "Romantic," "Sad," "Sarcastic," "Sensual," and "Solitary."

To evaluate the applicability of this CV, the authors annotated a sample collection of 617 video games. The sample was deliberately selected to ensure diversity in genres and release years, sourced from allgame.com. The empirical findings indicated distinct patterns in term usage. The most frequently assigned mood terms were "adventurous," followed by "aggressive" and "intense." Conversely, the least utilized terms were "sarcastic," "sad," and "sensual." The analysis also revealed potential correlations between specific video game genres and certain mood descriptors, as well as chronological trends in mood representation; for instance, the term "aggressive" showed increased frequency in games released after 1992.

A critical limitation identified during the evaluation was the inherent subjectivity of mood annotation. The authors note that mood perception is influenced by individual user states and fear responses, leading to inconsistent classifications (e.g., one user might classify a game as "horror" while another sees it merely as "dark"). To address this variability, the authors propose allowing multiple mood tags per game and suggest future crowdsourcing efforts to rank terms based on broader user input.

The implications of this work extend to both library science and user experience design. By establishing a standardized mood vocabulary, libraries can offer enhanced search and browse capabilities that align with how gamers naturally conceptualize media. The authors conclude by outlining plans for future research, specifically user studies designed to test the CV’s usability in real-world information-seeking scenarios. This study contributes to the nascent field of video game metadata by providing a structured, empirically tested framework for capturing the emotional dimensions of interactive media.

citation: Ryan, J., Porter, M., & Miller, R. (2010). Academic Library Services in Virtual Worlds: An Examination of the Potential for Library Services in Immersive Environments. Journal of Information Technology Education: Innovations in Practice, 9, 253–272.
target_sections: Service Purposes and Library Functions|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Ground Services in Purpose, Audience, and Institutional Context|Conceptual Terrain: Games, Play, and Library Service
summary_text:
Ryan, Porter, and Miller (2010) present a critical examination of the potential for academic libraries to utilize immersive virtual worlds, specifically focusing on Second Life, Active Worlds, and Openlife Grid. The authors note that while library literature has been saturated with enthusiasm for Second Life, it often lacks rigorous comparative analysis or critical assessment of practical implementation challenges. To address this gap, the authors conducted a structured evaluation of three virtual platforms against criteria relevant to library services: cost, technical requirements, ease of use, content creation, and risk.

The methodology involved the authors creating accounts in each platform and independently evaluating them. They also surveyed 170 undergraduate students, finding that only two had heard of Second Life and none had used it, highlighting a disconnect between institutional interest and student engagement. The evaluation revealed significant barriers to adopting virtual worlds for core library functions. For reference services, the authors concluded they are not advantageous due to low traffic, the high demand for technical assistance rather than information seeking, and licensing restrictions that prevent real-time access to library databases. Similarly, collection access was deemed impractical; virtual library buildings often merely link to external web pages, offering no benefit over standard websites while incurring high maintenance costs.

Instructional use was also found to be problematic. Real-time instruction is hindered by technical difficulties, navigation complexities, and the inability of instructors to monitor student activities outside the virtual environment. While asynchronous instructional modules are possible, they offer no distinct advantage over existing technologies. However, the authors identified exhibits and outreach as the most viable application. Immersive environments excel at creating rich, contextual "sims" for special collections or historical topics, providing an emotional and experiential depth that traditional media cannot match. Nevertheless, these exhibits require substantial bandwidth, technical skill to build, and are susceptible to performance lag.

The study highlights significant risks associated with virtual worlds, particularly Second Life. These include high financial costs for land and maintenance, technical instability, and safety concerns such as exposure to inappropriate content, "griefing" (malicious user behavior), and lack of content ownership. The authors emphasize that virtual world providers are commercial enterprises with limited liability, leaving libraries vulnerable to service disruptions and loss of intellectual property.

In conclusion, Ryan, Porter, and Miller argue that current virtual world technologies do not meet the specific needs of academic libraries. Rather than forcing library services into ill-fitting platforms, they recommend that libraries collaborate through consortia to demand developer improvements, such as web-based interfaces, faster startup times, and better navigation tools. Until these changes are realized, the authors advise caution, noting that the effort, cost, and risk currently outweigh the benefits for most academic library applications.

citation: Sánchez-Cabrero, R. (2024). Conexiones en las nuevas metodologías educativas del Lore como recurso educativo. Educación y Humanismo, 26(46), 126-153. https://doi.org/10.17081/eduhum.26.46.6668
target_sections: Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Learning and Literacy|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Game Design Workshops, Jams, and Participatory Creation
summary_text:
This article by Roberto Sánchez-Cabrero (2024) investigates the potential of "Lore"—the intricate, secondary narratives and world-building elements characteristic of video games—as a resource for formal education. The study addresses whether trends in digital leisure can be successfully translated into pedagogical tools, similar to how gamification and serious games have been adopted. The primary objective is to evaluate the feasibility of using Lore in educational settings by reviewing existing academic literature and identifying structural parallels between Lore and contemporary educational methodologies.

The methodology employed is a theoretical review following the PRISMA protocol for systematic literature exploration. Searches were conducted in October 2023 across Web of Science and Scopus databases using specific Boolean combinations related to "Lore," "video games," and "education." From an initial pool of 29 records, six publications published between 2016 and 2023 were selected for detailed analysis based on their explicit connection between video game Lore and educational application. Additionally, the author conducted a broader review of ludic-didactic methodologies (gamification, game-based learning, serious games) to understand precedents for cross-disciplinary adaptation.

The findings indicate that while only six studies have explicitly addressed this topic, the results are promising. Key empirical insights from the reviewed literature include: Mora-Cantallops and Sicilia (2016) found that Lore in *League of Legends* significantly motivated users to read and deepen their knowledge; Musabirov et al. (2017) demonstrated that Lore in *Dota 2* facilitated the construction of collective values and prosocial behaviors; Anderson (2019) highlighted the potential of Lore for tangential history learning through "interactive museums"; and Landa and Thompson (2023) showed how Lore can foster interest in archaeology. The qualitative analysis revealed strong affinities between Lore and modern educational paradigms such as Connectivism, Universal Design for Learning (UDL), Multi-level Teaching, Student-Centered Learning, Personal Learning Environments (PLEs), Learning Landscapes, Design Thinking, Problem-Based Learning (PBL), Challenge-Based Learning (CBL), and the Maker Movement. These methodologies share core principles with Lore, including personalization, learner autonomy, optional engagement, and non-linear progression.

The author concludes that integrating Lore into formal education is feasible and beneficial for fostering intrinsic motivation and deep engagement without causing learner reactance. However, this approach presents significant challenges, primarily the high initial cost and effort required for design and production. Unlike traditional methods where content delivery is ongoing, Lore requires upfront investment to embed all potential learning paths. Once established, however, it operates in an "unattended" manner, reducing long-term teacher workload and allowing simultaneous access for multiple students. The article suggests that emerging technologies, particularly artificial intelligence, may lower these barriers in the future. The study positions Lore as a valuable, albeit underexplored, resource for creating inclusive, personalized, and motivating educational experiences, warranting further scientific investigation.

citation: Sánchez-Ruiz, L.M.; López-Alfonso, S.; Moll-López, S.; Moraño-Fernández, J.A.; Vega-Fleitas, E. Educational Digital Escape Rooms Footprint on Students’ Feelings: A Case Study within Aerospace Engineering. Information 2022, 13, 478.
target_sections: Games for Learning, Health and Well Being|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Learning and Literacy|Cross-Cutting Conditions for Effective Games-Based Library Services|Educational Digital Escape Rooms
summary_text:
This article presents a case study evaluating the impact of digital escape rooms (dEERs) on student emotions, motivation, and perceived learning outcomes within a first-year Mathematics course for Aerospace Engineering students at the Universitat Politècnica de València. The primary objective was to determine whether implementing game-based learning (GBL) via dEERs fosters a positive emotional environment and enhances educational effectiveness in a STEM context.

The methodology involved the design and implementation of ten short-duration dEERs (20–30 minutes each) using Genial.ly and RPG Maker MZ software. These activities were integrated into the "Mathematics I" syllabus, covering calculus and series topics. The study utilized a mixed-methods approach: an ad hoc questionnaire completed by 296 students (18–19 years old) assessed their primary and secondary feelings, intensity of emotions, and perceptions of specific game elements such as teamwork, storytelling, and quiz formats. Additionally, semi-structured interviews were conducted with 25 volunteers. A separate analysis compared the final grades of 387 students based on their participation in dEERs. Statistical validity was confirmed with a Cronbach’s α of 0.97 and KMO of 0.834.

Key findings indicate a strong prevalence of positive emotions. "Motivation" was the most frequently cited feeling, selected by nearly 40% of students as either their primary or secondary emotion. Other dominant feelings included happiness and confidence. Although stress was reported by 7.4% of participants, qualitative data suggested this was less intense than traditional exam stress, largely due to the collaborative nature of the activity. Students highly valued the narrative elements; over 85% agreed that storytelling enhanced motivation, and 90% believed it contributed to a positive emotional atmosphere. Teamwork was also viewed positively for boosting motivation (70% agreement), though its impact on generating positive emotions was slightly lower (50%).

Regarding academic utility, students reported that dEERs effectively reinforced theoretical knowledge (Mean = 3.83/5) and developed soft skills like critical thinking (Mean = 3.90/5). The overall satisfaction with the activities was high (Mean = 4.44/5). Empirically, students who participated in dEERs achieved significantly higher final course grades (Mean = 7.27) compared to non-participants (Mean = 6.69), with a p-value of 0.009. However, the authors note that participation may be confounded by pre-existing student motivation. Gender analysis revealed that female students rated the educational value and motivational impact of teamwork higher than male students, though no significant gender gap was observed in final grades.

The study concludes that dEERs are a viable pedagogical tool for creating engaging, positive learning environments in STEM disciplines. The integration of storytelling and collaborative problem-solving appears critical to this success. While the results are promising, the authors acknowledge limitations, including the reliance on self-reported data for learning effectiveness and the potential bias in grade comparisons due to voluntary participation. They recommend further research to objectively measure the correlation between emotional states and academic performance.

citation: Sanders, Eugene R. (1982). Dungeons and Dragons in a Public Library. Indiana Libraries, 54(3), 93-98.
target_sections: Programming and Facilitation|Service Purposes and Library Functions|Community, Belonging, Wellness, and the Public Commons|Service Conditions and Institutional Capacity|Game Modalities in Library Contexts|Ground Services in Purpose, Audience, and Institutional Context
summary_text:
Eugene R. Sanders (1982) presents a case study of the implementation of a Dungeons & Dragons (D&D) club at the Michigan City Public Library, illustrating how games-based programming can engage young adults who are otherwise difficult to reach through traditional library services. The article serves as both a practical guide for librarians and a defense of the game against contemporary moral panics.

Sanders notes that young adults historically view public libraries primarily as places for academic study, making them a "hard audience to reach" during after-school hours. Inspired by reports from other institutions, Sanders initiated the club via a newspaper release. The initial meeting attracted twenty participants ranging from grade schoolers to college sophomores. Recognizing that players preferred experiential learning over lectures, the library organized sessions led by experienced Dungeon Masters (DMs) who facilitated games for beginners, intermediates, and advanced players. Sessions were held on Sundays for four hours. Due to scheduling constraints limiting meetings to once a month, the library also functioned as a networking hub, maintaining a contact list to help players form independent groups that met at private homes. Within months, membership grew to nearly fifty individuals.

A significant finding was the positive intergenerational dynamic within the club. Older players reported no issues with younger participants, noting that gameplay depended on "maturity of imagination and intelligence" rather than age. Older members often took on mentoring roles, teaching the game to novices. This structure fostered a collaborative environment where young adults interacted as equals.

The program faced public scrutiny following a negative editorial in the *Michigan City News-Dispatch*, which criticized D&D for potential obsession, financial entrapment, and promotion of "demonic thinking" and negative thought patterns like deception. However, Sanders reports that this controversy had minimal negative impact on operations. There were no organized demands to disband the club from parents or community members. Instead, parental inquiries increased, with many seeking reassurance about safety. Sanders addressed these concerns by comparing D&D to reading fantasy literature or watching adventure films, framing it as a harmless imaginative outlet similar to traditional childhood games like "cops and robbers."

Interestingly, the controversy appeared to enhance the club's appeal, creating an "underground" allure that boosted attendance. A tournament event drew at least forty participants and received favorable coverage from local media, including photos and radio segments. While the local public school system removed promotional flyers due to the controversy, a Catholic high school continued to allow distribution, and word-of-mouth remained the most effective recruitment tool.

Sanders cites external expert opinions to counter criticisms. Educators Linnea Vacca and Penny Jameson praised the game as a "collaborative enterprise" that allows children to enact hero quests and play out inner conflicts safely. References to Bruno Bettelheim’s work suggest that role-playing helps process tensions, and that only individuals with pre-existing fragile egos might be negatively affected. Additional support from a *Christian Science Monitor* article highlighted the game's benefits for group dynamics, noting its ability to integrate solitary children into social groups and its value in gifted education programs for fostering imagination and cooperation.

The implications for library services are significant. Sanders argues that libraries should evolve beyond being mere repositories of reading materials to becoming spaces where users actively create stories and engage in imaginative play. The D&D club demonstrated that games-based services can provide intellectual stimulation and social benefits, effectively drawing young adults into the library environment. Librarians are encouraged to view such programs as valuable community services that address the specific interests and social needs of young adult patrons, while being prepared to educate parents and the public about the constructive nature of role-playing games.

citation: Scicluna, R. (2026). Breaking the ice: A social exchange study on gamification, library anxiety, and the willingness to return at the University of Malta Library. The Journal of Academic Librarianship, 52, 103246.

summary_text:
This study by Ryan Scicluna (2026) investigates the impact of gamified library orientations on first-year undergraduate students at the University of Malta. Grounded in Social Exchange Theory (SET), the research posits that student engagement is a rational calculation weighing the "costs" (e.g., anxiety, cognitive load) against the "benefits" (e.g., information access, social value). The primary objective was to determine if gamification could lower the psychological cost of library entry and increase the willingness to return, while also assessing whether "Game Literacy" moderates these outcomes.

The methodology employed a quasi-experimental, post-test-only design with 132 first-year students. Intact classes were assigned to either a traditional lecture-and-tour orientation (Control, n=65) or a gamified narrative quest involving scavenger hunt elements (Experimental, n=67). Both interventions covered identical learning outcomes regarding library navigation and resource use. Data were collected immediately post-intervention using an adapted Library Anxiety Scale (LAS), Net Promoter Score (NPS), return intention metrics, and game literacy indicators. Statistical analyses included t-tests, Mann-Whitney U tests, and Two-Way ANOVA to assess main effects and interactions.

The findings demonstrated that gamification significantly reduced library anxiety compared to traditional methods, with a large effect size (Cohen’s d = 1.55). The gamified group reported lower anxiety scores (M = 2.14) than the traditional group (M = 3.42). Furthermore, the gamified intervention yielded higher return intentions and a median NPS of 9, compared to 6 for the control group, suggesting a stronger perceived benefit in the social exchange. However, the study identified a critical moderating variable: Game Literacy. A significant interaction effect revealed that while all students benefited from gamification relative to the lecture format, those with low game literacy experienced higher anxiety levels (M = 2.68) than their high-literacy counterparts (M = 1.85). The authors term this phenomenon a "literacy tax," attributing it to the additional cognitive load required for non-gamers to decipher game mechanics alongside library tasks.

The discussion emphasizes that the reduction in anxiety is partly due to the collaborative, team-based structure of the gamified activity, which distributes psychological risk and fosters peer-to-peer support. However, the authors caution against viewing gamification as a universal solution. The "literacy tax" highlights an equity concern: without careful design, gamification may inadvertently disadvantage users unfamiliar with game logic. Consequently, the study recommends implementing "low-threshold" mechanics to ensure inclusivity.

Several limitations are acknowledged. The post-test-only design precludes the measurement of absolute anxiety reduction from a baseline, as pre-testing was avoided to prevent sensitization effects. Additionally, the immediate timing of data collection raises questions about the longevity of the affective benefits, potentially influenced by novelty effects or demand characteristics. The authors suggest future research employ Solomon four-group designs and longitudinal studies to assess sustained engagement and information-seeking behavior over time. Ultimately, the study concludes that gamification is a valuable tool for humanizing academic libraries and breaking initial barriers, provided it is designed with equitable access in mind.

summary_text:
Jared Alexander Seay’s 2018 commentary, "Considering Games and Gamification in Libraries & Associated Entities — In Praise of Real People," published in *Against the Grain*, argues for the pedagogical superiority of immersive, analog games over digital alternatives in library and educational contexts. The article is not an empirical study but a synthesis of psychological theory, educational trends, and library practice observations. Its primary objective is to demonstrate how collaborative, face-to-face gaming formats can mitigate the cognitive challenges of learning, specifically short attention spans and rapid memory decay.

Seay begins by referencing Hermann Ebbinghaus’s "forgetting curve," noting that learners forget approximately 50% of new information within an hour and 90% within a month. He couples this with estimates that average attention spans in lecture settings last only seven to ten minutes. Given these constraints, Seay posits that traditional instruction is inherently limited. While acknowledging the popularity of digital games, he critiques them for often isolating learners ("everyone being together in the same room alone") and focusing primarily on rote memorization or dexterity rather than deep social engagement. He argues that digital interfaces fail to capture the "visceral difference" provided by real-time, non-verbal, and verbal cues inherent in physical proximity.

To address these limitations, Seay introduces the term "collaborative multi-player immersive games" to describe analog formats that place groups of players into shared problem-solving scenarios. He identifies three primary examples relevant to library services: escape rooms, Educational Live Action Role-Playing Games (Edu-LARPs), and megagames. Escape rooms are described as a form of problem-based learning that enhances engagement and subject recall. Edu-LARPs involve participants physically role-playing characters within historical or theoretical scenarios (e.g., the French Revolution or Salem Witch Trials), engaging cognitive, affective, and behavioral levels of learning through experiential methods similar to drama pedagogy. Megagames are characterized as large-scale hybrid formats involving hundreds of players divided into factions, combining elements of board games, LARPs, and escape rooms to simulate complex social dynamics.

The core argument rests on the premise that human beings are fundamentally social creatures whose learning potential is maximized through collaborative interaction. Seay cites secondary literature suggesting that analog game players expect a "slower-paced and chatty group experience," which fosters communication skills that digital games cannot codify. By leveraging these social dynamics, libraries can create learning environments that are not only more engaging but also more effective at retaining information. The article highlights specific library innovations, such as those at the University of Wollongong Library, as evidence of this growing trend.

Seay concludes by advocating for a "high tech high touch" approach, where libraries embrace analog interactivity to complement digital resources. He suggests that the future of library instruction lies in immersive, socially rich activities that allow patrons and students to collaborate creatively. While the article does not provide quantitative data on learning outcomes, it offers a strong theoretical framework for why analog games may outperform digital ones in fostering deep, retained understanding through social engagement. The piece serves as a persuasive call for librarians to integrate these collaborative formats into their service models to enhance educational impact.

summary_text:
Jared Alexander Seay’s 2018 column, "Considering Games and Gamification in Libraries & Associated Entities — Surely, You Can’t Be Serious: When Library Folk Go Game," published in *Against the Grain*, provides a qualitative overview of the growing intersection between library science and gaming culture. The article does not present empirical data or original research findings but rather synthesizes industry trends, anecdotal evidence, and existing literature to argue for the legitimacy and efficacy of games as tools for library instruction and community engagement.

The author begins by describing the mechanics and popularity of escape rooms, characterizing them as immersive experiences that require puzzle-solving, strategy, and information retrieval. Seay draws a direct parallel between these skills and traditional library competencies, noting that players often use knowledge retrieved from primary or secondary sources to solve problems within the game environment. He cites the proliferation of library-based escape rooms in both public and academic settings, referencing specific examples such as the University at Albany, SUNY, where an escape room platform was implemented for information literacy classes. The article posits that this trend is not merely a fad but a significant shift in how libraries approach outreach and instruction, leveraging the "mind stretching" nature of games to teach research skills.

Seay expands the scope beyond escape rooms to include board games and video games, asserting that these mediums have become ubiquitous in library spaces. He references search data indicating millions of results for "board game" and "public library," highlighting the prevalence of circulating collections and game nights. The author argues that this integration transforms libraries into community hubs, comparable to the historical impact of bookmobiles or story times. To support the seriousness of gaming as a sector, Seay includes market data: video games generated over $108 billion in revenue in 2017, and global board game sales reached $9.6 billion in 2016. These figures are used to counter historical perceptions of games as trivial pastimes, instead framing them as substantial economic and cultural forces.

A central theme of the article is the concept of "gamification," which Seay defines as the addition of game-like elements to non-game contexts to encourage participation. He distinguishes this from simple entertainment, describing it as a system of engagement that compels user interaction. In academic libraries, this manifests through the use of badges and incentives to teach information literacy, replacing traditional bibliographic instruction lectures. The author suggests that gamification serves as "user centered instruction engagement," allowing educators to simulate risk and reality without actual consequences. This approach is presented as a modern evolution of library services, aligning with broader trends in education and industry where "serious games" are used for training and learning.

The article concludes by predicting a continued convergence of libraries, education, the game industry, and publishing. Seay suggests that interactive multimedia and book apps are already merging with gaming principles, hinting at a future where these domains may unify into a single interactive entity. While the tone is informal and occasionally humorous, the underlying argument is that libraries must adapt to this "gamification maelstrom" to remain relevant and effective in engaging users. The piece serves as a persuasive endorsement of games-based library services, grounding its claims in observable trends and industry statistics rather than controlled experimental data.
